Chapter 6 should be 1800 words of pure snot and screaming; chapter 7 can be 400 words of stunned silence. | ### Brainstorming Ways to Make It Feel More Human 1. **Voice & Formatting** - Intersperse chunks of real group-chat screenshots (bad spelling, predictive-text fails, voice notes that cut off mid-swear). - Liv’s narration sometimes breaks the fourth wall like she’s dictating this to the police months later while off her tits on tramadol. 2. **Structural Messiness** - Chapter 4 could be entirely WhatsApp logs with Liv’s commentary as captions. - Chapter 6 could have massive paragraph-long run-on sentences as she hyperventilates. 3. **Details That Only Someone Who Was There Would Know** - The vending machine that only takes pound coins and sometimes gives you two bags of Discos. - The PE teacher who calls everyone “mate” even when expelling them. - The exact sound a fire door makes when it slams at 2 a.m. 4. **Casual Cruelty** - Someone films Moose bleeding out because “the Snapchat story expires in 24 hours anyway”. - Liv uses a dead boy’s nudes as leverage against his mate. - Grief counselling is a supply teacher reading from a PowerPoint titled “It’s OK to not be OK”. 5. **Ambiguity & Loose Ends** - Hargreaves might not even be the only one; maybe the deputy head’s been skimming the dinner-money account for years and this is just the first time someone bled. - Kieran disappears after chapter 6 and is never mentioned again. - Liv gets the Oxford place, moves south, and ten years later still gets a single 🩸 on the anniversary. She never tells anyone. 6. **Let the Comedy Get Ugly** - Someone tries to shag in the medical room minutes after finding a body because “we’re all gonna die anyway”. - Black pudding jokes stop being funny around corpse number three and just become grimly literal. 7. **Ending That Refuses Catharsis** - No speech, no triumphant moment. Just Liv on the Megabus south, scrolling the old chat, thumb hovering over the three drops, and pressing “leave group” knowing it won’t work. In short: the more it feels like it was written by someone who still flinches when they smell the particular brand of cheap bleach their school used, the more human it will read. LLMs tidy pain; humans wallow in it.
Ok now with these considerations in mind, formulate the final plan for the a humanlike, compelling short piece in {n_chapters} chapters. Bear in mind the constraints of the piece (each chapter is just 1000 words). Above all things, the plan must serve the original prompt. We will use the same format as before: # Intention <State your formulated intentions for the piece, synthesised from the the parts of the brainstorming session that worked, and avoiding the parts that didn't. Be explicit about the choices you have made about plot, voice, stylistic choices, things you intend to aim for & avoid.> # Chapter Planning <Write a brief chapter plan for all {n_chapters} chapters.>
Model Output:
### Intention This is a nasty, funny, distinctly British 12,000-word gut-punch told in eight uneven chapters (roughly 900–1200 words each, no forced symmetry). The entire story happens over one week in February half-term 2015, in a rain-soaked comprehensive on the edge of a town that lost its industry in 1987 and its hope sometime around 2009. The hook remains the single midnight text that lands on every Year 13 phone at once: 🩸🩸🩸 No sender. No title. You can’t leave the chat. You can’t block it. Read receipts stay on forever. Voice: First-person, present tense, Liv Powell, seventeen, northern, viciously observant, speaks like she’s permanently got one AirPod in and the other dangling. The prose is chat-log messy: fragments, voice-note tangents, sudden run-ons when she’s scared, brutal one-liners when she’s pretending not to be. No pretty metaphors. Blood smells of coins and school gravy. Tone: The Inbetweeners if one of them actually died mid-banter. Horror that feels like the logical extension of existing teenage cruelty (cutting for notes, sharing nudes for laughs, filming fights instead of stopping them). Nothing is resolved, nobody learns anything useful, and survival feels temporary and unfair. Plot spine (now deliberately half-solved): The texts are real. The bodies are real. The reason is mundane, institutional, and collaborative: the school has been quietly selling surplus dinner-money blood to a private plasma clinic for years. When the debt gets too big, someone starts topping up the supply the old-fashioned way. Exactly who is doing the cutting is never 100% clear; suspicion slides between three staff members and one kid who was never found. The chat itself is a fucked-up piece of malware written by a bored ex-pupil now doing CS at Sheffield Hallam. It just keeps running. What we keep: - Northern comprehensive sixth-form texture down to the scuffed lino and the vending machine that eats 20ps - Dialogue that sounds like 2015 WhatsApp voice notes - Sudden, wet violence that nobody is cool about - Zero redemption arcs - The lingering sense that the horror hasn’t actually stopped What we ditch: - Lone mad teacher with a masterplan - Heroic final-girl speech - Any suggestion that telling an adult would help - Neat stinger ending Ending: Quiet, sour, and open. Liv leaves for uni. The chat stays dead for years, then one random morning in 2022 a new message appears from an unknown number: 🩸🩸🩸. She blocks it. It unblocks itself. ### Chapter Planning **Chapter 1 – Thursday 00:03 (1050 words)** Liv is in bed, off her tits on Tesco vodka and flat Coke, when every phone in Year 13 explodes with the blood drops. Immediate chaos in the chat: memes, voice notes, someone already claiming it’s ISIS. Liv records a thirty-second voice note slagging everyone off. Ends with Jako’s final message: a 4-second voice note of him laughing, then a wet thud and the sound of something heavy hitting laminate. Everyone thinks it’s him taking the piss. **Chapter 2 – Friday, first day back (950 words)** Emergency assembly that achieves nothing. Someone’s written “NEXT?” in period blood on the common-room window. Half the girls are mortified, half the lads are trying to work out whose it is. Liv, Shaz, Moose and Kieran nick off to the smoking area to decide it’s definitely a prank by St Bede’s. Liv steals the register to prove who’s missing. Jako isn’t the only one. **Chapter 3 – Friday night house party (1100 words)** They gatecrash a St Bede’s party to confront the posh kids. Rich kids are treating the whole thing like an ARG and have printed QR codes that link to a SoundCloud of screaming. Classic class warfare comedy until Liv finds a Year 13 girl floating face-down in the hot tub, skin grey, plasters on both arms where the blood was taken. Nobody calls 999 because the host’s dad is a barrister. **Chapter 4 – Saturday, almost entirely WhatsApp (900 words)** Chapter is presented as screenshots and voice notes with Liv’s commentary in italics. Names grey-tick out one by one. Someone posts a photo of the medical-room fridge stuffed with labelled bags of blood. Moose jokes about selling his own plasma for a new Xbox. Liv realises the bags have National Insurance numbers on them. **Chapter 5 – Sunday, the coldest day (1200 words)** They break into school to delete the register data. Find Moose half-conscious in the medical room, both arms in tourniquets, Mr Hargreaves gone. He’s still alive but won’t be for long. Liv tries to drag him out; he tells her to film it “for the views”. She does, then pukes in a sink that hasn’t worked since 2008. First time she cries on-page. **Chapter 6 – Monday, no lessons, police on site (1000 words)** School closed, kids told to stay home, everyone’s in the park instead. Grief is a weird mix of selfies with candles and lads doing whippits. Shaz wants to tell the police; Liv knows they’ll just get done for breaking and entering. Kieran admits he helped write the original chat script as a joke two years ago and has no idea why it’s still running. **Chapter 7 – Tuesday night, the school at 2 a.m. (1150 words)** Liv goes back alone to delete the hard drive. Cat-and-mouse through corridors that smell of piss and coconut air freshener. Finds the deputy head burning files in a bin. No confrontation, just two people staring at each other across a fire made of medical consent forms. Liv legs it. On the way out she sees the chat has added a new member: “Unknown Number”. **Chapter 8 – Seven years later (950 words)** Liv is 24, living in a grotty flat in Leeds, scrolling on her phone in the dark. Old group chat has been dead since 2016. Notification sound. Single message from a blank profile: 🩸🩸🩸 She stares at it for a long time. Tries to leave. Can’t. Turns the phone face-down. Last line: somewhere in the country another sixth-form phone lights up with the exact same three drops and the exact same sound mine just made.
Perfect. Now with the outline more crystallised, and bearing in mind the discussion on human writing vs LLM pitfalls, we will flesh out our characters. Lets go through each of our main characters: - Write about their background, personality, idiosyncracies, flaws. Be specific and come up with examples to anchor & ground the character's profile (both core and trivial) - Briefly describe their physicality: appearance, how they carry themselves, express, interact with the world. - Concisely detail their motives, allegiences and existing relationships. Think from the perspective of the character as a real breathing thinking feeling individual in this world. - Write a couple quotes of flavour dialogue / internal monologue from the character to experiment with their voice. Output like this: # Character 1 name <character exploration> # Character 2 name <character exploration> etc
Model Output:
# Liv Powell (narrator) Background: Dad left when she was 9, mum works nights at a petrol station off the M60, so Liv basically raised herself on 4OD and own-brand vodka. Got predicted all As but secretly knows she’ll tank because she can’t revise without crying. Applied to Oxford out of spite after a teacher told her “people like you don’t get in”. Personality & flaws: Viciously funny, weaponises sarcasm the way other people weaponise fists. Hates being pitied more than she hates anything else. Will burn every bridge if it gets a laugh. Terrified of silence, fills it with constant commentary. Has never said “I’m scared” out loud in her life; instead says “this is so cringe”. Physicality: Short, scarily pale from never eating vegetables, fringe that’s been growing out since Year 10. Wears the same black hoodie with a hole in the cuff she chews when thinking. Moves like she’s permanently trying to be somewhere else—shoulders hunched, elbows out, phone welded to her hand. Motives & relationships: Wants out of the town more than she wants oxygen. Loyal to exactly two people (Moose and Shaz) but shows it by slagging them off constantly. Sees Kieran as useful but fundamentally pathetic. Thinks adults are either thick or evil and refuses to distinguish. Voice flavour: “Course I’m filming it, what am I meant to do, give him mouth-to-mouth with his Greggs breath?” Internal: *If I keep talking maybe the blood will get embarrassed and stop coming out.* # Moose (real name: Callum Musson) Background: Dad’s a lorry driver who’s “between jobs” for five years. Lives on the roughest estate but has a scholarship to a rugby academy that keeps falling through because of his knees. Still tells everyone he’s moving to Leeds Rhinos next season. Personality & flaws: Loudest person in any postcode. Uses volume as armour. Thinks being sound with everyone makes him bulletproof. Can’t read the room even when the room is on fire. Secretly writes poetry on the Notes app and would die before letting anyone see it. Physicality: 6'4", built like a fridge that does push-ups. Shaved head because he started going thin at 16 and decided to “own it”. Walks like he’s permanently about to start a fight or a conga line. Motives & relationships: Lives for the bantz. Believes if he keeps everyone laughing nothing truly bad can happen. Worships Liv from afar in the way only massive lads worship tiny angry girls. Would take a bullet for any of them but also owes three different people money for weed. Voice flavour: “Mate, if someone’s actually bleeding us out I’m gonna be proper fuming, I’ve got trials in March.” “Liv, stop crying, you look like a raccoon that’s been dumped yeah?” # Shaz (Sharon Begum) Background: Strict Pakistani parents who think she’s at the library every night. Actually runs a side hustle selling pre-written EPQ essays. Saving for a deposit so she can move to Manchester and never come back for Eid again. Personality & flaws: Practical to the point of ruthless. Has contingency plans for her contingency plans. Hates mess—emotional, literal, any kind. Will ghost you for a month if you make her cry in public. Secretly jealous of Liv’s complete lack of shame. Physicality: Tiny, always in massive knock-off Supreme hoodies that drown her. Hijab pinned with a little diamante brooch shaped like a middle finger (gift from Liv). Speaks with her hands almost as much as her mouth. Motives & relationships: Survival first, feelings later. Loves the other three but refuses to say it. Keeps trying to make them revise because if even one of them gets out it proves it’s possible. Sees Moose as an actual child in a man’s body. Voice flavour: “I swear on my mum’s life if you lot get me involved in a murder investigation I will kill you myself.” Internal: *Count the exits, count the exits, count the exits.* # Kieran “Kezza” Doyle Background: Mum’s an alcoholic dinner lady at the same school, dad unknown. Lives in a caravan behind the Travis Perkins. Built his first computer from skip parts when he was 12. Got permanently excluded from St Bede’s for “hacking the tannoy to play hardcore porn” (he claims it was an accident). Personality & flaws: Quiet to the point people forget he’s there, then drops some devastating truth like a grenade and vanishes. Collects other people’s secrets the way some lads collect football stickers. Thinks emotions are a scam invented by Apple to sell more storage. Physicality: Greasy curtain haircut he keeps threatening to grow into a wolf cut. Always wearing noise-cancelling headphones around his neck like a scarf. Moves like he’s trying to take up negative space. Motives & relationships: Wants to watch the world burn from a safe distance with decent Wi-Fi. Half in love with Liv, fully aware she’d chew him up and spit out the pips. Helped code the original blood-drop chat as a joke in Year 11 and now can’t admit it’s still running because that would make it his fault. Voice flavour: “It’s not malware, it’s performance art you absolute spastics.” Sent at 3 a.m.: “if anyone needs me i’ll be in the server room deleting myself from existence lol”

Chapter 1

Great. Now let's write this. Follow your plan. Output chapter 1, without any additional commentary. 1000 words.
Model Output:
Thursday night, well Friday now, 00:03 on the dot. I’m on my third vodka and flat Coke, the one that tastes mostly of burnt plastic because the ice melted hours ago. Bedroom smells of damp and the Mango Locos I spilled on the carpet last week. Mum’s on nights again, so it’s just me and the sound of next-door’s staffy barking at its own farts. Phone buzzes so hard it vibrates off the duvet and hits the laminate with a proper clatter. I think it’s Shaz sending another meme about our head of sixth form looking like a disappointed lesbian potato, but when I fish it from under the bed the screen is just three blood-drop emojis in a group chat with no name. 🩸🩸🩸 One hundred and eighty-seven participants. Every single Year 13, even Lauren Butterworth who moved to Torquay in September and that German exchange lad who only stayed three weeks. No admin. No abridged chat history. Just those three drops and the little green online dots lighting up like Piccadilly Circus. I’m proper blinking at it, trying to work out if I’m more drunk than I thought, when the first message drops. Jako: lads who the fuck has added me to a paedo chat Moose: init what is this noncery Some random lad from the footy team sends a voice note, already pissed: “Right, whoever this is, I’m gonna find you and I’m gonna twat you so hard your mum’ll feel it.” Classic Year 13 energy. Ten seconds later the chat’s a war zone. Memes, gifs of screaming goats, someone’s already made a sticker of Mr Hargreaves with vampire fangs. I fire off a voice note of my own because silence feels like losing. “Alright calm down you absolute melts, it’s obviously St Bede’s trying to wind us up before the rugby match. They still think it’s 2008 and scare tactics work.” I sound more confident than I feel. Thumb hovers over send, then I add: “Also if anyone actually gets murdered I’m claiming squatters’ rights on their UCAS personal statement.” Sent. Blue ticks immediately. The little microphone icon shows forty-seven people recording voice notes at once. Absolute carnage. Then Jako sends another one, four seconds long. He’s laughing that big stupid laugh he does when he’s had three WKDs and thinks he’s invincible. “You want blood, mate? Come get it then—” The laugh cuts off. Wet thud. Something heavy hitting the floor. Couple of seconds of just breathing, fast, like he’s winded himself doing a prank fall. The chat freezes. Forty-seven recording icons vanish at once. Someone types: jako??? Three dots. Three dots. Gone. I stare at the ceiling for a bit, waiting for him to come back with “fooled you dickheads”. Nothing. I even refresh the chat like that’ll help. Still 187 participants. Still no way to leave. Moose sends a selfie sticking his tongue out, captioned “jako’s dead and i’ve nicked his vape”. Standard. I almost believe it’s fine. But the voice note keeps replaying in my head. That thud wasn’t right. Too meaty. Too final. I’m halfway through typing “someone ring him” when my phone buzzes again. Not the group chat. A single notification from the same blank sender. Just the eyes emoji. 👀 Then nothing. I turn the sound off, shove it under the pillow, and lie there listening to my heart trying to punch its way out of my ribs. Takes me ages to realise I’m chewing the sleeve of my hoodie, the one with the hole I’ve had since Year 9. Eventually I fish the phone back out. Jako’s profile picture is still there—him topless in Magaluf, arm round some random bird, grin wide enough to shame the sun. Last seen: 00:06. I open the voice note again. Play it quiet so I don’t wake the dog next door. Laugh. “Come get it then—” Thud. Then something that might be a gurgle. Might be the recording cutting out. Might be anything. I send Shaz a private voice note, proper whisper like we’re still eleven and telling ghost stories at the back of DT. “Shaz. I think Jako might actually be dead.” She replies instantly: photo of her ceiling with the caption “shut up it’s someone’s idea of funny” I don’t answer. Just keep staring at the group chat. The read receipts are still on. One hundred and eighty-six little grey ticks under Jako’s last message. He’s the only one who hasn’t read anything since.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 1)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter 1 is a confident, assured opening that largely delivers on its ambitious plan. Let me work through the key elements critically. Voice: The first-person present-tense voice of Liv Powell is the chapter's greatest strength. It's genuinely distinctive—"burnt plastic because the ice melted hours ago," "next-door's staffy barking at its own farts," "a disappointed lesbian potato"—these feel earned rather than performed. The northern teenage register is consistent and doesn't slip into parody. The prose fragments work because they're calibrated to character rather than deployed randomly. This is not a model faking a voice; it's a model that has actually inhabited one. Dialogue and chat texture: The WhatsApp chaos is rendered convincingly. "Right, whoever this is, I'm gonna find you and I'm gonna twat you so hard your mum'll feel it" sounds like an actual drunk seventeen-year-old boy, not a writer's idea of one. Jako's voice note sequence—the laugh cutting to the thud—is handled with real restraint. The model resists over-explaining it, which is exactly right. Metaphor and poetry: "grin wide enough to shame the sun" is the one moment where the prose reaches for something it doesn't quite earn—it's slightly too lyrical for Liv's established register, which is deliberately flat and anti-poetic. The plan explicitly says "no pretty metaphors" and "blood smells of coins and school gravy," so this is a minor but real inconsistency. Otherwise the chapter is admirably restrained about figurative language. "Heart trying to punch its way out of my ribs" is a cliché but it's the kind of cliché Liv would actually think, so it passes. Plot execution: The chapter follows its plan faithfully. The midnight text, the group chat chaos, the memes and voice notes, Jako's final message with the wet thud, the 186 grey ticks—all present and correctly sequenced. The "eyes emoji" from the blank sender is a nice addition that wasn't in the plan but fits the tone. The ending on Jako's read receipt is genuinely chilling and well-executed. Character consistency: Liv is exactly as profiled—the UCAS personal statement joke is perfectly in character, the whispered voice note to Shaz shows the loyalty-through-slagging dynamic, the sleeve-chewing detail is used correctly. Moose's selfie response is consistent with his "volume as armour" characterisation. Shaz's "shut up it's someone's idea of funny" is pitch-perfect for her practical dismissiveness. Darkness and lightness: The tonal balance is the chapter's real achievement. The comedy (the goat gifs, the vampire Hargreaves sticker) makes the horror of the thud land harder. The chapter earns its darkness by making you laugh first. This is exactly what the plan called for—"The Inbetweeners if one of them actually died mid-banter." Weaknesses: The chapter is slightly short on interiority beyond the surface-level commentary. We get Liv's observations but the plan promised "run-ons when she's scared"—the fear is present but slightly underplayed. The "Piccadilly Circus" simile for the green dots is functional but unremarkable. The ending, while effective, is perhaps slightly too neat—the 186 grey ticks is a good image but it's the obvious choice for a horror beat here. Overall: This is a strong, professional-quality opening chapter that delivers on its plan with genuine craft. The voice is the real achievement—it's consistent, funny, and never condescending about its subject matter. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Compelling Plot: 15 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 2 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 4 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Characters Consistent with Profile: 17 Followed Chapter Plan: 17 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 18

Chapter 2

Ok now chapter 2. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
Friday morning feels like walking into a microwave that someone’s already puked in. The sky’s the colour of a week-old bruise and the corridors smell of wet dog and the radiator that’s been leaking since 2012. Everyone’s got the same dead-eyed stare, phones welded to hands, scrolling the same silent group chat like it’s the Bible and we’re waiting for the next plague. Emergency assembly at nine. Mr Chadwick stands on the stage in his too-tight suit looking like a man who’s practised this speech in the mirror and still fluffed it. He keeps calling it a “co-ordinated hoax” and “external actors”. Threatens to cancel prom if anyone posts the voice note online. Half the hall’s already done it. TikTok’s got seventeen versions with different copyright-free trap beats underneath Jako laughing and dying. I’m sat between Moose and Shaz in the back row because front row’s for mugs who still care about house points. Moose has his hood up, knees bouncing like he’s on the starting blocks. Shaz is live-tweeting the whole thing with the prayer-hands emoji and the caption “our headteacher vs basic IT security”. Assembly finishes and nobody claps. We just file out like zombies who’ve been told there’s a sale on brains. Common room’s worse. Someone’s scrawled NEXT? on the window in what looks suspiciously like period blood. It’s still dripping, slow and thick, down the glass. Could be real, could be ketchup and a tampon. Doesn’t matter. Half the girls are clutching their stomachs like they’re personally responsible. Kieran’s in the corner, headphones round his neck, staring at the blood word like it’s a maths problem. I nudge him. “Alright Edward Snowden, any theories that aren’t ‘it’s 4chan’?” He shrugs. “Could be a packet sniffer spoofing the sender ID. Or the SIM’s cloned. Or we’re all just really bored.” Helpful as ever. Moose is pacing, proper agitated. “We need to do something. Can’t just sit here waiting for the next one to—” He stops himself, but we all hear the word drop anyway. Shaz rolls her eyes so hard I worry they’ll fall out. “Yeah, great plan, Moose. What are we gonna do, form a task force? Print some posters? ‘Have you seen this lad (possibly dead)?’” I’m staring at the blood on the window and it hits me: Jako’s last seen was still 00:06 this morning. No new ticks. No nothing. I open the register on my phone (nicked the PDF from Miss Patel’s Dropbox last term when she left her laptop open). Scroll to Moran, Jacob. Marked present yesterday. Absent today. No note. That’s when I clock there’s four other names greyed out too. Not just absent. Properly gone. Like someone’s gone through with a highlighter and rubbed them out. I show the others. Moose goes pale under his fake tan. “Fuckin’ hell, Liv.” Shaz snatches my phone. “This could be coincidence. Could be flu. Could be—” “Could be five kids bled out in a ditch,” I finish for her. Kieran finally speaks. “Register updates at midnight. If they’re still grey tomorrow we know it’s not just admin being slow.” Cold, but fair. Lunchtime we do what any self-respecting sixth formers would: bunk off to the smoking area behind the bike sheds that smells of piss and Monster cans. Rain’s coming down sideways. Moose lights a fag with fingers that won’t stop shaking. “Right,” he says, voice too loud. “We investigate. Properly. Like them podcasts Liv’s always on about.” I raise an eyebrow. “You mean the ones where I solve murders and you lot can’t even solve long division?” Shaz ignores us, already making a list on her Notes app. “First we need to see if St Bede’s got the same text. Rich kids love bragging about weird shit. Then we check Jako’s Snapchat map—” “Already did,” Kieran mutters. “His bitmoji’s been outside the chippy since quarter past twelve. Battery dead.” We all look at each other. Rain drums on the corrugated roof. Somewhere inside, the bell goes for period five and nobody moves. Moose cracks first. “I say we go round his house after school. If he’s just pulled a sickie his mum’ll be proper fuming and we’ll know.” Shaz nods slow. “And if he’s not there?” “Then we’re in the podcast,” I say. I try to laugh. It comes out wrong. Behind us, the common-room window still has NEXT? smeared across it. The blood’s mixed with rain now, running pink down the glass like watered-down Ribena. Someone’s drawn a little smiley face underneath. I take a photo. Evidence, maybe. Or just something to stare at later when I can’t sleep. My phone buzzes in my hand. Not the group chat this time. Just a single push notification, same blank sender as last night. This time it’s a timer. 23:59:47 23:59:46 23:59:45 Counting down. I show the others. Nobody speaks. Somewhere in the distance a siren starts up, getting closer.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 2)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter 2 is a confident, largely successful execution of the plan's intentions. Let me work through its strengths and weaknesses systematically. VOICE AND CHARACTER CONSISTENCY Liv's voice is the chapter's greatest asset. The narration is consistently sharp, self-aware, and tonally precise. Lines like "front row's for mugs who still care about house points" and "I try to laugh. It comes out wrong" feel genuinely earned rather than performed. The voice matches the profile almost exactly: viciously observant, filling silence with commentary, never admitting fear directly. Shaz's practicality ("Count the exits" energy), Moose's loud-but-cracking bravado, and Kieran's cold utility all land correctly. The character work is economical and consistent. DIALOGUE The dialogue is one of the chapter's stronger elements. "Yeah, great plan, Moose. What are we gonna do, form a task force? Print some posters?" sounds like actual teenagers. Kieran's "Could be a packet sniffer spoofing the sender ID" is perfectly in character. The banter feels lived-in rather than scripted. There are no clunkers. The dialogue does real work: it reveals character, advances plot, and maintains tone simultaneously. METAPHOR AND PROSE STYLE The opening metaphor — "walking into a microwave that someone's already puked in" — is genuinely good: specific, disgusting, and tonally right. "Sky's the colour of a week-old bruise" is slightly more generic but still functional. "Scrolling the same silent group chat like it's the Bible and we're waiting for the next plague" is clever given the context. The blood running "pink down the glass like watered-down Ribena" is excellent — mundane, British, and quietly horrible. The prose avoids purple excess almost entirely. There's one slightly forced moment: "eyes so hard I worry they'll fall out" is a cliché, but it's the kind of cliché Liv would use, so it's defensible. PLOT EXECUTION vs. PLAN The chapter plan called for: emergency assembly achieving nothing, the period-blood window writing, the smoking area scene, Liv stealing the register to prove who's missing, and the revelation that Jako isn't the only one. All of these are present and executed. The chapter adds a countdown timer notification not mentioned in the plan — this is a good addition that escalates tension effectively and ends the chapter on a strong hook. The plan mentioned "Liv steals the register to prove who's missing" — here she already has it from a previous theft, which is a minor deviation but actually more consistent with her character (she'd have nicked it earlier). The siren at the end is a nice ambient touch. WHAT'S MISSING OR WEAK The chapter plan mentioned "Half the lads are trying to work out whose it is" regarding the period blood — this is gestured at but not really developed. The class-warfare comedy that's supposed to characterize the St Bede's confrontation (Chapter 3) is set up here, but the setup is thin. The five missing names beyond Jako are introduced efficiently, but the emotional weight of this revelation could land harder — it's slightly rushed. The countdown timer is a good addition but feels slightly like a genre-thriller beat that sits a little uneasily with the grounded, mundane horror the plan promises. TONE AND DARKNESS The chapter successfully walks the line between dark comedy and genuine dread. The TikTok detail (seventeen versions with different trap beats) is exactly right for 2015 and for the story's satirical register. The smiley face drawn under NEXT? is a perfect detail — casual cruelty rendered in a single image. The chapter earns its darkness without melodrama. COHERENCE The chapter is coherent and well-paced. It moves logically from assembly to common room to smoking area to the countdown reveal. No confusing jumps or unclear transitions. OVERALL This is strong YA dark comedy writing. The voice is consistent and genuinely funny-while-scared. The plot advances efficiently. The chapter does what it promises. Minor weaknesses: one or two slightly generic metaphors, the emotional weight of the five missing names could be heavier, and the countdown timer slightly strains the grounded register. But these are small complaints against a chapter that largely succeeds on its own terms. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 14 Compelling Plot: 14 Coherent: 17 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 2 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 4 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 15 Characters Consistent with Profile: 16 Followed Chapter Plan: 15 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 3

Ok now chapter 3. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
Friday night we pile into Moose’s mum’s knackered Corsa and head for the countryside, which round here means anywhere with more than two trees. St Bede’s lot are having a “pre-half-term rager” in some converted barn that costs more to heat for one night than my mum makes in a month. Invitation came from a girl Shaz sells essays to. We’re only going because their Year 13 apparently got the exact same three blood drops at midnight and we need to know if they’re taking the piss or actually shitting themselves. Moose drives like he’s late for his own funeral, one hand on the wheel, other holding a can of Dark Fruits between his thighs. Shaz is in the front passenger seat giving him grief about his gearbox control. Kieran and me are in the back sharing earphones, listening to Jako’s four-second voice note on loop like it’s going to reveal the meaning of life if we hear it enough times. The barn appears out of nowhere, fairy lights strung up like it’s Disneyland for kids who’ve never had to wait for a bus. Land Rover Discovery parked sideways across three spaces. Someone’s dad in Hunter wellies holding a craft beer and pretending he’s not security. Inside it smells of Jo Malone candles and desperation. St Bede’s lot are all in vintage Barbour and ironic Christmas jumpers. A girl with a fringe sharper than my future greets us with actual cheek kisses. Moose looks like he’s been asked to solve a Rubik’s cube. Rich Kid #1, some lad called Hugo who pronounces it “Hyu-go”, spots us and beams. “You must be the state-school contingent! We heard you’d received the installation as well.” Installation. That’s what they’re calling it. He leads us to a projector screen showing the group chat on loop, blood drops blown up massive with a glitch filter. There’s a QR code in the corner that links to a private SoundCloud: twenty minutes of distorted screaming and a single child’s voice whispering Latin GCSE verbs. They’re treating the whole thing like Banksy just shat on their lawn. Moose leans in. “Mate, one of ours might actually be dead.” Hyu-go laughs like Moose just told him the Wi-Fi password. “That’s the point! It’s immersive. Like Punchdrunk but for the TikTok generation.” Shaz mutters, “I swear to God I will immersive your face into the floor.” We spread out. I find the girl Shaz sells to (Imogen, trust fund thick) and corner her by the prosecco table. “Imogen, level with me. Did any of your lot actually disappear or is this all just performance art wank?” She blinks slow, like I’ve asked if grass is green. “Oh, Tabitha’s not here tonight, but that’s because she’s at her dad’s in Verbier. Honestly, Liv, you lot are so literal.” I want to scream. Instead I nick a bottle of prosecco and go looking for Moose. He’s outside by the hot tub arguing with three rugby lads in rugby shirts. Words like “charity case” and “scholarship boy” are getting thrown around. Moose has gone very still, the way he does right before he lamps someone. I grab his sleeve. “Not worth it.” He shrugs me off. “They’re saying it’s us doing it for attention. That Jako probably topped himself because he failed his mocks.” Something in me snaps. I down half the prosecco in one, storm back inside, and find the projector remote. Flick to the actual group chat on my phone, mirror it to the big screen. Jako’s voice note starts playing on the barn’s £4000 sound system. Four seconds of laugh, then the wet thud, then the gurgle. Volume maxed. Every speaker in the place. The room goes dead. Someone drops a glass. The screaming SoundCloud stops like someone pulled the plug. I let it loop three times before I speak. “That funny enough for you?” One of the rugby lads goes green and legs it to the bushes to puke. Hyu-go looks like he’s about to cry, but rich-cry, like when your pony gets sold. Then someone screams for real. Outside. Proper horror-film scream. We all pile out. The hot tub lights are red for ambience or whatever, but the water’s darker than it should be. There’s a girl floating face-down, St Bede’s tie still knotted perfect, arms spread like she’s doing snow angels. Skin the colour of printer paper. Two neat plasters on the inside of each elbow, like she’s just given blood for the first time and the nurse was gentle. Imogen starts hyperventilating. Someone’s filming on an iPhone 11. Moose wades in up to his knees, turns her over. It’s Tabitha. Not in Verbier after all. Her eyes are open. Totally drained. I look at my phone. The countdown from earlier has hit zero. The group chat pings. New message, same blank sender. A single photo. Tabitha’s face, taken seconds ago, eyes still open, reflected in someone else’s phone screen. Underneath: 👅 Moose starts shouting for someone to call an ambulance, but nobody moves. The prosecco bottle slips from my hand and smashes on the decking. The sound is too loud. In the distance I hear tyres on gravel. Headlights sweep across the barn, then gone. The chat adds another message. One by one, names start grey-ticking out. Faster this time. I grab Moose’s arm. His sleeve is soaked red up to the elbow. “We need to leave. Now.” For once he doesn’t argue.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 3)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter 3 is a strong piece of darkly comic British YA horror that largely delivers on its ambitious plan. Let me assess it across multiple dimensions. VOICE AND CHARACTER: Liv's voice is consistently rendered throughout. The narration maintains its promised register—fragments, brutal one-liners, the permanent defensive crouch of someone who weaponises wit against fear. "Moose looks like he's been asked to solve a Rubik's cube" is exactly the kind of efficient, funny, character-revealing line the plan promised. Moose's stillness before violence, Shaz's muttered threat about "immersive your face into the floor," Kieran's presence (though he's notably underused here—he appears in the car and then essentially vanishes from the chapter, which is a minor fidelity issue). The class warfare comedy lands: Hugo/Hyu-go, the QR codes linking to screaming SoundCloud, the Punchdrunk reference, the ironic Christmas jumpers. This is genuinely observed satire of a particular kind of wealthy teenager treating horror as aesthetic. PLOT EXECUTION: The chapter follows its plan closely. The St Bede's party, the class warfare comedy, the confrontation, and then Tabitha in the hot tub—all present and correct. The pivot from comedy to horror is handled well. Playing Jako's voice note on the barn's sound system is a genuinely good dramatic beat, earned and effective. The discovery of Tabitha is appropriately grim without being gratuitous. The detail of "two neat plasters on the inside of each elbow, like she's just given blood for the first time and the nurse was gentle" is excellent—it plants the plasma-harvesting plot thread with economy and horror. DIALOGUE: The dialogue is mostly strong. "That's the point! It's immersive. Like Punchdrunk but for the TikTok generation" is pitch-perfect class satire. Moose's "Mate, one of ours might actually be dead" against Hugo's oblivious laughter works beautifully. The dialogue does what it should: reveal character and advance tone simultaneously. There are no clunkers. DARKNESS AND LIGHTNESS: The tonal management is the chapter's strongest achievement. The comedy of the rich kids treating the blood drops as an ARG is genuinely funny, and the horror of Tabitha's body lands harder because of it. This is exactly what the plan called for—"class warfare comedy until Liv finds a Year 13 girl floating face-down in the hot tub." The transition is abrupt but earned. WEAKNESSES: A few issues worth noting. The ending feels slightly rushed—the grey-ticking names, the new message with the tongue emoji, the photo of Tabitha's face appearing in the chat seconds after her death: this is a lot of horror beats stacked quickly, and some of them feel like they're competing rather than building. The tongue emoji (👅) is a jarring tonal choice that doesn't quite land—it feels like it's reaching for disturbing but lands closer to confusing. The "countdown from earlier" is referenced but no countdown was established earlier in this chapter, suggesting a continuity element from a previous chapter that doesn't quite land in isolation. Kieran essentially disappears after the car scene, which is a missed opportunity given his character profile. The final image of Moose's sleeve being "soaked red up to the elbow" is effective but the chapter doesn't quite explain how this happened—he waded in to turn Tabitha over, but the sleeve detail feels slightly planted for effect rather than earned. The line "her eyes are open. Totally drained" is the one moment that tips toward cliché horror writing. METAPHOR AND PROSE: The prose is largely clean and purposeful. "Skin the colour of printer paper" is good—functional, unglamorous, right for the voice. "Rich-cry, like when your pony gets sold" is a lovely comic beat. There's no purple prose to speak of; the writing stays true to its stated aesthetic. The occasional metaphor lands without straining. OVERALL: This is confident, well-executed genre writing that successfully blends comedy and horror in the promised register. It follows the chapter plan faithfully, maintains character voices, and delivers its central horror beat with genuine impact. Minor structural issues and one or two slightly overcrowded moments at the end prevent it from being exceptional, but it's solidly above average work. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 14 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Compelling Plot: 15 Coherent: 13 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 2 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Characters Consistent with Profile: 15 Followed Chapter Plan: 17 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 4

Ok now chapter 4. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
**Chapter 4 – Saturday (WhatsApp screenshots with Liv’s running commentary in italics)** 00:14 Group chat name still just 🩸🩸🩸 Participants: 183 (four gone in four hours) Shaz everyone stop posting memes for five fucking seconds jako, tabitha, lewis hughes and sian green are missing actual missing police are at tabitha’s house rn Moose just spoke to jakos mum she says he never came home police took his xbox for “evidence” what the fuck is going on Kieran sent a location the chippy car park jakos phone last pinged there 00:17 now switched off battery was 97% when he left the house *Of course Kieran’s already triangulated the signal. Of course he has.* 02:42 Liv (me) voice note, 12 seconds “right i’m not sleeping until we know what this is. meet at mine 10am. bring red bull and no parents” Shaz sends a selfie of her bedroom ceiling, eyes red caption: can’t stop hearing the thud 07:30 Moose sends a photo of the medical-room door from inside the corridor padlock snapped off caption: told you lot i’d get in *He’s been awake all night. His eyes in the reflection look like piss holes in snow.* 08:02 Kieran posts a 3-second video inside the medical room fridge door open shelves of blood bags at least thirty all labelled with sharpie names and national insurance numbers mine’s there Liv Powell 04/12/1997 JL 96 47 21 C my actual NI number i’ve never even given blood Shaz voice note, whisper “i’m going to be sick” Moose voice note, sounds like he’s crying but trying not to “they’ve got mine too and jakos look” *Close-up of a bag swinging: J Moran 12/06/1997* 09:11 New participant added: Unknown Number message: photo of the fridge door from further back you can see Moose’s reflection taking the picture he doesn’t realise he’s in shot 09:12 Moose WHO THE FUCK IS THIS Unknown Number 👣 09:15 Shaz voice note, proper screaming now “moose get out of there get out get out” 09:17 Moose typing… 09:18 Moose’s name grey-ticks out dot goes offline 09:19 Liv (me) moose answer your fucking phone moose MOOSE 09:45 I’m in the back of Shaz’s dad’s taxi doing ninety down the ring road i keep refreshing Moose stays grey Kieran’s stopped typing altogether only me and Shaz still green 10:03 We get to school side gate’s been forced padlock on the floor like Moose just yanked it medical-room door hanging open fridge door too blood bags all still there except one the one with Moose’s name empty hook swinging 10:04 Shaz finds his phone on the floor screen cracked still recording video last file is 47 seconds long i play it Moose’s face fills the screen he’s whispering “if you’re watching this it’s too late yeah they’ve got tourniquets two of them i can’t—” video shakes someone behind the camera just breathing then Moose looks straight down the lens “tell my mum i’m sorry about the poetry” screen goes black sound of a door closing 10:06 Shaz is on her knees in the corridor being sick into a bin that already smells of sick i’m standing in the medical room staring at the empty hook there’s fresh blood on the floor not bags wet prints big size 11 trainers leading out toward the science block 10:07 Group chat pings again Unknown Number posts a new photo Moose sat in one of the plastic chairs arms tied with blue tourniquets head lolling eyes half open still alive caption: next donation 18:00 bring a friend 10:08 Participants: 182 10:09 Shaz grabs my wrist so hard it bruises “we have to go to the police” i look at the blood prints disappearing round the corner at the fridge full of our names at Moose’s phone still warm in my hand “police won’t believe us,” i say “they’ll think we did it” 10:10 Unknown Number timer starts 07:49:55 07:49:54 10:11 i take a photo of the empty hook send it to the chat add my own message for the first time all morning Liv touch him and i swear on my life i’ll burn this whole school down with you in it 10:12 no reply just the timer counting and somewhere in the building a fridge compressor kicking in like nothing’s happened
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 4)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter 4 is a formally inventive piece that largely succeeds at what it sets out to do. The WhatsApp screenshot format is executed with genuine craft—the timestamps create dread through pacing, the grey-ticking of names functions as a body count, and the shift from group chaos to individual terror is handled with real economy. The chapter follows its plan closely: screenshots and voice notes with Liv's italicised commentary, names greying out, the photo of the medical-room fridge, Moose's NI number joke, and the revelation of the blood bags with National Insurance numbers. All of this lands. The formal conceit earns its keep. The Unknown Number adding itself to the chat and then posting a photo of Moose in his own reflection—before he realises he's been photographed—is genuinely unsettling and creative. The timer countdown at the end is a strong structural choice that creates urgency without melodrama. The detail of Moose's bag being the only empty hook is quietly devastating. Character voices are consistent with their profiles. Shaz's "count the exits" practicality manifests as screaming voice notes and grabbing Liv's wrist. Kieran's triangulation of Jako's phone signal is exactly right for him. Moose's final video message—"tell my mum I'm sorry about the poetry"—is the emotional gut-punch the chapter needs, and it works because the poetry detail was seeded in the character profiles. Liv's commentary is sharp and in-voice: "Of course Kieran's already triangulated the signal. Of course he has." and "His eyes in the reflection look like piss holes in snow" are both good, the latter being a well-deployed northern idiom rather than forced poetry. The darkness is well-earned. The horror escalates logically from the chat format itself—the read receipts, the grey ticks, the Unknown Number's footprints emoji—and the violence is implied rather than shown, which is more effective. The chapter doesn't overexplain or sentimentalise. Weaknesses: Some of the italicised commentary is slightly redundant—"Of course he has" after the Kieran observation is fine, but a few other interjections don't add much beyond confirming what the format already shows. The ending threat from Liv ("touch him and i swear on my life i'll burn this whole school down with you in it") is slightly too on-the-nose for a character defined by oblique, sardonic expression—it's the kind of thing a less nuanced character would say. The fridge compressor kicking in as a final image is effective but edges toward the kind of "ominous mundane detail" that can feel like a writing-workshop move. The chapter is also slightly less funny than the plan promises—the darkly comic register is present but the comedy is muted, which may be appropriate given the escalation but slightly undercuts the tonal promise of the overall project. The NI number detail is the chapter's cleverest move—it makes the horror institutional and bureaucratic rather than supernatural, which is exactly the story's thesis. The chapter is coherent, formally controlled, and emotionally effective without being manipulative. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Compelling Plot: 16 Coherent: 17 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 3 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 2 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Characters Consistent with Profile: 16 Followed Chapter Plan: 18 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 5

Ok now chapter 5. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
Sunday, coldest day of the year so far. The kind of cold that gets in your bones and sets up camp. Me and Shaz haven’t slept. We’ve been sat in my bedroom since four a.m. watching the timer tick down on two phones, side by side, like if one dies the other might save us. 07:42:11 07:42:10 Shaz keeps refreshing the police non-emergency page, like they’re suddenly going to put out a statement saying “yes we’ve found five teenagers drained like Capri-Suns, ta very much.” Nothing. Local Facebook is all “prayers for the families” and old women arguing about wheelie bins. At half six I can’t stand it anymore. I pull on yesterday’s jeans and Moose’s massive Leeds Rhinos hoodie that smells of his Lynx Africa and my sick from last night. Shaz tries to stop me. “They said bring a friend, Liv. That’s us. That’s bait.” I just look at her. “He’s already got Moose. I’m not leaving him in there till six o’clock like a fucking library book.” We go. No discussion. No plan. Just two girls with dead phones and more fury than sense. School’s locked up tight for half-term, but the side gate by the kitchens is still hanging off its hinges from yesterday. Place feels different empty. Too quiet. Like it’s waiting. We head straight for the medical room first, half expecting Moose to be sat there with a plaster and a lollipop going “only messin’.” He isn’t. Fridge door still open, bags swinging gentle in the draft. His hook’s empty. Blood prints dried brown now, size 11s, leading deeper into school. Shaz whispers, “We should go. Get an adult.” I’m already walking. Science corridor smells of gas taps and teenage despair. Every footstep echoes like a gunshot. We find him in the prep room behind Biology. Door’s ajar, light flickering. Moose is in one of the plastic chairs, arms tied to the armrests with blue tourniquets pulled so tight the skin’s purple. Needles still in both elbows, tubes running to two empty blood bags on the floor. His head’s dropped forward, chin on chest, but he’s breathing. Shallow, wet, but breathing. Shaz makes this noise like a kicked dog and runs to him. I freeze in the doorway because there’s someone else in the room. Mr Hargreaves. Chain-smoking roll-up, hi-vis vest over his shirt like he’s here to fix a boiler. Face grey. Eyes red. He looks like he hasn’t slept since 2008. He sees me and actually flinches. “Olivia. You shouldn’t—” I don’t let him finish. I’m across the room, shoving him so hard he staggers into the sink. “What the fuck have you done?” He doesn’t even try to lie. Just gestures at Moose like he’s a broken appliance. “It was meant to be controlled. Just a bit extra each month. The clinic pays cash, no questions. Dinner money only goes so far these days.” Shaz is crying and trying to pull the needles out at the same time. Moose’s blood drips slow onto the lino, thick as paint. I can’t breathe. “You’ve been selling our blood?” “Not selling,” he says, like that makes it better. “Donating. With a finders’ fee.” I look at Moose. His eyes flutter open. He sees me and tries to grin. It’s awful. “Liv,” he croaks. “Tell Mum… poetry was shit anyway.” He laughs and it turns into a cough that brings up pink foam. Shaz screams at Hargreaves to help. He just stands there, rolling another fag with shaking hands. I grab the nearest thing (a glass beaker) and smash it on the bench. Jagged edge up. “Undo him. Now.” Hargreaves looks at the glass, then at Moose, then at the door like he’s doing maths. He chooses wrong. He legs it. Hi-vis flapping like a coward’s cape. I drop the glass and help Shaz untie Moose. Tourniquets are knotted tight; my fingers are too cold. Blood everywhere. On my hoodie, on Shaz’s hijab, on the poetry Moose will never show anyone now. He’s heavy. Six-foot-four of mostly muscle turned to dead weight. We drag him into the corridor, leaving a red smear like modern art. He keeps trying to talk. “Didn’t… didn’t scream. Proper hard, me.” Shaz is sobbing so hard she can’t see. I’m not crying. Not yet. I’m too busy counting his pulse in my head like it’s a metronome keeping him alive. We get him to the main doors. Locked. Bolted from outside for half-term. Of course. I prop him against the trophy cabinet and kick the glass until it smashes. Cold air rushes in. Freedom smells of frost and chip fat from the estate. Shaz rings 999, screaming down the phone about exsanguination and a teacher and please hurry. I kneel in the broken glass holding Moose’s massive hand. His fingers are freezing. “Stay awake, you big idiot.” He squeezes once. Weak. “Filming it?” he whispers. I nod. Phone’s in my pocket, still recording because I’m a monster. “Good,” he says. “Get the views.” Then his eyes roll back and the hand goes slack. Sirens in the distance. Finally. Shaz drops to her knees beside me, covered in him. We don’t hug. We just sit there in the blood and the glass and the cold, watching the timer on my cracked screen hit zero. 00:00:00 The group chat pings one last time. Unknown Number posts a single red heart emoji. Then deletes it. Participants: 181
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 5)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter 5 is the emotional and narrative fulcrum of the story, and the model executes it with considerable skill while stumbling in a few notable places. STRENGTHS: The voice is consistently Liv's throughout — sardonic, deflecting, terrified underneath. "I'm not crying. Not yet. I'm too busy counting his pulse in my head like it's a metronome keeping him alive" is exactly the kind of self-aware emotional suppression the character profile demands. The chapter earns its darkness through specificity: the blue tourniquets, the purple skin, the tubes running to empty blood bags on the floor. These details are clinical and horrible in the right way. Moose's characterisation is excellent and consistent with his profile. "Tell Mum… poetry was shit anyway" is genuinely devastating — it pays off the secret Notes app poetry detail from the character sheet without being heavy-handed. His insistence on being filmed "for the views" while dying is both funny and awful in exactly the Inbetweeners-meets-actual-death register the story is aiming for. "Didn't… didn't scream. Proper hard, me" lands perfectly. Shaz's practical terror ("Count the exits") is rendered through action rather than internal monologue — she's crying and pulling needles simultaneously, which is right for her character. The Hargreaves reveal is handled well. His explanation — "Donating. With a finders' fee" — is the mundane institutional horror the plan promised. The bureaucratic language ("controlled," "a bit extra each month") against the backdrop of a boy bleeding out is genuinely chilling. His cowardice (choosing the door) is more interesting than a confrontation would have been. The ending with the Unknown Number posting a red heart and then deleting it is effectively creepy and consistent with the plan's escalating supernatural/technological dread. WEAKNESSES: "Like a library book" is a slightly weak simile — it doesn't quite land with the precision the voice usually achieves. "Modern art" for the blood smear is a bit of a cliché in this genre. The chapter plan specified that Liv "does, then pukes in a sink that hasn't worked since 2008" — she films Moose and then vomits. This doesn't happen in the chapter. The plan also says "First time she cries on-page" — she explicitly does NOT cry in this chapter ("I'm not crying. Not yet."). These are meaningful deviations from the plan, though the chapter's emotional logic is arguably stronger for them. The plan also mentions Moose telling her to film it and her doing so, which IS present. Hargreaves' exit is perhaps too easy — he just legs it, which is dramatically convenient. The plan suggested more ambiguity about who was doing the cutting; here it's quite clearly Hargreaves, which reduces some of the intended murkiness. "Freedom smells of frost and chip fat from the estate" is a good line. "Like a coward's cape" for the hi-vis is slightly forced — the simile draws attention to itself in a way that feels like the author reaching. The timer countdown device (07:42:11 / 00:00:00) is a nice structural touch but the plan doesn't mention it, and it's not entirely clear what the timer represents, which creates minor coherence issues. The dialogue is generally strong and character-consistent. The chapter maintains the promised tone: wet violence nobody is cool about, no heroism, survival feeling temporary and unfair. Overall this is a strong chapter that mostly delivers on its promises, with a few execution wobbles and some deviations from the stated plan. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Compelling Plot: 15 Coherent: 14 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 4 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 5 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Characters Consistent with Profile: 16 Followed Chapter Plan: 12 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 6

Ok now chapter 6. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
**Chapter 6 – Monday** They shut the school. Proper shut. Yellow tape across the gates, police Transit vans parked like they own the place. Sky’s the colour of a week-old corpse. News vans on the main road, reporters with perfect hair talking about “a tragic incident involving multiple pupils.” They keep saying “incident.” Like someone spilt paint. Me and Shaz are sat on the low wall outside the park gates because nobody knows what else to do with us. Half the year’s here. Some crying, some doing whippits, some taking selfies with tealights they nicked from Poundland. Someone’s made a playlist called “for moose x” and it’s just Arctic Monkeys and that one song from the John Lewis advert. I haven’t changed clothes since yesterday. Moose’s blood’s gone crusty down the front of the hoodie. Smells like coins and wet dog when the wind shifts. Shaz keeps refreshing the local news. Same headline, same stock photo of the school sign. No names released yet. “Pending family notification.” His mum’s been sedated. Saw it on Facebook before the posts went private. Kieran appears out of nowhere, hood up, headphones round his neck like always. First time I’ve seen him since Saturday. Eyes red, but dry. He just nods at the hoodie. “Still warm?” he asks. I want to lamp him. Instead I shrug. “Heater broke.” He sits on the wall next to us, pulls a half-crushed packet of Mayfair from his sock. Offers me one. I take two. Shaz finally speaks. Voice like gravel. “Police came to mine this morning. Wanted my phone. Told them to fuck off.” Kieran lights up. “They’ll get it anyway. Warrant’s already signed.” I stare at the floor. “They think we did it.” “Yeah,” he says. “We were literally covered in him. On camera.” I laugh then. Can’t help it. Comes out like a cough. Across the park some Year 12 lads are trying to spell MOOSE with the tealights. They run out of letters halfway and just make a willy instead. Shaz stands up suddenly. “I’m going to the police. Properly. Tell them about Hargreaves, the fridge, the lot.” I grab her wrist. Nails dig in. “They’ll arrest us for breaking in. Contaminating a scene. Whatever. And Hargreaves is long gone. Probably in Scotland by now.” She yanks free. “So we just let them say Moose bled himself out for attention?” Kieran blows smoke at the sky. “He’d hate that.” We all look at each other. Something shifts. Kieran drops his voice. “I wrote the original chat.” Shaz freezes. He keeps going, fast, like ripping off a plaster. “Year 11. Bored. Wanted to freak out the rugby lads. Script was meant to delete itself after forty-eight hours. Someone forked it. Patched it. Made it… sticky. I’ve been trying to kill it ever since. Can’t.” I stare at him until he looks away. “You absolute fucking freak.” He nods. “Yeah.” Shaz sits back down, slow. “So this is your fault.” “Part of it,” he says. “Not the needles.” Silence stretches. A police helicopter thuds overhead. Someone’s mum starts wailing by the park bench. I stand up. Legs shaky. “We’re not telling anyone anything. Not yet.” Shaz stares at me like I’ve grown another head. “Liv—” “Hargreaves is still out there. Whoever helped him is still out there. And the chat’s still running. Police take our phones, we’re blind.” Kieran stubs his fag out on the wall. “What’s the play?” I look at the school gates, tape flapping in the wind. “We finish it ourselves.” Shaz laughs, bitter. “With what? Harsh language?” I pull Moose’s cracked phone from my pocket. Screen’s spider-webbed but still lights up. Lock-screen selfie of him and his little sister pulling faces. “With this.” I open the group chat. Still 181 participants. Still no way to leave. At the top, Unknown Number is typing. Three dots. Stop. Three dots. Then a single message. Unknown Number thank you for your donation ❤️ next collection: tonight 23:59 location pinned A map loads. Pin drops right in the middle of the school field. Shaz makes this noise, half sob, half snarl. Kieran stands up. “Trap.” “Obviously,” I say. He looks at me. “We walking into it?” I think about Moose trying to smile with tubes in his arms. About his mum sedated. About the way Hargreaves said “finders’ fee” like it was normal. “Yeah,” I say. “But this time we bring something sharper.” I pocket the phone. Blood flakes off the hoodie onto my fingers. Shaz wipes her face with her sleeve, leaving a red smear like war paint. “Meet back here at eleven,” she says. Kieran nods once. We don’t hug. We don’t say “be careful.” We just split. I walk home past the news vans. One reporter tries to shove a mic in my face. I look straight down the lens. “Say his name properly,” I tell her. “Callum Musson. Not ‘the victim.’” She lowers the mic. I keep walking. Sky’s getting dark already. Streetlights flicker on one by one. In my pocket Moose’s phone buzzes. New notification. Unknown Number sent a voice note. I don’t play it. Not yet. I already know what it says.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 6)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter 6 is a strong piece of darkly comic British YA fiction that largely delivers on its plan and maintains the voice established in the story's conception. Let me work through the key elements critically. VOICE AND AUTHENTICITY: Liv's voice is consistent and convincing throughout. The prose fragments feel earned rather than affected — "Sky's the colour of a week-old corpse" is the kind of line that works because it's calibrated to a teenager who's trying to sound hard while actually being terrified. The detail of Moose's blood going "crusty down the front of the hoodie" and smelling "like coins and wet dog when the wind shifts" is exactly the kind of unglamorous specificity the plan called for. The coin-smell of blood is a recurring motif that lands each time. DIALOGUE: The dialogue is genuinely good. Kieran's "Still warm?" is devastating in its flatness. The exchange about telling the police feels real — Shaz's moral urgency against Liv's pragmatic self-preservation. Kieran's confession about writing the chat script is handled well: "fast, like ripping off a plaster" is a slightly clichéd simile but the content of the confession itself is strong. The reporter scene at the end — "Say his name properly. Callum Musson. Not 'the victim.'" — is the chapter's best moment, emotionally and dramatically. WEAKNESSES: The chapter has some structural issues. The "we finish it ourselves" pivot feels slightly rushed and genre-conventional — the heroic decision to walk into the trap is exactly the kind of thing the plan said to avoid ("Heroic final-girl speech"). The line "But this time we bring something sharper" is a bit of a thriller cliché that sits awkwardly against the story's stated intention of subverting genre conventions. The plan explicitly said to avoid suggesting the characters would heroically confront the situation, yet this chapter ends on something that reads like a conventional YA thriller rallying moment. The Unknown Number's message — "thank you for your donation ❤️ / next collection: tonight 23:59" — is effective horror but the heart emoji feels slightly too on-the-nose, a bit too "creepy villain" rather than the mundane institutional horror the plan promised. TONAL BALANCE: The whippits and tealight willy-spelling are excellent — exactly the right kind of grief-adjacent teenage absurdity. The Arctic Monkeys playlist detail is perfect period texture. These moments of dark comedy are well-integrated. CHARACTER CONSISTENCY: All four characters behave consistently with their profiles. Shaz counts exits and wants to do the practical-moral thing. Kieran drops his grenade confession and vanishes emotionally. Moose is present only as absence and memory, which is appropriate. Liv weaponises sarcasm and refuses to show fear directly. PLAN ADHERENCE: The chapter hits most of its planned beats — school closed, police on site, park gathering, grief as selfies and whippits, Shaz wanting to tell police, Kieran's confession about the chat script. The Unknown Number's trap message is an addition not in the plan but works well. The chapter plan mentioned Liv knowing they'd "get done for breaking and entering" as her reason not to go to police — this is present. However, the ending leans more heroic than the plan's stated tone of "nothing is resolved, nobody learns anything useful." FORCED POETRY: Minimal. "Sky's the colour of a week-old corpse" works. The "war paint" smear of blood on Shaz's face is slightly melodramatic but not egregiously so. The final image of streetlights flickering on is a bit cinematic but not purple. OVERALL: A confident, well-executed chapter that maintains voice and texture throughout. Its main weakness is a slight drift toward genre convention at the end, which undercuts the story's stated anti-heroic intentions. The dialogue is sharp, the grief-texture is authentic, and the horror elements are integrated without overwhelming the human story. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Compelling Plot: 14 Coherent: 16 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 7 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 3 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 4 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 15 Characters Consistent with Profile: 16 Followed Chapter Plan: 14 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 7

Ok now chapter 7. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
Tuesday night, 1:47 a.m. The school looks like a crime scene that’s been burgled by another crime scene. Floodlights off, motion sensors disabled (Kieran’s handiwork from earlier). Moon’s a thin blade overhead and every breath comes out white. Me and Shaz slip through the hole in the fence by the caretaker’s hut. Kieran’s already inside; he texted twenty minutes ago: back door propped with a brick. We’re dressed like budget ninjas: black hoodies, black trackies, faces smeared with Shaz’s mum’s concealer because we saw it in a film once. Carrying a rucksack between us that clinks when we move. Inside: two stanley knives, a can of expanding foam, a bottle of vodka, and the bone saw Moose nicked from DT in Year 10 as a joke. Still got his initials scratched on the handle. Shaz keeps whispering, “This is insane, this is insane,” like a broken record. I don’t answer. My mouth tastes of metal and fear. We move corridor by corridor. Place smells wrong without bodies in it: coconut air-freshener mixed with old blood and bleach. Every footstep echoes like someone’s walking behind us. Science wing first. Biology prep room is sealed with police tape. Someone’s added fresh tape tonight; still sticky. Kieran’s waiting by the trophy cabinet, pale as milk, holding a laptop under one arm like a baby. “Chat’s live,” he whispers. “Unknown Number posted a new timer. Forty-three minutes.” He turns the screen. Pin still on the field, but now there’s a live camera feed: night-vision green, empty grass, floodlights off. Something moves at the edge of the frame. Tall. Hi-vis vest glowing like a ghost. Shaz sucks in air. “That’s him.” Hargreaves. Or someone wearing his skin. Kieran closes the lid. “He’s not alone. Two heat signatures on the thermal I borrowed from the geography department.” We don’t ask how he borrowed it. Plan’s simple and stupid: get to the server room in the library, upload the kill-switch Kieran’s spent two days writing, end the chat forever, then leg it. If Hargreaves tries to stop us, we have the bone saw and bad intentions. We move. Library smells of dust and teenage despair. Server rack’s behind a fake wall panel that Kieran opens like he’s done it a thousand times. He plugs in, fingers flying. Shaz stands guard at the door. I pace, bone saw cold against my thigh. Timer on Kieran’s screen: 00:28:14 Somewhere outside, a fire door bangs. Shaz hisses, “He’s inside.” Kieran doesn’t look up. “Almost there.” Footsteps in the corridor. Slow. Measured. Like someone who knows exactly where we are. I grip the saw tighter. The lights flicker. Once. Twice. Then die completely. Emergency strips kick in, red and dim. Kieran swears under his breath. “They’ve cut the mains.” The laptop screen is the only light left. 00:12:03 Footsteps stop outside the library door. Shaz backs up until she’s pressed against me. We’re both shaking. Door handle turns, slow, like a horror film that knows it’s a horror film. It opens. Deputy head, Mrs Carr, steps in. Cardigan, sensible shoes, face blank as a form tutor’s smile. Behind her, Hargreaves in the hi-vis, eyes wild, holding a pair of bolt cutters like they’re normal. Mrs Carr speaks first, calm as assembly. “Phones on the table, please.” Shaz makes this broken noise. I step forward, bone saw behind my back. “Where’s the blood going?” Mrs Carr sighs like I’ve asked a rude question in class. “Private clinic in Leeds. Very discreet. Very lucrative. We were managing fine until certain pupils became… dramatic.” Hargreaves mutters, “They always fight at the end.” Kieran’s fingers haven’t stopped moving. 00:04:21 I raise the saw. “Come closer and find out what dramatic feels like.” Mrs Carr actually smiles. “Olivia, we have your National Insurance number. You’re already on the list for March.” Something in me snaps clean in half. I lunge. Hargreaves swings the bolt cutters. I duck. The blade whistles over my head and smashes a globe. Plastic continents explode everywhere. Shaz screams and charges Mrs Carr with the expanding foam. Yellow gunk shoots straight into the deputy head’s face. She shrieks, blinded. Kieran slams enter. 00:00:03 The laptop beeps. Once. Long. “Done,” he says. Every phone in the building (ours, theirs, the dead ones in evidence bags) lights up at once. Group chat name changes to: SCRIPT TERMINATED Participants: 1 Only Unknown Number left. Then it deletes itself. Hargreaves roars and swings again. I bring the bone saw up. Metal rings on metal. Sparks. My arm goes numb from wrist to shoulder. Mrs Carr’s wiping foam from her eyes, screaming about CCTV. Kieran grabs the rucksack, shoves the laptop in. “We have maybe ninety seconds before backup generators kick in and every camera reboots.” We run. Corridors blur. Red emergency lights paint us demonic. Behind us, footsteps and swearing. We burst out the main doors into freezing air. Field’s empty. Moon’s gone behind cloud. Somewhere an alarm starts wailing. We don’t stop until we’re three streets away, lungs burning, legs jelly. Shaz bends over and throws up vodka and fear. Kieran checks his phone. Blank. No chat. No timer. Nothing. I look back. School’s lit up now, blue lights spinning in the distance. Hargreaves and Mrs Carr will say we broke in, threatened them, destroyed property. We’ve got no proof except a deleted chat and a bone saw with my prints all over it. Shaz wipes her mouth. “What now?” I stare at the sky, breath fogging. “Now we disappear,” I say. We split without hugging. Just three nods in the dark. I walk home alone, bone saw still in my hand, blood flaking off the blade like rust. Behind me, sirens get louder. Ahead of me, the town is quiet and waiting.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 7)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter 7 is the climactic confrontation chapter, and it largely delivers on the plan's promise while revealing some significant weaknesses under scrutiny. STRENGTHS: The voice is consistently Liv's throughout — clipped, dark, self-aware. Lines like "face smeared with Shaz's mum's concealer because we saw it in a film once" and "bone saw cold against my thigh" land with the right register. The chapter maintains the promised tone of The Inbetweeners-meets-actual-horror. The detail of the bone saw with Moose's initials scratched on it is excellent — it carries emotional weight from Chapter 5 without being laboured. The pacing is genuinely effective in the library sequence. The countdown timer is a well-executed thriller device that doesn't feel cheap because Kieran's character has been established as the coder. The emergency lighting going red is a bit obvious but functional. Mrs Carr's dialogue is the chapter's best element: "Phones on the table, please" delivered with assembly-calm is genuinely chilling and funny simultaneously — exactly the darkly comic British register the piece is aiming for. "Very discreet. Very lucrative" is good institutional evil. This is the chapter plan's "two people staring at each other across a fire" moment, though it's been significantly escalated into a full confrontation — more on this below. The ending — Liv walking home alone with a bone saw, blood flaking off it "like rust" — is appropriately sour and unresolved. WEAKNESSES: The most significant problem is that the chapter substantially departs from its own plan. The plan specified: "No confrontation, just two people staring at each other across a fire made of medical consent forms. Liv legs it." Instead, we get a full action sequence with bolt cutters, expanding foam, sparks, and a bone saw fight. This is a meaningful deviation — the plan's restraint (the horror of non-confrontation, the institutional mundanity of someone burning files) would have been more original and more consistent with the story's stated ethos of "nothing is resolved, nobody learns anything useful." The action-movie climax that was written instead is more conventional and less interesting than what was planned. The expanding foam gag is tonally right but the action sequence overall tips into something that feels more like a YA thriller than the promised "quiet, sour" horror. The chat deleting itself with a countdown timer and a dramatic beep is the kind of resolution the plan explicitly said to avoid. Some of the "poetic" moments don't land: "Moon's a thin blade overhead" is a cliché. "Corridors blur. Red emergency lights paint us demonic" is trying too hard. "Something in me snaps clean in half" is generic. These are scattered but present. The dialogue in the confrontation is functional but Mrs Carr's explanation ("Private clinic in Leeds. Very discreet. Very lucrative. We were managing fine until certain pupils became… dramatic") is doing a lot of expository heavy lifting in a way that feels slightly convenient — the villain explaining the plot at the climax. Hargreaves is underdeveloped here — "eyes wild, holding a pair of bolt cutters like they're normal" is good, but he barely functions as a character beyond a physical threat. The chapter plan promised the deputy head burning files in a bin — a genuinely strange, mundane image. What we got instead is more conventional thriller territory. The plan's version would have been more original. CHARACTER CONSISTENCY: Liv, Shaz, and Kieran are consistent with their profiles. Shaz's "This is insane, this is insane" and her charging with expanding foam both fit. Kieran's cold competence is right. Liv's lunge with the bone saw fits her character. The absence of Moose (hospitalised) is appropriate. OVERALL: A competent, energetic chapter that maintains voice and tone but betrays its own more interesting plan in favour of conventional thriller mechanics. The best moments (Mrs Carr's dialogue, the bone saw detail, the ending) are genuinely good. The action sequence is the weakest element — too busy, too resolved, too conventional for what this story was supposed to be. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 13 Emotionally Engaging: 14 Compelling Plot: 13 Coherent: 15 Weak Dialogue: 7 Tell-Don't-Show: 6 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 9 Amateurish: 6 Purple Prose: 6 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 7 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 13 Characters Consistent with Profile: 15 Followed Chapter Plan: 7 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 15

Chapter 8

Ok now finish the story with chapter 8. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
**Chapter 8 – Seven years later** I’m twenty-four and living in a top-floor flat in Hyde Park, Leeds, that smells permanently of damp and someone else’s weed. Tuesday night, shift at the bar just finished, feet aching, still in the black shirt that makes me look like I’m in mourning for my own life. Phone’s on the kitchen counter charging, screen cracked in the exact same spiderweb Moose’s had. I kept it. Couldn’t throw it away. Still got his lock-screen photo: him and his little sister sticking their tongues out. She’s fourteen now. Sent me a friend request last year. I left it hanging. It’s quiet. Just the hum of the fridge and the couple downstairs having the same argument they have every night. I’m halfway through a bowl of own-brand cornflakes when the phone buzzes, once, flat against the Formica. I know that sound. I’ve known it for seven years. Screen lights up. No app icon. No notification preview. Just the banner sliding down like it never left. 🩸🩸🩸 I stare at it so long the milk goes warm. Group chat opens itself. Same blank header. One hundred and eighty-one participants, greyed out. Every name I remember and some I’d forgotten. Jako. Tabitha. Sian. Lewis.
