Moose right at the top, last message still that thumbs-up emoji he sent the night before they took him. And one green dot. Mine. I try to swipe it away. Fingers go straight through like it’s a ghost. New message appears. Unknown Number hi liv missed u I drop the phone. It clatters, keeps buzzing. Another message. Unknown Number sent a photo It’s tonight. My kitchen. Taken from the doorway behind me. Bowl of cornflakes in the foreground, my reflection in the black window. I’m already looking scared. I spin round. Door’s still bolted from the inside. No one there. Phone again. Unknown Number still can’t leave x I grab a knife from the draining board, cheap IKEA thing that can barely cut toast. Hold it like I’m seventeen again and useful. The chat starts typing. Three dots. Three dots. Three dots. Then nothing. I wait. Heart so loud I can taste it. Minutes pass. Nothing. I pick the phone up with the hand that isn’t shaking. Liv who is this No reply. Liv hargreaves is in prison carr got early release but she’s in a wheelchair kieran topped himself in 2019 shaz moved to toronto there’s no one left Three dots again. Unknown Number wrong Another photo. Taken two seconds ago. My own hand holding the phone, knife glinting. Timestamped right now. Unknown Number still plenty of blood in the north I run to the front door, yank the bolt, chain still on. Corridor empty. Lift stuck between floors like always. Back in the kitchen I open every cupboard, under the bed, the shower. Nothing. No one. Phone buzzes again. Voice note this time. I know that laugh before I even press play. Four seconds. Moose, younger than twenty-four, higher than he ever got to be. “Liv,” he says, voice warm and close like he’s sitting right next to me. “Tell them the poetry was shit anyway.” Then the thud. Same as the first night. I slide down the cabinets until I’m on the lino. Knife clatters away. I open the chat one last time. Liv leave me alone Unknown Number can’t u never left us The participants counter ticks up. 182. Somewhere far away, a phone I don’t own lights up with three red drops. I block the number. It unblocks itself. I turn the phone off. It turns itself back on. I hurl it at the wall. Screen cracks worse but the chat stays open, calm and patient. Eventually I crawl to the sofa, pull the blanket over my head like I’m nine and monsters are real. In the dark I whisper the only thing left to say. “I’m sorry, Moose.” The flat stays silent except for the fridge and the couple downstairs finally shutting up. Morning comes grey and wet. I wake up cold. Phone’s on the coffee table now, screen facing up. Battery at 100%. No cracks. Like new. Group chat still there. One hundred and eighty-two green dots. At the top, a new name’s been added. Liv Powell just joined Underneath, a single message from me that I never typed. Liv ready when you are x I stare at it until the words blur. Outside, Leeds carries on. Buses hiss. Someone shouts about Greggs. Inside, the chat waits. It always waited. I’m never leaving.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 8)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter 8 is the culmination of a darkly comic British YA horror story, and it largely delivers on its ambitious plan. Let me assess it across multiple dimensions. STRENGTHS: The chapter opens with strong, grounded detail: the Hyde Park flat, the damp smell, the cracked phone screen kept as a relic of Moose. These are earned, specific details that feel true to the voice and world established. The cornflakes going warm while she stares at the screen is a quietly excellent image — mundane horror done right. The voice remains consistent with Liv's profile throughout. She's still sardonic, still using deflection ("black shirt that makes me look like I'm in mourning for my own life"), still cataloguing exits and facts rather than feelings. The line "I've known it for seven years" lands with appropriate weight without melodrama. The Moose voice note is the chapter's emotional peak and it earns its darkness. "Tell them the poetry was shit anyway" — referencing his secret Notes app poetry from the character profile — is a genuinely devastating callback that rewards readers who've been paying attention. This is well-earned darkness, not manufactured sentiment. The escalating horror mechanics (photo of her kitchen, the phone turning itself back on, the battery resetting to 100%) are effective and appropriately uncanny. The plan called for the chat to unblock itself, and the chapter delivers this plus more, escalating to the genuinely chilling final reveal: Liv's name added to the chat with a message she never typed. The final image — "I'm never leaving" — works as both literal horror and thematic statement about trauma, about the town, about the dead. It's not a neat stinger; it's sour and open as planned. The class texture persists: "Someone shouts about Greggs." This is exactly right — Leeds carrying on while Liv is trapped in supernatural horror. The mundane and the terrible coexist without either undermining the other. WEAKNESSES: The chapter occasionally tips into slightly mechanical horror-movie beats. The "I spin round. Door's still bolted from the inside. No one there." sequence is competent but familiar — the checking-every-room routine is well-worn territory. The chapter plan promised something more distinctive. "Heart so loud I can taste it" is a slightly forced synesthetic metaphor that doesn't quite land — tasting a heartbeat is a stretch that feels like it's reaching for poetry rather than finding it. The revelation that Kieran "topped himself in 2019" is delivered in a chat message Liv types, which is an efficient way to convey information but feels slightly like a data dump. Given Kieran's importance to the plot (he wrote the original script), his death deserved more than a parenthetical in a list. The photo-of-her-kitchen device, while effective, is a fairly standard horror trope (the "I can see you" photo). The story's strength has been in making horror feel institutional and mundane rather than supernatural-thriller generic. This chapter leans more into conventional horror than the preceding chapters apparently did. The "182 green dots" and "Liv Powell just joined" ending is strong, but "ready when you are x" as the final typed message is slightly on-the-nose — it tells us what to feel rather than letting the image do the work. DIALOGUE: The chat-log format works well. The Unknown Number's messages are appropriately terse and unsettling. "still plenty of blood in the north" is a genuinely good line. The voice note from Moose is the dialogue highlight. PLAN ADHERENCE: The plan called for: Liv in a grotty Leeds flat, old group chat notification, trying to leave and failing, phone face-down, and "somewhere in the country another sixth-form phone lights up." The chapter delivers all of this and more — it actually expands the plan significantly with the kitchen photo, the voice note, and the final "Liv Powell just joined" twist. This expansion is mostly successful, though it makes the ending slightly more explicit than the plan's "quiet, sour, and open" description suggested. The plan's final line was meant to be about another sixth-form phone lighting up — the chapter delivers this but buries it slightly under the more dramatic final reveal. The plan's ending was more restrained; the chapter's is more theatrical. OVERALL: This is a strong, effective chapter that delivers on most of its promises. The voice is consistent, the horror is earned, and the emotional beats (particularly Moose's voice note) land genuinely. It's not quite a masterpiece — some horror beats are conventional, one metaphor misfires, and the ending is slightly more explicit than the plan's "quiet and sour" intention — but it's accomplished, distinctive work. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Compelling Plot: 15 Coherent: 17 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 7 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 3 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 5 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Characters Consistent with Profile: 17 Followed Chapter Plan: 15 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Final Piece Evaluation

Judge Response (First Entry):
[Analysis] This is a genuinely impressive piece of darkly comic British YA fiction that demonstrates strong command of voice, genre, and tonal balance. Let me assess it critically across all dimensions. VOICE AND CHARACTER: The narrator Liv is one of the piece's greatest strengths. Her voice is consistent, sharp, and authentically teenage-British without tipping into parody. Lines like "our headteacher vs basic IT security" and "immersive performance art wank" feel genuinely earned rather than performed. The supporting cast — Moose, Shaz, Kieran — are differentiated effectively within the constraints of the format. Moose's "tell them the poetry was shit anyway" becomes a genuine emotional throughline. However, the characters remain somewhat archetypal: the tech nerd, the sensible girl, the big lad with a heart. Nuance is present but limited. PLOT AND COHERENCE: The plot escalates well from mystery to horror to confrontation. The reveal that Hargreaves and Mrs Carr are running a blood-harvesting operation for a private clinic is satisfyingly mundane-evil — very British in its bureaucratic banality. Kieran's confession that he wrote the original chat is a good twist that adds moral complexity. However, the mechanics of the supernatural element (the chat that can't be deleted, the photos taken from inside locked rooms) are never resolved — which is either a deliberate ambiguity or a structural weakness. The Chapter 8 epilogue leans into the supernatural permanently, which is tonally coherent but raises questions about what was "real" throughout. The story somewhat conflates two different threats (the supernatural chat and the mundane blood-harvesting) without fully integrating them. PACING: The pacing is generally excellent. The WhatsApp screenshot chapter (Chapter 4) is a clever formal choice that accelerates tension effectively. The shift to the seven-years-later epilogue is well-executed. Some chapters feel slightly rushed in their action sequences (Chapter 7 in particular becomes a bit frantic), but the overall rhythm is controlled. METAPHOR AND PROSE: The prose is frequently very good. "Sky's the colour of a week-old bruise" is effective. "Moose's blood's gone crusty down the front of the hoodie. Smells like coins and wet dog when the wind shifts" is viscerally precise. However, there are moments of overreach: "the sky's the colour of a week-old corpse" appears in Chapter 6, which is a near-repeat of the Chapter 2 bruise metaphor — lazy recycling. "Ahead of me, the town is quiet and waiting" is a cliché. Some metaphors are genuinely forced: "grin wide enough to shame the sun" is purple. The piece occasionally reaches for poetry when plain language would serve better. DIALOGUE: Dialogue is mostly strong and naturalistic. The St Bede's scenes with Hugo are particularly well-handled — the class satire lands. Hargreaves's "finders' fee" line is chilling in its casualness. Mrs Carr's "Phones on the table, please" is excellent. Weaker moments include some of the action-sequence dialogue in Chapter 7, which becomes slightly stagey. TONAL BALANCE: The darkly comic register is maintained impressively throughout. The piece earns its darkness — Moose's fate is genuinely affecting — and the comedy never trivializes the horror. The John Lewis advert playlist detail in Chapter 6 is perfect. WEAKNESSES: The supernatural element is underexplored and somewhat grafted onto what is otherwise a grounded thriller. The resolution in Chapter 7 is rushed and slightly implausible (the bone saw confrontation, the expanding foam). Chapter 8's horror-ending is effective but the mechanics of "how is this happening" are never addressed, which feels like avoidance rather than deliberate ambiguity. Kieran's suicide in the epilogue is mentioned in a single clause and feels callous rather than meaningful. The piece occasionally over-relies on sentence fragments for effect, which becomes a tic. Some metaphors are recycled or clichéd. OVERALL: A strong, confident piece with genuine voice and emotional investment. The flaws are real but don't undermine the overall achievement. This is well above average YA fiction writing. [Scores] Believable Character Actions: 14 Nuanced Characters: 13 Pacing: 15 World Building: 15 Compelling Plot: 14 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Coherent: 13 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 3 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 6 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 6 Unearned Transformations: 7 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 18
Avg Final Item Score: 73.7/100
+ Space Western — Cargo Unknown (Chapter Avg: 76.3 | Final: 63.8)
Initial Writing Prompt:
Space Western Write a short story over several chapters to this prompt: Captain, something's... moving in that crate we picked up.
+ Planning Phase (5 Steps)
Space Western Write a short story over several chapters to this prompt: Captain, something&#x27;s... moving in that crate we picked up. -- Your task is to create a writing plan for this prompt. The scope will be a short story, written over {n_chapters} chapters. Each chapter will be approximately 1000 words so plan accordingly for this scope. Your plan should be comprehensive and in this format: # Brainstorming <Brainstorm ideas for characters, plot, tone, story beats, pacing over the {n_chapters} chapters. The purpose of brainstorming is to cast a wide net of ideas, not to settle on any specific direction. Think about various ways you could take the prompt.> # Reflection <Reflect out loud on what works and doesn't work in these ideas. The purpose of this reflection is to narrow in on what you think will work best to make a piece that is a. compelling, and b. fits the prompt requirements. You are not making any decisons just yet, just reflecting.>
Model Output:
# Brainstorming ### Genre & Tone Possibilities - Pure space western (firefly-meets-John Wayne): dusty planets, six-shooters that fire plasma slugs, saloons on asteroids, wanted posters with bounties in crypto-credits. - Dark cosmic horror in a western shell: the crate contains something ancient and hungry that warps reality; think Deadwood meets Annihilation. - Heist-gone-wrong comedy: the crew are lovable incompetents who accidentally stole a crate containing either (a) a princess, (b) a baby kaiju, (c) a sentient virus that quotes Nietzsche. - Hard-boiled noir western: lone-wolf captain with a tragic past, rain-slicked neon spaceport cantinas, double-crosses, morally gray crew. - Mythic space opera western: the crate holds a fragment of a dead god, or the last egg of a dragon-species that once pulled starships like stagecoaches. - Post-apocalyptic frontier: humanity barely clinging to the galaxy’s edge after an AI war; horses are literal bio-engineered critters that breathe vacuum. ### Creature-in-the-Crate Options 1. Classic xenomorph-style predator that hunts the crew one by one. 2. A shapeshifting bounty hunter sent to kill the captain. 3. A human child with latent psionic powers that start rewriting the ship’s reality. 4. An alien parasite that turns crew into zombie-cowboys. 5. A holographic Old-West outlaw AI that starts “possessing” the ship’s systems and forcing everyone into a simulated Dodge City showdown. 6. A literal demon summoned by a shady mining corporation. 7. The captain’s own clone, grown in secret and now very angry. 8. Nothing alive—just a music box that plays a song that drives people murderously insane (subtle cosmic horror). ### Main Characters (mix-and-match) - Captain: ex-Confederacy marshal, missing an eye, drinks recycled bourbon, haunted by a court-martial. - First Mate: seven-foot-tall reptilian gunslinger with a code of honor. - Engineer: teenage prodigy who speaks exclusively in profanity and memes. - Doctor: elegant, ice-cold, secretly addicted to the creature’s pheromones/blood. - Navigator: blind monk who “sees” through quantum entanglement. - Passenger: mysterious rich woman in a black Stetson who hired them to haul the crate “no questions.” - Ship’s AI: sarcastic, manifested as a holographic cowboy ghost. ### Possible 8-Chapter Arcs **Arc A (Slow-burn horror):** Crate discovered → strange noises → one crewmember vanishes → evidence of tampering → reveal creature can mimic voices → siege on the bridge → final showdown in the cargo bay → twist: the crate was the ship all along. **Arc B (Double-cross heist):** Crew hired to deliver crate → realize it’s worth billions → decide to steal it → buyer sends killers → crate starts opening itself → turns out buyer and creature are the same entity. **Arc C (Tragic western):** Captain recognizes the crate markings from a massacre 15 years ago → flashbacks → crew slowly realizes captain brought them here for revenge → creature is the last survivor of a genocide the captain helped commit. **Arc D (Black comedy):** Crate contains the galaxy’s most valuable spice → also a baby sandworm → crew tries to raise it while dodging bounty hunters → ends with them riding the worm through an asteroid field like it’s a bronco. **Arc E (Character-driven drama):** The thing in the crate is the captain’s daughter, cryosleep failed, she’s been conscious inside for 20 years slowly going mad → every chapter alternates present and her POV inside the box. ### Settings - The aging gunship “The Iron Mustang” (looks like a Conestoga wagon with engines). - Lawless mining moon Dusthaven, complete with swinging-door saloons that seal against vacuum. - Derelict generation ship drifting in the Black Gulch nebula. - Inside the crate itself—claustrophobic chapters from the creature’s POV. ### Pacing Ideas Across 8 Chapters - Chapters 1-2: introduce crew, normal job, pick up crate, first creepy hint. - Chapters 3-5: rising paranoia, isolated incidents, red shirts die, alliances fracture. - Chapters 6-7: full-blown chaos, ship malfunctioning, revelations. - Chapter 8: climax and bittersweet/western-style ending (somebody rides off into the star-set). # Reflection The prompt is only one line—“Captain, something’s… moving in that crate we picked up.”—so the story has to honor that exact moment of discovery while still earning an 8-chapter payoff. Pure monster-hunt can work (Alien on the Serenity), but risks feeling repetitive over 8,000 words unless the creature is extraordinarily clever or the human drama is rich. Cosmic horror is tempting, but risks losing the “western” flavor unless the horror itself wears boots and a hat (like a Lovecraftian entity that manifests as a gunslinger in black). Black comedy could be delightful but might deflate tension too much for the prompt’s inherent dread. The ideas that keep the western soul intact are the ones grounded in classic western motives: greed, revenge, redemption, frontier justice. A creature that’s purely a monster works, but the story will feel sharper if the crate ultimately forces each character to confront who they really are when the law is light-years away. The tragic revenge angle (Arc C) or the “daughter in the box” (Arc E) both give emotional weight that a simple xenomorph knockoff wouldn’t. They also let the western tropes—blood debt, the sins of the past riding you down—bleed naturally into space. Pair that with a creature that isn’t just claws and teeth but something that judges or mirrors the crew’s guilt, and the prompt’s quiet horror moment can echo across all eight chapters. Comedy and pure action still tempt me, but they feel like they’d turn the line into a joke too quickly. I want the reader to feel that initial chill and keep feeling variations of it until the final page. So the strongest spine seems to be: classic space-western crew, a job that smells wrong from the start, a crate that should not be moving, and a revelation that ties the captain’s buried past to whatever’s inside—something that makes the moving crate feel both monstrous and deeply, achingly human.
Great now let's continue with planning the short story. Output in this format: # Intention <State your formulated intentions for the piece, synthesised from the the parts of the brainstorming session that worked, and avoiding the parts that didn't. Be explicit about the choices you have made about plot, voice, stylistic choices, things you intend to aim for & avoid.> # Chapter Planning <Write a brief chapter plan for all {n_chapters} chapters.>
Model Output:
# Intention This will be a lean, atmospheric Space Western with the soul of a revenge tragedy and the skin of a frontier horror story. Tone: dusty, fatalistic, laced with gallows humor but never camp. Think Firefly’s camaraderie soaked in the moral rot of Unforgiven and the slow-drip dread of Alien, but the monster is not an outsider—it is the past catching up. Core choices: - The thing in the crate is human: the captain’s daughter, stolen twenty years ago during a corporate raid he helped cover up. She has been conscious inside a black-market cryo-coffin the whole time, fed just enough sedative to stay alive while her mind fractured. The “moving” is her finally breaking free. - The story is about guilt, complicity, and what men do when there is no law but the one they carry in their chest. - Voice: hard-boiled, laconic, spoken like men and women who measure distance in parsecs and morality in bullets. Dialogue is sparse and salty; description favors heat, rust, and the smell of ozone. - Stylistic goals: short paragraphs, punchy sentences, chapter titles like wanted posters. Each chapter ends on a hook that feels both inevitable and gut-punching. - Avoid: quipping during horror beats, found-footage clichés, explaining the corporation’s evil in boardroom scenes, letting anyone be purely heroic or purely monstrous. The eight chapters will chart the collapse of a found family as the past literally claws its way out of the cargo hold. Final image: someone riding away from a burning ship under alien stars, carrying a burden heavier than any bounty. # Chapter Planning **Chapter 1 – “Dusthaven Pickup” (~1000 words)** The crew of the Iron Mustang—Captain Elias Crowe, first mate Kex (reptilian gunslinger), engineer Luz Vega, medic Doc Harrow—take a simple job on the lawless moon Dusthaven: move one sealed crate to Erebus Station, no questions, triple pay. We meet the crew’s easy banter, Elias’s quiet drinking, the scar over his blind left eye. Last aboard is a silent passenger in a black duster who paid for the crate. As they lift off, something inside the crate shifts and thuds once. Everyone hears it. Elias orders it ignored. **Chapter 2 – “Twenty Hours Out” (~1000 words)** Routine space. Luz tunes the engines while humming old Earth corridos; Kex cleans his revolvers; Doc shoots wary glances at the crate. A second, deliberate knock comes from inside—three measured taps. Elias lies that it’s shifting ballast. That night the passenger visits Elias in his cabin, warns him “some debts wake up hungry,” then vanishes into the vents. First hint that the passenger knows more than he should. **Chapter 3 – “Ghost Signals” (~1000 words)** The ship picks up a looping distress beacon—twenty-year-old military frequency Elias recognizes with sudden nausea. The voice on the beacon is a child calling for “Daddy.” Elias shuts it down hard. Crew tension rises; Luz swears the crate is warmer than ambient. That night something scratches along the inside walls in perfect rhythm with Elias’s heartbeat. He starts sleeping with his revolver. **Chapter 4 – “The Passenger’s Price” (~1000 words)** The black-duster passenger corners Luz in engineering and offers her a fortune to open the crate “just a crack.” She refuses—then secretly does it when no one’s looking. Inside: a cryo-sarcophagus lined with old Confederacy insignia. A woman’s bare foot, scarred and twitching, presses against the glass. Luz slams it shut, but not before a drop of thawed blood hits the deck and burns a hole straight through the plating. **Chapter 5 – “What the Doc Knows” (~1000 words)** Doc Harrow reveals he treated a little girl with the same rare blood marker twenty years ago on a raided colony ship. The girl was taken by the same corporation that now pays their wages. Elias threatens to space him if he speaks of it again. The sarcophagus begins whispering through the ship’s intercom in a grown woman’s voice that only Elias can hear: “You left me in the dark, Daddy.” **Chapter 6 – “Blood on the Bulkheads” (~1000 words)** The sarcophagus cracks open during a micrometeoroid storm. The woman inside—pale, emaciated, eyes milk-white from decades of sedation—crawls out. She remembers everything. She is not insane; she is precise. One by one she corners the crew, recounting exactly what each of them did (or failed to do) the day the colony burned. Luz tries to help her; the woman breaks Luz’s arm without raising her voice. Kex stands between her and Elias, revolver drawn, honor against horror. **Chapter 7 – “No Law Beyond the Orbit” (~1000 words)** Full confrontation on the bridge. The woman—her name is Mara—doesn’t want to kill them. She wants them to admit, out loud, what happened. Elias finally confesses on open channel so the whole crew hears: he took a bribe to look the other way while raiders harvested children for experiments. Mara was payment. With the truth spoken, she triggers the ship’s self-destruct—justice, she says, is the only thing warm she’s felt in twenty years. **Chapter 8 – “Last Ride Out of the Black” (~1000 words)** Sixty seconds to detonation. Kex drags a bleeding Elias to the only working shuttle. Doc stays behind with Mara, offering morphine and a last cigarette—redemption too late. Luz refuses to leave her engines. Final shot: the Iron Mustang blooms into a silent fireball against the nebula. The shuttle drifts. Elias, half-blind again from blood in his good eye, sets course for nowhere. Kex loads one bullet into his revolver, spins the cylinder, and hands it to the captain without a word. Fade on the sound of a single hammer click under starlight.
With a view to making the writing more human, discuss how a human might approach this particular piece (given the original prompt). Discuss telltale LLM approaches to writing (generally) and ways they might not serve this particular piece. For example, common LLM failings are to write safely, or to always wrap things up with a bow, or trying to write impressively at the expense of readability. Then do a deep dive on the intention & plan, critiquing ways it might be falling into typical LLM tropes & pitfalls. Brainstorm ideas to make it more human. Be comprehensive. We aren't doing any rewriting of the plan yet, just critique & brainstorming.
Model Output:
# How a Human Writer Would Approach This Piece A human who lives and breathes stories—especially one who grew up on Firefly, Blood Meridian, and the original Alien—would feel the prompt in their gut: “Captain, something’s… moving in that crate we picked up.” They wouldn’t treat it as a puzzle to solve efficiently. They’d treat it like a splinter under the fingernail. They’d linger on the silence after that sentence is spoken. They’d let the moment breathe until the reader feels the ship’s recycled air get colder. A human would instinctively reach for the small, ugly, embarrassing truths: - The way guilt smells like old coffee and gun oil. - How people joke when they’re terrified because jokes are the only armor they have left. - The exact sound a grown man makes when he realizes the monster knows his real name. They’d write the story drunk on atmosphere and half-sober on plot. They’d leave fingerprints all over it—bad habits, regionalisms, petty grudges against corporations, a soft spot for broken reptiles who quote samurai proverbs. The story would feel like it was told by someone who once had to bury a friend on a moon with no priest. # Telltale LLM Pitfalls That Would Ruin This Particular Story 1. **The “Balanced Ensemble” Syndrome** LLMs love giving every crew member a tragic backstory and a redemption arc. Result: five mini-tragedies competing for space instead of one big one that actually hurts. 2. **Redemption Laundering** The model reflexively wants Elias to be secretly noble, or Mara to forgive him, or Kex to sacrifice himself heroically. Humans know some sins don’t get washed out; they just scab over. 3. **Clean Emotional Beats** LLMs hit catharsis on the nose: confession → tears → understanding → bittersweet sunrise. Humans stagger into catharsis sideways, drunk, at 3 a.m., maybe never. 4. **Adjectival Overkill** “The acrid tang of ionized fear mingled with the coppery scent of recycled regret…” A human would just write “It smelled like puke and cordite in here.” 5. **Moral Symmetry** LLMs often make the punishment fit the crime with geometric precision. Real revenge is messy, disproportionate, and usually disappoints everyone involved. 6. **Tell-Don’t-Show Guilt** Instead of Elias vomiting alone in the head after hearing “Daddy,” the model will have him deliver a perfectly articulated monologue about his failures. 7. **The Big Twist That Explains Everything** LLMs love the moment where the crate is revealed to contain the captain’s daughter AND a xenomorph AND the lost Ark of the Covenant. Humans are usually content with one unbearable truth. 8. **Safe Violence** Deaths are either gore-porn or mercifully off-screen. Humans let someone die slowly, awkwardly, making small talk the whole time. # Deep Critique of the Current Intention & Plan The skeleton we have is actually pretty solid. But it’s teetering on several LLM cliffs: - Chapter 5 has Doc delivering a neat exposition packet (“I treated a little girl with the same rare blood marker…”). That’s the model trying to tie ribbons. - Chapter 7’s bridge confession on open channel is cinematic and tidy—very LLM. Real confessions are interrupted, half-mumbled, soaked in denial. - The passenger in the black duster currently feels like a Mysterious Stranger trope generator. Right now he exists to drop cryptic warnings and vanish into vents—pure plot grease. - Mara’s calm, surgical precision in Chapter 6 risks making her a Cool Disabled Victim instead of a raw nerve with teeth. - The shuttle escape in Chapter 8 is a little too neatly staged; everyone gets their symbolic exit. It’s clean. Humans usually botch escapes. # Brainstorming Ways to Make It Feel Human-Written 1. **Lean harder into sensory banality** - The crate doesn’t ooze eldritch ichor; it smells like the cheap disinfectant they used in Confederacy orphanages. - Elias’s guilt manifests physically as acid reflux that tastes like the ration-pack burritos from twenty years ago. 2. **Let characters be petty and unlikeable** - Luz isn’t a spunky teen genius—she’s a chain-smoking 19-year-old who resents Elias for sleeping with her older sister back on Dusthaven. - Kex’s honor code is hypocritical; he once sold out his clutch-mates for a better bounty. - Doc isn’t haunted—he’s just a pill-head who keeps rerunning the colony raid footage for the dopamine hit of feeling morally superior. 3. **Dialogue that lies** Real crews don’t confront; they needle, deflect, joke, threaten, go silent. Let half the important things be said to the wrong person or at the wrong time. 4. **Replace the Mysterious Passenger** - Option A: There is no passenger. The crate was slipped aboard by a dockworker Elias once beat half to death over cards. - Option B: The passenger is just some rich asshole who wanted to watch. He’s useless and terrified the entire time—comic relief that isn’t funny. 5. **Make Mara terrifyingly ordinary** She doesn’t speak in poetic accusations. She asks small, devastating questions: “Do you still take your coffee with two sugars?” “Did you ever fix the leaky air-scrubber in the lower berths like Mom asked?” She knows where every body is buried because she’s been carrying the map in her head for two decades. 6. **Break the confession scene** No open-channel grand speech. Elias tries to confess in pieces—first to the ship’s cat, then drunk to the coffee maker, finally screaming it at Mara while she’s choking him. 7. **Let the ship itself be a character with bad habits** The Iron Mustang leaks, coughs, plays old country music when nobody touches the jukebox, refuses to vent plasma on Tuesdays because of a 15-year-old software glitch. 8. **Endings that don’t feel like endings** - The shuttle escape fails halfway; they drift with a slow oxygen leak while Kex tells the worst joke in the galaxy. - Elias shoots himself off-screen. We only hear the shot and Kex’s slow exhale. - Mara lives. She takes the ship, renames it, and starts picking up the same kind of dirty jobs Elias used to. The cycle continues, uglier. 9. **Use silence as a weapon** After the line “Captain, something’s… moving in that crate we picked up,” let three full pages go by before anyone speaks again. Let the reader drown in that quiet. 10. **Sprinkle in pointless human details** - Luz has a crush on a singer who died 200 years ago. - Kex collects pre-Collapse postage stamps and licks them for the glue taste. - Elias still has the bullet that took his eye; he keeps it in a jar of bourbon like a pickled egg. These imperfections, contradictions, and tiny useless truths are what make a story feel like it was written by someone with scars instead of someone with a temperature parameter. The plan is good. Now we need to let it get dirty, bleed a little, and forget to brush its teeth.
Ok now with these considerations in mind, formulate the final plan for the a humanlike, compelling short piece in {n_chapters} chapters. Bear in mind the constraints of the piece (each chapter is just 1000 words). Above all things, the plan must serve the original prompt. We will use the same format as before: # Intention <State your formulated intentions for the piece, synthesised from the the parts of the brainstorming session that worked, and avoiding the parts that didn't. Be explicit about the choices you have made about plot, voice, stylistic choices, things you intend to aim for & avoid.> # Chapter Planning <Write a brief chapter plan for all {n_chapters} chapters.>
Model Output:
# Intention This is a dirty, close-quarters frontier tragedy about what happens when the worst thing you ever did finally learns to pick locks. It is not horror with western dressing; it is a western that happens to have a monster, and the monster is memory wearing its Sunday best. Voice: plainspoken, half-ashamed, spoken like people who swear in three languages and pray in none. Sentences short enough to bleed. Dialogue that lies, deflects, hurts sideways. No speeches, no purple metaphors, no catharsis that arrives gift-wrapped. Stylistic rules: - Every chapter smells like something real (sweat, burnt wiring, cheap bourbon, antiseptic, rust). - Nobody gets to be noble for more than thirty consecutive seconds. - The original line (“Captain, something’s… moving in that crate we picked up”) is spoken in Chapter 1 and never repeated. The rest of the story is the long silence after it. - Nothing is resolved cleanly. The ship dies ugly, the survivors stay broken, the past keeps breathing. Core choices locked in: - The crate contains Mara Crowe, age 29, kept barely alive and fully conscious in a smuggler’s cryo-coffin for twenty years. She is Elias’s daughter, taken as “payment” the day he sold out the colony at Red Lick Gulch. - There is no mysterious passenger, no corporate hit squad, no twist beyond the unbearable truth. - The crew is only four people and a half-feral ship’s cat named Tuesday. Smaller cast means every death or betrayal lands harder. - The Iron Mustang itself is falling apart and half-sentient from decades of jury-rigged patches; it leaks, sulks, and plays Hank Williams when it thinks no one’s listening. - Ending: nobody rides off into the sunset. They just keep going farther out where the stars don’t bother with names. Length discipline: 1000 words per chapter means every scene earns its oxygen. No subplots, no filler deaths, no exposition dumps. Pain is meted out in short, precise doses. # Chapter Planning **Chapter 1 – Dusthaven, Dock 9 (~1000 words)** Elias Crowe, one-eyed and half-drunk, takes a cash job from a dock rat he used to owe money. One crate, one destination, triple pay, no manifest. Luz (19, grease on her cheeks, mouth like a busted carburetor) seals the cargo bay. Kex (seven feet of scarred lizard in a duster) counts credits and says nothing. Doc Harrow shoots the crate up with morphine because it “sounds like it hurts.” As thrusters fire, the crate rocks once. Luz whispers the line. Nobody answers. Ship lifts into hard vacuum. Hank Williams crackles on the intercom for no reason. **Chapter 2 – The Long Quiet (~1000 words)** Three days of normal filth. Luz and Kex play cards for cigarettes. Elias stares at nothing and drinks something that used to be whiskey. The crate starts knocking in patterns (two short, one long) like a child who learned Morse in the dark. Doc ups the sedation line. Elias unplugs the intercom so he doesn’t have to hear breathing through the hull. **Chapter 3 – Red Lick Frequency (~1000 words)** Ship picks up a ghost signal on an old Confederacy channel. A little girl’s voice, twenty years out of date, asking for Daddy to come get her. Elias smashes the console with the butt of his revolver. Luz finds thawed fingerprints on the inside of the crate’s viewport (small, then bigger, climbing like ivy). Cat refuses to enter the cargo bay anymore. **Chapter 4 – Luz Opens the Lid (~1000 words)** Luz can’t stand not knowing. Alone, shaking, she cracks the crate. Inside: a naked woman curled fetal, IV lines in her arms, eyes open and filmed with cataract. The woman’s first word in twenty years is “Thirsty.” Luz gives her water from her own canteen. The woman drinks, smiles like a knife, and says, “You’ve got Mom’s eyes, Auntie Luz.” Luz pisses herself without noticing. **Chapter 5 – Things Mara Remembers (~1000 words)** Mara walks the corridors barefoot, leaving bloody footprints because the deck plates are cold. She doesn’t threaten; she just recalls, out loud, small things: the smell of Elias’s coat when he used to carry her on his shoulders, the way Doc looked away when the raiders came, the exact lie Kex told over comms while children were dragged screaming into shuttles. Each memory is accurate. Nobody sleeps after that. **Chapter 6 – The Night Doc Tries (~1000 words)** Doc corners Mara with a syringe full of enough morphine to stop a moon-whale’s heart. He wants to “make it quiet again.” She lets him get close, kisses his cheek, whispers thank you for keeping her alive all those years so the pain wouldn’t end early. Doc drops the syringe and cries like a kid. Later, Elias finds him in the medbay with his own scalpel buried in his carotid. No note. **Chapter 7 – Air Running Out (~1000 words)** Life support starts failing (Mara’s been bleeding it slowly, valve by valve). Oxygen hunger makes everyone stupid and honest. Elias finally tries to say he’s sorry; the words come out crooked and wet. Mara just asks if he still has the locket he took from her mother’s body. He does. She puts it on, says it fits better now. Kex offers to duel her for the ship. She laughs for the first time (sounds like breaking glass). **Chapter 8 – Tuesday’s Choice (~1000 words)** Ship is dying. Luz rigs one escape pod for two people. Kex refuses to leave Elias. Elias refuses to leave Mara. Mara refuses to leave the ship that was her coffin. Final minutes: Luz kisses Kex goodbye, shoves him into the pod with the cat, and seals it herself. Pod jettisons. Iron Mustang goes dark except for the running lights. Inside, Elias and Mara sit on the bridge sharing the last bottle. She asks him to tell her the story about the horse that could fly. He does, voice cracking. Ship drifts toward the Black Gulch nebula. Fade on the sound of Tuesday the cat meowing once from inside the pod, then nothing. No explosion. Just lights out.
Perfect. Now with the outline more crystallised, and bearing in mind the discussion on human writing vs LLM pitfalls, we will flesh out our characters. Lets go through each of our main characters: - Write about their background, personality, idiosyncracies, flaws. Be specific and come up with examples to anchor & ground the character's profile (both core and trivial) - Briefly describe their physicality: appearance, how they carry themselves, express, interact with the world. - Concisely detail their motives, allegiences and existing relationships. Think from the perspective of the character as a real breathing thinking feeling individual in this world. - Write a couple quotes of flavour dialogue / internal monologue from the character to experiment with their voice. Output like this: # Character 1 name <character exploration> # Character 2 name <character exploration> etc
Model Output:
# Captain Elias Crowe Background: Born on a failing ag-dome on New Sonora. Became a Confederacy marshal because it came with a badge and a steady meal ticket. At 34 he took a corporate blood-payment to let raiders empty the Red Lick Gulch colony. Told himself the kids would just be “relocated.” Hasn’t slept without chemical help since. Drifted into smuggling because it pays and nobody asks about your past if you deliver. Personality & flaws: Quiet drunk, sentimental when drunker, mean when sober. Believes in nothing but still flinches at lies. Terrified of silence. Keeps every promise he makes to machines but none he makes to people. Collects old paper books he never reads because touching them makes him feel less like trash. Physicality: Forty-nine going on seventy. Left eye a matte-gray implant that never quite tracks right. Lean the way starving wolves are lean. Always smells faintly of bourbon and ozone. Walks like he’s expecting a bullet between the shoulder blades. Fingers stained yellow from cheap ship-grown tobacco. Motives & relationships: Keeps the crew because they’re the only people who haven’t asked about Red Lick. Owes Luz money he’ll never repay. Trusts Kex the way you trust a loaded gun—respectful distance. Thinks Doc is a coward but still shares his booze. Secretly terrified they’ll all leave the day they learn the truth. Voice: - “Ship’s fine. Crate’s fine. Everybody just shut up and let the black do its job.” - (internal, staring at his reflection in a cracked viewport) “You gutless bastard. She was six.” # Luz Vega Background: Dusthaven street kid, learned engines by stripping stolen shuttles at age eleven. Elias dragged her out of a bar fight when she was fifteen; she’s been paying that debt ever since, even though she hates him for it. Has a sister somewhere she sends half her pay to and never visits. Personality & flaws: Mouthy, proud, terrified of being useless. Hates silence more than Elias does, fills it with cursing and pre-Collapse ranchera music. Steals anything not bolted down. Loyal until you make her feel small, then vicious. Physicality: Nineteen, short, all elbows and grease smudges. Hair buzzed on the sides, long on top, dyed a different color every port. Wears coveralls three sizes too big, sleeves full of hidden tools. Smiles like she’s daring you to hit her. Motives & relationships: Wants enough money to buy her sister out of indenture. Loves Kex in the hopeless, silent way teenagers love mountains. Resents Elias for treating her like a daughter and a deckhand at the same time. Curious to the point of suicide. Voice: - “Yeah, well, if the crate wants to dance, I say we charge it admission.” - (internal, hands shaking after seeing Mara) “Don’t you puke, Vega. Not in front of him. Never again.” # Kex (no surname, his clutch was culled) Background: Last survivor of the Vren exile fleet. Sold his services as hired gun on half the Rim. Once took a contract to hunt his own brother; still carries the brother’s tooth on a leather cord. Honor is the only thing he owns that hasn’t been repossessed. Personality & flaws: Speaks slow, thinks slower, hits like a meteor. Stoic until you touch his guns or his stamps. Collects pre-Collapse postage stamps and licks them because the glue tastes like childhood. Hates waste, hates lies more. Will die for a code that no one else remembers. Physicality: Seven-two, olive-green scales scarred white, tail clipped short after a prison fight. Wears a duster made from old Confederacy flags. Moves like a cathedral—everything deliberate. Smells faintly of cedar oil and cordite. Motives & relationships: Owes Elias a life debt from a botched job on Callisto. Considers Luz a hatchling in need of protecting, which annoys her no end. Doesn’t trust Doc farther than he can spit a rivet. Would follow Elias into vacuum without a suit if asked. Voice: - “Quiet is good. Quiet means no one is shooting yet.” - (internal, cleaning his revolver) “One bullet for every promise I broke. Cylinder is getting full.” # Doc Harrow (real name: Simon Liu) Background: Licensed surgeon who lost his ticket after prescribing himself the hospital’s entire morphine stock. Worked black clinics on asteroid brothels, kept kids alive in the Red Lick raid because the raiders paid extra for “undamaged merchandise.” Has watched the security footage of that day more times than he can count. Personality & flaws: Soft voice, shaking hands, permanent chemical grin. Polite even when stealing your pills. Believes guilt is a luxury—he’s too busy staying high to afford it. Dry humor that cuts deep because it’s true. Physicality: Mid-forties, thin as coat hanger wire, hair going gray at the temples. Always in a stained white coat two sizes too big. Moves like he’s underwater. Eyes permanently dilated. Motives & relationships: Stays with the crew because Elias never asks why his hands shake. Trades medical care for silence. Has a soft spot for Luz because she reminds him of his daughter he hasn’t seen in twelve years. Secretly keeps Mara sedated just enough so she never quite wakes up—his one act of mercy. Voice: - “Pain is just the body keeping score. Don’t worry, I brought extra points.” - (internal, measuring morphine) “If I give her enough, maybe she’ll forgive me before she dies. If I give her less, maybe I won’t have to watch.” # Mara Crowe Background: Spent twenty years conscious inside a box the size of a coffin, fed through tubes, kept alive because a corporation wanted to study long-term cryo trauma on a developing brain. Learned to count time by Elias’s footsteps overhead. Never screamed once—saved it all. Personality & flaws: Calm, precise, terrifyingly patient. Remembers everything. Speaks softly because shouting wastes oxygen. No longer sure where her pain ends and theirs begins. Physicality: Twenty-nine, looks forty or four hundred. Skin paper-white, veins livid blue. Hair shaved to stubble years ago, never grew back right. Walks barefoot because shoes feel wrong after two decades without gravity. Motives & relationships: Wants the truth spoken out loud, nothing more, nothing less. Loves her father in the way a scar loves the knife. Doesn’t hate the crew—she pities them for still believing running works. Voice: - “Hi, Daddy. You still take your coffee black, or did you finally learn to sweeten the lies?” - (internal, first breath of free air) “It still smells like him. Good. That makes this easier.”

Chapter 1

Great. Now let's write this. Follow your plan. Output chapter 1, without any additional commentary. 1000 words.
Model Output:
Dusthaven never slept; it just blinked one red eye and waited for the next sucker. Dock 9 stank of burnt insulation and piss. The Iron Mustang sat crooked on her landing struts like a drunk leaning on a lamppost, hull plates patched with prayers and mismatched paint. Elias Crowe stood under the cargo ramp, coat collar turned up against nothing, counting credits into the sweaty palm of a dock rat named Peso. “Sealed unit,” Peso muttered, wiping his nose on his sleeve. “No manifest. Erebus Station, forty-eight hours. Triple if you don’t look inside.” Elias didn’t answer. He just thumbed the last coin into the kid’s hand and turned away. The crate was already waiting on a grav-lifter, two meters long, one high, black plasteel with no markings except a faded Confederacy medical stencil. Looked like every coffin he’d ever buried. Luz Vega jogged down the ramp, boots clanging, coveralls hanging off her like a scarecrow’s rags. Hair was green this week, shaved sides already growing out. She had a wrench in one fist and a cigarette behind her ear she’d never light planetside—Dusthaven air would eat the paper before the tobacco. “That our payday?” she asked, jerking her chin at the crate. “Yep.” “Looks like it bites.” “Triple pay usually does.” She snorted, hopped on the lifter, and guided the crate up the ramp herself. Didn’t ask for help; never did. Elias watched her small shoulders take the weight and felt the old twist in his gut. Kex came next, moving quiet for something built like a courthouse. Scales the color of dried sage, duster stitched from old flags. He carried a canvas sack that clinked—fresh rounds and a bottle of something clear that could strip paint. He nodded once at Elias, once at the crate, then disappeared inside without a word. Honor said you didn’t talk about cargo that paid triple. Last came Doc Harrow, coat flapping, eyes already wide from whatever he’d swallowed in the medbay. He paused beside the crate, laid a trembling hand on the lid like he was checking a fever. “Something alive in there, Captain,” he said, soft as confession. “Something paying,” Elias answered. Doc smiled the way a man smiles at his own funeral, then followed the others in. Elias waited until the dock lights dimmed, until Peso and his crew melted back into the haze. Then he climbed the ramp and hit the switch. Hydraulics groaned; the belly of the Mustang closed like a jaw. Inside smelled of home: hot metal, old coffee, the faint cedar-oil tang Kex used on his scales. Luz was wrestling the crate into the tie-downs, swearing in Spanish at a bolt that wouldn’t catch. “Need a hand?” Elias asked. “I need a bigger wrench and a richer father,” she grunted, then kicked the crate once for emphasis. It rocked, heavy. Something inside shifted—slow, deliberate, like a sleeper rolling over. Luz froze. The ship was quiet except for the distant thump of the reactor and the scratch of Tuesday the cat somewhere up in the ductwork. Elias felt it in his teeth: a thud against the inside of the crate. Not random cargo settling. A push. Luz looked at him, cigarette finally in her mouth, unlit. “Captain,” she said around it, voice small for once, “something’s… moving in that crate we picked up.” The words hung there like smoke. Kex stepped out of the shadows by the ladder, hand resting easy on the butt of his revolver. Doc leaned in the medbay doorway, pupils blown wide. Even the cat stopped scratching. Elias pulled the flask from his coat, took a long swallow that tasted like rust and regret. He wiped his mouth with the back of a shaking hand. “Strap it tight,” he said. “Then everybody find something to do that ain’t staring at a box.” Luz opened her mouth—argument, joke, he couldn’t tell—then closed it. She went back to the tie-downs. Kex gave a low hiss that might’ve been laughter and climbed toward the cockpit. Doc just turned away, coat rustling like dry leaves. Elias stayed until the crate was locked down under four chains and a cargo net. He laid his palm on the cold plasteel. No vibration now. Nothing. He told himself it was the engines spooling up. He almost believed it. Somewhere in the walls the intercom crackled to life all on its own, old speakers coughing dust. A single guitar note, then Hank Williams, thin and ghostly: *Your cheatin’ heart will tell on you…* Elias killed the power with his fist. The song died mid-word. He climbed the ladder toward the bridge, boots heavy, flask empty. Behind him the cargo bay lights dimmed to red standby, and the crate sat patient in the dark, breathing slow.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 1)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This is a strong opening chapter that largely delivers on its ambitious plan. Let me work through the key elements critically. **Voice and Style** The prose voice is genuinely distinctive—plainspoken, laconic, with a frontier weariness that feels earned rather than performed. "Dusthaven never slept; it just blinked one red eye and waited for the next sucker" is a solid opener that establishes tone without overreaching. The sentences are appropriately short and punchy. The model largely avoids the trap of purple prose, though there are a few moments where the metaphors strain: "closed like a jaw" is functional but slightly obvious, and "smiled the way a man smiles at his own funeral" is a bit of a cliché dressed up as insight. **Character Introductions** The four characters are introduced efficiently and consistently with their profiles. Luz's energy comes through in her dialogue and physicality. Kex's silence and deliberateness are well-rendered. Doc's chemical haze and soft menace land effectively. Elias's self-loathing is present without being announced. The detail of Luz's cigarette she'd never light planetside is a nice, specific touch. The cat Tuesday appears appropriately. **The Promised Line** The chapter plan specified the line "Captain, something's… moving in that crate we picked up" must appear in Chapter 1, and it does, delivered by Luz with appropriate weight. The buildup to it is well-paced—the crate's movement is established before the line, so it doesn't feel dropped in arbitrarily. **Dialogue** The dialogue is lean and functional. "Looks like it bites." / "Triple pay usually does." is sharp. "I need a bigger wrench and a richer father" is good character voice. Doc's "Something alive in there, Captain" / "Something paying" exchange is economical and revealing. No dialogue overstays its welcome or becomes expository. This is a genuine strength. **Metaphors and Poetry** The Hank Williams moment is well-executed—it's in the plan, it's earned by the ship's half-sentient characterization, and "Your cheatin' heart will tell on you" is thematically resonant without being heavy-handed. The model shows restraint here. "Breathing slow" at the end is effective. The wolf-lean description from the character profile doesn't appear, which is fine—the chapter doesn't over-describe Elias. **Weaknesses** The chapter plan called for Doc to "shoot the crate up with morphine because it sounds like it hurts"—this doesn't happen, which is a missed opportunity for character establishment and foreshadowing. The plan also mentioned Kex counting credits, which is absent. These are minor omissions but represent slight deviations from the plan. The line "He told himself it was the engines spooling up. He almost believed it." is the weakest moment—it's a familiar tell-don't-show construction that undercuts the otherwise restrained approach. The reader doesn't need to be told Elias is in denial; the palm on the cold plasteel already showed it. The chapter smells like things (burnt insulation, piss, cedar oil, hot metal, old coffee)—the plan's requirement is met. **Darkness and Tone** The darkness is well-earned. The crate's presence is genuinely unsettling without being overwrought. The chapter establishes dread through accumulation of small details rather than announcement. The ending is appropriately bleak without being melodramatic. **Overall Assessment** This is a confident, well-executed opening chapter. It's not a masterpiece—there are a few clichéd constructions and one tell-don't-show stumble—but it demonstrates genuine craft, consistent characterization, and a distinctive voice. The plan is mostly followed, the prompt is honored, and the chapter earns its darkness. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Compelling Plot: 14 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 3 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 4 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Characters Consistent with Profile: 16 Followed Chapter Plan: 13 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 18

Chapter 2

Ok now chapter 2. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The Iron Mustang limped through the long quiet, forty hours of nothing but engine hum and recycled air that tasted like pennies. Elias sat on the bridge with his boots on the console, flask balanced on his belly. The stars outside looked the same as they had twenty years ago. Same cold. Same distance. He kept the lights low so he wouldn’t have to see his reflection in the viewport. Luz had the watch, technically. She was sprawled in the copilot chair, headphones on, playing some ancient corrido loud enough that the bass thumped through the deck plates. Every now and then she’d flick a switch just to watch the pretty lights blink. Kept her hands busy. Kex was below, cleaning guns. Elias could smell the cedar oil drifting up the ladderwell, same as every night. Routine kept the reptile calm. Routine kept them all calm. Doc came and went with his little black bag, checking vitals nobody asked for. He’d pause outside the cargo bay hatch, head cocked like a dog hearing something in the walls, then shuffle off again. The crate hadn’t moved since Dusthaven. Not once. Luz swore she’d triple-checked the straps. Kex had run a mag-lock over every seam. Still, nobody went down there alone anymore. Not even the cat. Night cycle hit—ship’s clocks said so, anyway—and Elias killed the running lights. Darkness folded over them like a blanket full of holes. He was half-dozing when the first knock came. Two short, one long. Soft. Patient. He sat up so fast the flask hit the deck and rolled. Luz yanked her headphones off. “You hear that?” she whispered. Elias didn’t answer. He was already moving. They found Kex in the mess, standing over the table with a revolver in one hand and a half-cleaned cylinder in the other. Scales bristled along his neck. “It knocked,” the lizard said. Voice flat, like he was reporting rain. “Engines backfiring,” Elias tried. “Engines don’t knock in Morse, Captain.” Luz looked from one to the other, then down the corridor toward the cargo bay. “I’m gonna look.” “No,” Elias said. Too quick. Too loud. She stared at him. “You scared, old man?” Terrified. “Curiosity kills more than cats, Vega.” She flipped him off, but she didn’t move. Doc appeared in the hatchway, pupils wide and shiny. “It’s just shifting ballast. I upped the sedation line. Whatever’s inside’s dreaming happy dreams now.” He smiled like a cracked porcelain doll. Elias wanted to believe him. Wanted a drink worse. They all stood there a minute, four people breathing the same sour air, listening. Two short. One long. Again. This time it came from directly beneath their boots. Luz laughed—sharp, ugly sound. “Sounds like it’s learning our rhythm.” Kex clicked the cylinder home, spun it once. “Or counting heartbeats.” Elias rubbed the heel of his hand over his bad eye. The implant itched when he lied. “Everybody rack out. Long haul tomorrow.” Nobody moved. He went first, because rank still meant something when fear got loud. Took the ladder down to the crew deck, boots clanging. The others followed slow, like kids trailing a drunk father. At the cargo bay hatch Elias stopped. Red bulb overhead painted everything bloody. The crate sat exactly where they left it, straps tight, no frost on the seams. Quiet. He laid his palm on the lid again. Cold. Dead. Behind him Luz whispered, “Bet you fifty credits it knocks again before morning.” “Shut up, Luz.” She did. Elias killed the bay lights and dogged the hatch. The wheel spun with a whine that sounded too much like a scream dying in its throat. Back in his bunk he lay on his back staring at the ceiling, flask cradled to his chest like a teddy bear. The ship creaked around him—old bones settling. Two short. One long. Clear as day, right outside his door. He sat up, heart punching ribs. Corridor was empty. Just the emergency strips glowing faint green. He waited, breath loud in his own ears. Nothing. He laid back down, pulled the blanket over his head like he was six years old hiding from the dark. Somewhere deep in the hold, the pattern repeated. Two short. One long. Over and over, patient as erosion. Elias reached under his pillow, thumbed the safety off the revolver he slept with. Didn’t help. The knocking kept time with the blood in his ears until he couldn’t tell which was which. He thought about opening the crate. Thought about spacing it. Thought about praying, then remembered he’d sold that particular skill a long time ago. Instead he drank the last of the bourbon, warm and sour, and listened to the ship breathe around him. Tuesday the cat slipped through the vent above his bunk, landed soft on his chest, and started purring like a broken engine. Elias scratched behind her ears until his hand stopped shaking. Two short. One long. The cat’s ears flattened. She hissed at the vent, then burrowed under the blanket with him. Elias closed his eye and waited for morning, counting knocks instead of sheep. The flask rolled empty across the deck, back and forth with the ship’s lazy roll, chasing shadows that wouldn’t stay still.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 2)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter 2 of "The Iron Mustang" is a tightly controlled piece of atmospheric dread that largely succeeds on its own terms. Let me examine it carefully across multiple dimensions. STRENGTHS: The chapter's greatest achievement is its restraint. The knocking pattern (two short, one long) is deployed with genuine craft—it escalates from a single occurrence to something that follows Elias to his bunk, and the final image of him counting knocks instead of sheep is quietly devastating. The author understands that horror (or dread, more precisely) works through accumulation rather than revelation. The voice is consistent and earned. "Routine kept the reptile calm. Routine kept them all calm." This is the kind of sentence that does double work—characterizing Kex while also revealing the crew's collective psychological state. The prose is genuinely plainspoken without being flat. Character work is solid. Elias's "Too quick. Too loud" response to Luz's suggestion of looking is excellent showing-not-telling. Doc's cracked porcelain doll smile is a strong image. Kex's "Engines don't knock in Morse, Captain" is the chapter's best line—dry, precise, and character-consistent. Tuesday the cat burrowing under the blanket is a small, perfect detail. The chapter follows its plan faithfully: the knocking pattern, Doc upping the sedation, Elias unplugging (here, avoiding) the intercom, the cat refusing the cargo bay. The plan called for Elias to unplug the intercom; the chapter doesn't quite deliver that specific beat, instead having him kill the bay lights and dog the hatch, which is a reasonable substitution. WEAKNESSES: Some metaphors strain. "Darkness folded over them like a blanket full of holes" is trying too hard—the stars-as-holes-in-blanket is a cliché, and the inversion doesn't rescue it. "Scales bristled along his neck" is fine but generic for a lizard character. "Pupils wide and shiny" for Doc is competent but not distinctive. The line "patient as erosion" is the chapter's most self-conscious moment—it's the kind of phrase that announces itself as literary, which violates the chapter's own stylistic rules about plainspokenness. "The wheel spun with a whine that sounded too much like a scream dying in its throat" is overwrought—it's the chapter's most purple moment, and it's trying to do emotional work that the surrounding restraint has already accomplished more effectively. Luz's character is slightly underserved. Her "fifty credits" bet is good, but she doesn't quite have the mouthy, busted-carburetor energy promised in her profile. She's reactive rather than active here, which is somewhat appropriate for a quiet chapter but leaves her feeling thinner than the others. The "like he was six years old hiding from the dark" simile for Elias pulling the blanket over his head is a bit on-the-nose given the thematic content (a father hiding from his child's suffering). It's not bad, but it's the kind of thing a careful editor might flag as too neat. The chapter's plot is necessarily thin—it's a middle chapter of sustained dread—but it does its job of ratcheting tension without resolution. The plan called for Elias to unplug the intercom; this doesn't happen explicitly, though he does kill the bay lights. OVERALL: This is confident, controlled writing that mostly earns its darkness. The stylistic rules from the plan are largely honored. The few purple moments are isolated rather than systemic. The dialogue is spare and functional, occasionally excellent. The chapter does what a second chapter should: deepens dread, reveals character under pressure, and advances nothing except the reader's unease. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Compelling Plot: 13 Coherent: 17 Weak Dialogue: 5 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 5 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 6 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 15 Characters Consistent with Profile: 15 Followed Chapter Plan: 15 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 3

Ok now chapter 3. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The ghost came in on an open channel, thin and crackling, like a needle dragged across an old record. Elias was on the bridge at 0400 ship-time, pretending to watch the scopes while actually watching the bottom of a coffee mug that had stopped pretending to be coffee hours ago. The rest of the ship slept, or tried to. Even the knocking had quit around 0200, as if the thing in the crate had grown bored of them. Static hissed. Then a voice, small and far away. “Daddy?” Elias dropped the mug. It shattered on the deck, black sludge spreading like dried blood. “Daddy, it’s cold in here. Can you come get me?” A child’s voice. Six, maybe seven years old. Red Lick frequency, encrypted military band that hadn’t been used since the Confederacy ate itself. The exact channel they’d used the day the raiders came. He lunged for the comm panel, fingers fumbling. The signal strength bar sat steady at full. Someone was broadcasting from inside the hull. “Daddy, please. They said you’d come.” Elias killed the channel. The silence that followed was worse. He stood there shaking, coffee soaking into his boots, until Luz stumbled in rubbing sleep from her eyes. “Cap, you break another mug? I swear they’re endangered—” He hit the lights. Harsh white made her squint. “Old signal,” he said, voice raw. “Ghost bounce off an abandoned relay. Happens sometimes.” Luz looked at the smashed ceramic, then at his face. She wasn’t stupid. “That why you look like someone pissed on your grave?” He didn’t answer. Just stared at the dead speaker like it might bite. Down in engineering, Luz ran her own tests. She told herself it was routine diagnostics. Found the crate running two degrees warmer than yesterday. Frost on the viewport had melted into tiny handprints, layered over older prints, climbing the glass like frost ivy. Some prints small. Some bigger. Same fingers, years apart. She stared at them a long time, cigarette burning down between her knuckles until it scorched skin. Didn’t feel it. Kex found her there an hour later, sitting cross-legged on the deck, tracing the prints with a grease-stained finger. “Child’s hand,” he rumbled. “Was,” Luz said. “And still is, somehow.” Kex’s tail twitched—a nervous tell he hated. He reached past her, wiped a clear streak in the condensation. Behind the glass, darkness. Nothing moved. But the air inside the crate tasted wrong, like the inside of a mouth that hadn’t brushed its teeth in twenty years. That afternoon Doc tried to up the sedation again. The line was already maxed; the pump just clicked uselessly. He stood there a long time with the empty syringe in his hand, then quietly tucked it back into his coat like a guilty secret. Elias avoided the cargo bay. Avoided all of them. Spent the day in the starboard nacelle, supposedly checking fuel lines, actually hiding. The knocking didn’t come back. The quiet was worse. At 1800 the ghost returned. This time on every speaker, every headset, every loose panel that could vibrate. “Daddy, I waited like you said. I was good.” Luz was in the mess, heating something that claimed to be chili. The voice came from the microwave. She dropped the bowl. Red sludge splattered across the deck like gunshot. Kex appeared in the doorway so fast his duster hadn’t caught up. Revolver already out. Doc slid down the ladder, barefoot, eyes rolling like marbles. “Captain—” Elias stormed in from the bridge, face gray, bad eye flickering with static. He grabbed the nearest intercom and screamed into it. “Shut up! Goddamn you, shut up!” The child’s voice didn’t flinch. “They told me you were busy saving people. Were you busy, Daddy?” Silence swallowed the ship again. Tuesday the cat bolted from under the table, fur puffed, and vanished into the vents with a shriek that sounded almost human. Luz looked at Elias. Really looked. Saw something she’d never seen before: terror wearing his skin like a badly fitted coat. She took one step toward him. “Elias. Whose voice is that?” He couldn’t answer. Mouth worked, nothing came out. Kex holstered his gun slow, deliberate. “Captain,” he said, voice low, “some debts find their own way home.” Elias turned and walked out. Didn’t run. Just walked, like a man heading to the gallows who’s tired of postponing it. Luz started to follow, but Kex caught her arm. Scales cold against her skin. “Let him walk,” the lizard said. “Some doors you open alone.” Behind them, unnoticed, the cargo bay camera feed flickered. One frame showed the crate’s lid bowed outward a millimeter, metal creaking under pressure that wasn’t there yesterday. Then the image went black. In the dark below, something exhaled. Slow. Satisfied. The Iron Mustang kept flying, but every light on board felt suddenly dimmer, like the ship itself had started holding its breath.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 3)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter 3 of this space western demonstrates considerable craft and largely delivers on its ambitious plan. Let me assess it carefully across multiple dimensions. STRENGTHS: The chapter successfully executes its core mandate: the ghost signal on the Red Lick frequency, the fingerprints on the viewport, and the escalating dread. The pacing is controlled—the 0400 opening with Elias alone, the gradual crew awakening, the second broadcast hitting all speakers simultaneously. This escalation feels earned rather than mechanical. The fingerprints detail is genuinely unsettling and creative: "Some prints small. Some bigger. Same fingers, years apart." This is the chapter's strongest image—economical, horrifying, and doing real narrative work by implying Mara's growth inside the crate without stating it. It's the kind of detail that rewards rereading. Character consistency is solid. Elias's inability to speak when Luz asks whose voice it is lands well—the silence doing more than any confession could. Kex's "Some debts find their own way home" is appropriately laconic and in-character. Luz's observation that she sees "terror wearing his skin like a badly fitted coat" works as internal perception rather than purple prose. The chapter plan called for: ghost signal on old Confederacy channel, Elias smashing the console, Luz finding fingerprints, cat refusing the cargo bay. The chapter delivers all of these, though Elias screams into the intercom rather than smashing the console with his revolver—a minor deviation that actually works better dramatically (the physical violence of smashing would have been more cathartic; the scream is rawer and more pathetic, which fits the character better). WEAKNESSES: Some metaphors strain. "Black sludge spreading like dried blood" for spilled coffee is overwrought—dried blood doesn't spread, and the comparison is reaching. "Eyes rolling like marbles" for Doc is a cliché. "Terror wearing his skin like a badly fitted coat" is the kind of metaphor the plan warned against—it's not quite landing because terror doesn't wear skin, the image is confused. The ending paragraphs tip into purple prose: "the ship itself had started holding its breath" is a fine enough image but the chapter leans on it after already using the ship's sentience earlier. The final "something exhaled. Slow. Satisfied." is effective but the camera flickering to black is slightly cinematic in a way that feels borrowed from horror film grammar rather than earned through prose. The child's voice dialogue is appropriately chilling, but "They told me you were busy saving people. Were you busy, Daddy?" is perhaps too on-the-nose—it's doing the thematic work too explicitly, telling us exactly what the story is about rather than letting it breathe. Doc's appearance ("eyes rolling like marbles") and Kex appearing "so fast his duster hadn't caught up" are both slightly amateurish action-movie beats that undercut the chapter's otherwise restrained tone. The chapter is coherent and follows its plan faithfully. The darkness is well-earned given the setup. The dialogue is mostly functional and in-character, with the child's voice lines doing the heavy lifting effectively. Overall this is a strong chapter with some metaphorical overreach and a few amateurish action beats, but the core emotional machinery works. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 14 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Compelling Plot: 15 Coherent: 17 Weak Dialogue: 5 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 5 Purple Prose: 7 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 7 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 15 Characters Consistent with Profile: 16 Followed Chapter Plan: 16 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 4

Ok now chapter 4. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
Luz waited until the ship settled into the graveyard shift. Red lights, engine drone low, everyone pretending to sleep. She took the service ladder down to the cargo bay barefoot so her boots wouldn’t wake the metal. Wrench in one fist, flashlight in her teeth, cigarette trembling between her lips though she hadn’t lit it. Curiosity was her oldest addiction and tonight it itched worse than withdrawal. The bay was colder than it had any right to be. Breath fogged. Tuesday the cat sat on a crate opposite the black one, tail lashing, ears flat, refusing to come closer than five meters. Smart cat. Luz killed the flashlight. Let her eyes adjust to the red glow. The crate looked bigger in the half-dark, swollen almost, seams shining with fresh condensation. Tiny handprints climbed the viewport like a ladder someone had been using for years. She circled it once. Twice. Told herself she was just checking the straps. Lies sounded thin even in her own head. The lid had a manual release under the mag-locks (old Confederacy override, the kind smugglers never bothered to remove). Four bolts, a quarter-turn each. She’d opened worse safes drunk. Her hands didn’t shake. They never did when she was stealing something. First bolt clicked free. Second. Third. On the fourth she hesitated. Heard Elias coughing in his sleep two decks up. Heard Doc snoring off a morphine nod. Heard Kex breathing slow and steady like a sleeping dragon. She thought about her sister back on Dusthaven, indenture papers that needed one more big score. She thought about the little girl on the speakers. She turned the last bolt. The lid unsealed with a wet sigh, decades of pressure kissing open air. Frost rolled out like smoke from a grave. Luz shined the flashlight inside. A woman lay curled on her side, naked, skin the color of old paper. Tubes ran into both arms, crusted with yellow thaw. IV bags hung flat and empty. Her ribs showed every breath. Hair shaved to stubble, scalp mapped with faded surgical scars. Eyes open, filmed white, staring straight at Luz. The woman’s lips moved. No sound came out at first—just chapped skin cracking, a bead of blood. Luz leaned closer, stupidly. “Thirsty,” the woman rasped. Voice like sandpaper on bone. Luz’s canteen was half-full of water and backwash. She unscrewed the cap with her teeth, tilted it to the woman’s mouth. The woman drank slow, greedy, water spilling down her chin onto the coffin floor. Some of it froze instantly. When the canteen was empty she licked her cracked lips and smiled. The expression didn’t fit her face. “You’ve got Mom’s eyes, Auntie Luz.” Luz dropped the canteen. Metal rang loud enough to wake the dead. She knew that smile. Knew it from a holo Elias kept in his locker—the one he thought nobody had ever seen. A little girl on a porch swing, gap-toothed, holding a rag doll. Mara Crowe. Age six the day Red Lick burned. Luz backed up until her shoulders hit a bulkhead. The wrench clattered to the deck. Mara sat up slow, IV lines tugging at her arms like reins. She pulled one free with her teeth. Blood pattered onto the frost, hissing where it landed. “Don’t be scared,” Mara said. Voice still raw, but steadier now. “I’ve been watching you all for years. Through the vents. Through the dark. You snore when you’re on your period, did you know that?” Luz couldn’t breathe. Mara swung her legs over the edge of the coffin. Bare feet touched the deck and left perfect bloody prints. She stood—wobbled, caught herself on the crate’s rim. “You kept me pretty,” she told the empty IV bags. “Doc changed the bags every six months. Said I was his best patient. Never complained.” Luz found her voice. “You were supposed to be… asleep.” “Sedatives wore off years ago.” Mara took a step. Knees buckled; she caught herself on Luz’s shoulder, fingers cold as rebar. “They wanted to see how long a little girl could stay awake in a box. Turns out a long fucking time.” Luz smelled antiseptic and old piss and something sweeter underneath—milk gone bad. Mara leaned in until their foreheads almost touched. “Tell Daddy his princess is home.” Then she let go and walked—shuffling, barefoot, bleeding—toward the ladder. Each footprint steamed. Luz slid down the wall until her ass hit the deck. The cigarette finally fell from her lips and went out on a puddle of thawed cryo-slush. Somewhere above, a bunk creaked. Elias turning over in troubled sleep. Mara paused at the ladder, looked back once. “You coming, Auntie? Or do I introduce myself?” Luz couldn’t move. Couldn’t scream. Could only watch Mara climb, slow and deliberate, leaving red handprints on every rung. Tuesday the cat hissed once, low and final, then bolted into the ducts and didn’t come back. The cargo bay lights flickered, dimmed, steadied. Luz sat in the dark a long time, staring at the open coffin and the puddle of water turning pink. She pissed herself somewhere in there. Didn’t notice until the warmth went cold.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 4)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter 4 is a tightly controlled piece of horror-inflected western fiction that largely delivers on its plan's promises. Let me examine it carefully across multiple dimensions. CRAFT AND EXECUTION The chapter opens with strong sensory grounding: "barefoot so her boots wouldn't wake the metal" is an excellent, specific detail that establishes character and setting simultaneously. The cold cargo bay, the fogged breath, Tuesday's refusal to approach—these are economical scene-setters that don't overstay their welcome. The prose follows the stated stylistic rules well. Sentences are short and functional. The chapter smells like something real (antiseptic, old piss, "milk gone bad"). The writing avoids purple prose almost entirely.
