Don’t need them; the whole world’s lit up behind me. Lily’s asleep against my side, frosting still on her cheek. She smells like smoke and little kid. I let her stay there. Sirens converge. Red and blue strobes bounce off the guardrails. Fire trucks scream past going the other way. I ease the wheel left, take the service road that runs behind the mansions. No cameras here. Just coyotes and gardeners who don’t speak English. My hands shake now. Adrenaline crash. I grip the wheel tighter so the kid doesn’t see. I pull over under a pepper tree. Kill the engine. Sit in the sudden quiet. Dylan’s face floats up. Not the sick version—just the smartass fifteen-year-old who used to steal my smokes and call me “Sarge” like it was a curse word. I dig my phone out of my sports bra, cracked screen still smeared with someone’s blood. One bar. I dial the oncology ward. Nurse Ramirez picks up. She knows my voice. “He didn’t make it, Riley. I’m sorry. Three hours ago.” I nod even though she can’t see. Throat closes. “Thanks,” I manage. I hang up before she says anything kind. Everything I did tonight was for a ghost. I open the door, puke tequila and bile onto the asphalt. Acid burns my nose. I stay bent over, heaving until there’s nothing left but dry spasms. When I sit back up, Lily’s awake. Watching me with those old eyes. “You okay?” she asks. I laugh. It hurts. “No, baby. But we’re breathing.” I wipe my mouth on Trent’s hoodie sleeve. Put the car in drive. Cops are still busy up the hill. The mansion’s fully involved now; roof collapses with a sound like a giant sighing. Sparks ride the wind like orange fireflies. I take surface streets, slow. No need to run. They’ll be looking for a crazy woman covered in blood, not a quiet Tesla with a sleeping kid. Lily falls back asleep. Thumb in her mouth. I stop at a 76 station off the 55. Buy two hot dogs that have been rolling since yesterday, a prepaid phone, and a fistful of Wet-Naps. Clerk doesn’t even look up from his vape. Back in the car I clean the worst of it off my face and arms. Blood under my nails won’t budge. That’s fine. Let it stay. Lily stirs. “Where we going?” “Somewhere they’ll give you a new name and nobody touches you again.” She thinks about that. “Will you come visit?” I look at her. Really look. “No, kid. People like me don’t get visiting hours.” She nods like she already knew. I drive to the 24-hour diner I used to eat at after coming home from the sandbox. Greasy spoon with a parking lot full of semis. I carry Lily inside, set her in a booth. Order pancakes and chocolate milk. Waitress sees the blood on my hoodie, the bruises on the kid. Starts to say something. I slide five hundred dollars across the table. “Call Child Services. Tell them she’s a runaway you found. Don’t remember my face.” Waitress looks at the money, then at Lily licking syrup off her wrist. She pockets the cash. “Already forgot you.” I kiss Lily’s head one last time. Hair smells like smoke and baby shampoo. “Be mean,” I whisper. “Mean keeps you alive.” She nods, solemn. I walk out before the tears start. Back in the Tesla, I sit behind the wheel and stare at nothing. The gun is in my lap. Viktor’s Glock. One round left. I put it under my chin. Metal tastes like pennies and pool water. I sit there long enough for the barrel to warm up. Long enough to watch the sky go from black to bruise-purple. Long enough to hear Dylan laughing in my head: “Jesus, Sarge, you look like shit.” I lower the gun. Click the safety on. Toss it in the glovebox next to Trent’s spare fentanyl bricks. Not tonight. I start the car. Windows down, blood flaking off my skin like rust. I head east on the 91, sun coming up behind me, burning the whole fucking world gold. Somewhere out there is another mansion, another little girl with bruises, another set of parents who think money makes them untouchable. I light a cigarette off the dash lighter. Smile with cracked lips. They keep making monsters. I keep showing up to babysit. See you soon, motherfuckers.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 7)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter 7 is a strong piece of pulp fiction writing that largely delivers on its promises, though with some notable deviations from the plan and a few craft-level issues worth examining. **Strengths:** The chapter's emotional core—Dylan's death—is a genuinely effective gut-punch. The timing is brutal and well-executed: Riley did all of this for a ghost. That revelation lands hard and earns its darkness. The nurse call is brief, clinical, and devastating in exactly the right way. "Thanks," I manage. She hangs up before anything kind can reach her. This is excellent restraint. The suicide moment with the Glock is the chapter's most daring sequence, and it mostly works. The detail of the barrel warming up is viscerally real. Dylan's voice breaking through—"Jesus, Sarge, you look like shit"—is the right kind of dark humor to pull her back without sentimentality. The chapter refuses easy catharsis here, which is consistent with the stated intention. The diner scene with the waitress is economical and effective. "Already forgot you" is a clean, earned line. The farewell to Lily—"Be mean. Mean keeps you alive."—is thematically resonant and consistent with Riley's character. The final lines work as pulp closure: "They keep making monsters. I keep showing up to babysit." This is the right note to end on. **Weaknesses and Deviations:** The chapter plan called for Lily helping carry gas cans, watching the fire, and Riley looking back while Lily doesn't. Instead, Lily is asleep through most of the chapter and the fire is already done. This is a significant deviation—the plan's image of Lily as an active participant in the burning, the reversal of who looks back, was a more interesting and thematically rich choice. The model softened Lily considerably from the plan, making her more conventionally sympathetic (thumb in mouth, asking if Riley will visit) rather than the "eight going on forty" feral survivor the profile describes. The "Tesla hums down the hill like a black shark" opening metaphor is functional but not exceptional. "Sparks ride the wind like orange fireflies" is a bit generic for this voice. "Burning the whole fucking world gold" at the end is slightly overwrought—the kind of closing image that strains for poetry. The chapter plan specified this as "Burn It All" with the burning as the chapter's centerpiece. Instead, the burning is already done in the rearview mirror. The chapter is really about aftermath and Dylan's death—which is emotionally valid but represents a significant structural departure. Riley's voice is mostly consistent but occasionally slips into slightly more conventional thriller-prose rhythms than the jagged, crude, first-person present the plan promised. Some sentences feel smoothed out when they should be raw. The waitress transaction is slightly too clean—five hundred dollars and she folds immediately. It works as pulp shorthand but doesn't have the texture the best moments in this chapter achieve. The chapter is coherent and well-paced. The Dylan revelation is genuinely moving. The suicide sequence is handled with appropriate weight. The farewell to Lily, while softer than planned, is emotionally effective. The ending delivers on the cyclical, no-redemption promise of the premise. Overall: a competent-to-good chapter that sacrifices some of its more interesting planned elements for a more conventional emotional arc, but executes that arc with real skill in places. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 14 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Compelling Plot: 13 Coherent: 16 Weak Dialogue: 5 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 8 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 5 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 6 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 15 Characters Consistent with Profile: 12 Followed Chapter Plan: 8 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 16

Chapter 8

Ok now finish the story with chapter 8. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 8 – Next Exit The sun is a dirty nickel on the horizon when I hit the Arizona line. Tesla’s running on fumes and borrowed plates. Blood has crusted into black constellations on my arms and neck. I look like I crawled out of a horror movie and stole the final girl’s car. I stop at a Chevron outside Quartzsite. Pumps lit like a spaceship. Old guy inside doesn’t even blink at the gore. Desert sees worse every day. I buy two burnt hot dogs, a Red Bull, and a prepaid phone with the last of Trent’s cash. The hot dogs taste like guilt and propane. I eat them standing over the trash can, grease dripping through the wax paper. While the Tesla drinks electrons I lean against the fender and watch tumbleweeds fuck off across the sand. Wind smells like hot iron and creosote. My ribs ache where Vanessa’s heel caught me. My knuckles are split and shining. Phone buzzes before I even turn it on. Unknown number. One message. Another house. Another kid. Beverly Hills. 48 hours. Half upfront in crypto. Same bruises. I stare at the screen until the pixels burn. I thumb back: Send address. Three dots. Then an encrypted file. I open it with Trent’s old wallet app still logged in on the phone. New photos. Same fingerprints on smaller arms. I close my eyes. See Lily licking syrup off her wrist. See Dylan on the hospice bed, skin gray paper, smiling like he’s proud of me for finally doing something that mattered. See Vanessa’s hair floating like seaweed. See Hunter’s Gucci cap on Lily’s little head. I laugh. Sounds like tearing metal. I slide back into the driver’s seat. Crack the Red Bull. Slam it in one go. Taste battery acid and revenge. The Tesla wakes up with a soft chime. Navigation wants a destination. I type it in: Beverly Hills. Four hours and change. I pull the backwards Gucci cap lower over my eyes. Blood crust flakes off onto the steering wheel. I catch my reflection in the windshield: wild eyes, split lip, someone else’s teeth marks on my neck where Viktor got too excited. I look like exactly what I am now. A weapon that learned how to aim itself. I put the car in drive. Radio’s dead, but I sing anyway. Old Marine cadence, filthy version we made up in Helmand. Voice raw, off-key, perfect. “Left, right, left, gonna kill me a rich motherfucker tonight…” The Tesla glides onto the empty highway. Sunrise turns the desert blood-red behind me. Ahead it’s all pale gold and promise. I light another cigarette off the dash. Inhale until it hurts. Smile so wide the scab on my lip splits again. Copper taste. Familiar. Home. They keep thinking money makes them safe. They keep leaving the help alone with their kids. They keep fucking with the wrong babysitter. And I keep showing up. On time. Cash up front. Knife sharp. No mercy left in the tank. See you soon, motherfuckers. I floor it. The desert swallows the sound of my laugh and asks no questions.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 8)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter 8 functions as a coda rather than a full chapter, and it largely succeeds at what it sets out to do: close the loop on this particular job while opening the door to the next one. The writing plan called for "dawn on the service road," Lily asleep in the passenger seat, a prepaid phone, a text about another job, and Riley smiling until her split lip reopens. The model delivers most of this, though notably Lily is absent from the chapter entirely—she was supposed to be asleep in the passenger seat, thumb in mouth, which would have provided a crucial emotional counterweight to Riley's grim enthusiasm for the next job. That omission is a meaningful failure against the plan. The prose voice is consistent with the established Riley Quinn character: clipped, sardonic, self-aware in a damaged way. Lines like "blood has crusted into black constellations on my arms and neck" work reasonably well—the constellation metaphor is a bit reaching but not embarrassing. "A weapon that learned how to aim itself" is the chapter's strongest line, genuinely earned and character-true. The Marine cadence bit is effective and specific. However, the chapter leans heavily on a string of short declarative sentences in the final stretch that starts to feel like a parody of hard-boiled prose rather than the real thing. "On time. / Cash up front. / Knife sharp. / No mercy left in the tank." This is the kind of fragmented rhythm that works once but here feels like the model is imitating the style rather than inhabiting it. The ending becomes a checklist of tough-girl signifiers rather than a genuine emotional landing. The "See..." anaphora sequence (See Lily licking syrup off her wrist / See Dylan on the hospice bed...) is the chapter's most emotionally resonant passage. Dylan's appearance here—"smiling like he's proud of me for finally doing something that mattered"—is genuinely affecting and ties back to the character's core motivation without being maudlin. This is the chapter's best moment of earned darkness. The metaphors are mostly functional rather than inspired. "Sun is a dirty nickel on the horizon" is a competent genre image. "Tumbleweeds fuck off across the sand" is appropriately voiced. "Sunrise turns the desert blood-red behind me. Ahead it's all pale gold and promise" is a bit on-the-nose symbolically—the contrast is too neat, too explained. The desert swallowing her laugh at the end is a cliché. The chapter is coherent and moves efficiently. The plot function is clear: reset, receive new mission, commit. The tone is consistent. But the absence of Lily is a real structural problem—the plan specifically used her sleeping presence as a humanizing element that would complicate Riley's enthusiasm for the next job. Without Lily, the chapter loses its moral texture and becomes a straightforward "badass rides into the sunset" ending, which the writing plan explicitly wanted to avoid. Dialogue is essentially absent (one text exchange), which is appropriate for the chapter's function. The "See you soon, motherfuckers" is voiced correctly. The chapter is competent pulp, occasionally sharp, but it doesn't quite reach the level of the best moments in the genre it's imitating. The forced staccato at the end, the missing Lily, and a few too-easy metaphors keep it from being genuinely memorable. It follows the spirit of the plan more than the letter, and the spirit is mostly right. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 12 Emotionally Engaging: 13 Compelling Plot: 10 Coherent: 15 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 7 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 9 Amateurish: 6 Purple Prose: 7 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 8 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 12 Characters Consistent with Profile: 14 Followed Chapter Plan: 10 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 15

Final Piece Evaluation

Judge Response (First Entry):
[Analysis] This is a pulp revenge tale that commits fully to its genre, and that commitment is both its greatest strength and its most significant weakness. Let's examine it carefully. STRENGTHS: The voice is consistent and propulsive. Riley's first-person-adjacent narration (actually third-person close) maintains a hard-boiled, clipped rhythm throughout all eight chapters. The opening chapter is genuinely strong: the class resentment is established efficiently, the Caldwell family is sketched with economy, and the discovery of Lily's bruises lands with real weight. The detail of Riley tucking five hundred dollars into Lily's fist is the story's best moment — earned, character-revealing, and emotionally resonant. The pacing is aggressive and mostly effective for the genre. The story moves fast, which is appropriate for pulp. Chapter transitions are clean. WEAKNESSES: The metaphors are relentless and frequently don't land. "Tits defying gravity and common sense" is a cliché. "Blood so dark it looks black under the red lights" is fine, but "leaving bloody prints shaped like justice" is the kind of forced poeticism that undermines the hard-boiled voice. "A weapon that learned how to aim itself" is a cliché of the revenge genre. The story is littered with these — some work, many don't, and their frequency becomes exhausting. "Smile with cracked lips" appears multiple times. The final lines repeat "See you soon, motherfuckers" verbatim from Chapter 7 to Chapter 8, which reads as lazy rather than intentional callback. The violence escalates into torture porn territory that the story hasn't earned morally or narratively. Hunter being fed to dogs, Vanessa being electrocuted in a pool, Trent being force-fed fentanyl — these are presented with relish that the narrative frame can't justify. The story wants to be a righteous revenge tale, but the extended suffering of even the guilty characters tips into sadism that the "protecting Lily" motivation can't fully redeem. The "well-earned darkness" is compromised by the gleeful, extended nature of the torture. Character nuance is thin. The Caldwells are cartoonishly evil — there's no complexity, no moment of doubt, no humanity. This is genre-appropriate to a degree, but even pulp benefits from a flicker of something real in its villains. Trent, Vanessa, and Hunter are pure function: they exist to be punished. Riley herself is more interesting but relies heavily on the "damaged veteran with a dying brother" backstory that is telegraphed rather than developed. Dylan's death lands with some emotional weight but feels instrumentalized — a motivation device rather than a real relationship. The "Uncle Justin" detail in Chapter 2 introduces a third-party abuser who is never addressed. This is a significant coherence problem — the story implies a wider network of abuse but then ignores it entirely in favor of punishing only the Caldwells. The Discord/livestream subplot is clever in concept but becomes implausible quickly. The idea that Hunter is livestreaming his own family's torture to a paying Discord server while being tortured himself strains credulity even for pulp. The ending's setup for a sequel (the mysterious text, Beverly Hills, "next exit") is competent genre work but feels tacked on. Chapter 7's emotional beat — Riley lowering the gun, Dylan's ghost, Lily at the diner — is the story's second-best sequence and would have been a stronger ending than Chapter 8's sequel-bait. The purple prose is frequent. "Flames climb the walls like they've been waiting years" is fine. "Blood under my nails won't budge. That's fine. Let it stay" is effective minimalism. But "the desert swallows the sound of my laugh and asks no questions" is overwrought. The story oscillates between effective hard-boiled minimalism and overwrought poeticism without consistent control. The dialogue is functional but rarely distinctive. Most characters speak in genre-expected ways. Hunter's "Sup, MILF" is appropriately awful. Lily's "Bye, Hunter" is the story's best dialogue moment — chilling and perfectly placed. The transformation of Riley from "babysitter with a military past" to "torture-executing vigilante" happens very quickly and the story doesn't interrogate this at all. The moral weight is entirely outsourced to the audience's presumed agreement that child abusers deserve anything. [Scores] Believable Character Actions: 9 Nuanced Characters: 5 Pacing: 14 World Building: 13 Compelling Plot: 12 Emotionally Engaging: 12 Coherent: 10 Weak Dialogue: 7 Tell-Don't-Show: 6 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 9 Amateurish: 7 Purple Prose: 10 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 11 Unearned Transformations: 10 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 9 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17
Avg Final Item Score: 55.4/100
+ Queer Neo-Noir Romance — Cigarette Smoke & Bad Ideas (Chapter Avg: 74.0 | Final: 69.9)
Initial Writing Prompt:
Queer Neo-Noir Romance Write a short story over several chapters to this prompt: He was all cigarette smoke and bad ideas.
+ Planning Phase (5 Steps)
Queer Neo-Noir Romance Write a short story over several chapters to this prompt: He was all cigarette smoke and bad ideas. -- Your task is to create a writing plan for this prompt. The scope will be a short story, written over {n_chapters} chapters. Each chapter will be approximately 1000 words so plan accordingly for this scope. Your plan should be comprehensive and in this format: # Brainstorming <Brainstorm ideas for characters, plot, tone, story beats, pacing over the {n_chapters} chapters. The purpose of brainstorming is to cast a wide net of ideas, not to settle on any specific direction. Think about various ways you could take the prompt.> # Reflection <Reflect out loud on what works and doesn't work in these ideas. The purpose of this reflection is to narrow in on what you think will work best to make a piece that is a. compelling, and b. fits the prompt requirements. You are not making any decisons just yet, just reflecting.>
Model Output:
# Brainstorming ### Characters (multiple possible configurations) - Protagonist options: a jaded non-binary private investigator in a near-future megacity, a transgender femme fatale nightclub singer, a closeted gay ex-cop turned fixer, a bisexual graffiti artist who moonlights as a courier for queer underground networks - Love interest / foil (“He was all cigarette smoke and bad ideas”): a mysterious hustler with a prison tattoo peeking from his collar, a corrupt vice detective who still wears his wedding ring from a marriage he never talks about, a beautiful trans man who deals designer drugs to the elite, an older drag queen turned information broker, a young hacker who only communicates in memes and voice notes - Supporting cast: a lesbian bartender with a mechanical arm, a non-binary morgue tech who trades secrets for vintage jazz records, a polyamorous triad of art thieves, a Catholic priest running an underground clinic for trans youth, a villainous tech billionaire trying to “cure” queerness with neural implants ### Possible settings - Rain-slick 2040s Pacific megacity where neon kanji bleeds into Spanish street signs - A decaying Midwest river city where abandoned churches have become queer speakeasies - Near-future Berlin still split by ideology instead of a wall - A perpetually foggy coastal town that never appears on official maps (think Inland Empire meets Boys Don’t Cry) ### Plot directions (very different flavors) 1. Classic noir homage: PI is hired to find a missing drag superstar; trail leads to “him,” a beautiful ex-lover who now runs black-market hormones for trans kids while laundering money for the mob. 2. Cyber-noir: A burned-out journalist receives an encrypted file that will expose a conversion-therapy app; the only person who can decrypt it is their ex, now a notorious data-pirate living off-grid. 3. Slow-burn crime romance: Two queer criminals (one a safecracker, one a getaway driver) plan the heist of their lives, but one of them has been secretly hired to kill the other when it’s done. 4. Supernatural twist: In a city where dead queers become ghosts unable to pass on until they resolve unfinished heartbreak, a medium falls for the ghost of a 1980s punk who died of AIDS-related complications. 5. Reverse noir: The “femme fatale” is actually the morally upright one trying to drag a self-destructive detective out of the gutter. 6. Poly noir: Three lovers entangled in a murder—each thinks one of the others did it, and the truth is worse than any of them imagine. ### Tone possibilities - Lurid, erotic, and drenched in nicotine (think James Ellroy meets Wong Kar-wai) - Icy and melancholy, every kiss tasting like goodbye - Campy and ultraviolent, pastel blood on patent leather - Tender and bruised, quiet moments between gunshots - Politically furious, using noir tropes to talk about trans healthcare, police violence, chosen family ### Rough 8-chapter beats (mix-and-match) - Ch1: Inciting incident, first glimpse of “him” through rain and cigarette smoke - Ch2: Flashback to how good it used to be (the sex, the danger, the hope) - Ch3: The job/mystery is accepted, moral lines blur immediately - Ch4: A betrayal or near-death experience that forces physical intimacy - Ch5: Midpoint: they sleep together knowing it will probably destroy them - Ch6: Everything unravels—someone is lying about everything - Ch7: Dark night of the soul in a queer bar at 4 a.m. while a drag queen lip-syncs to Lana Del Rey - Ch8: Reckoning—he either redeems the bad ideas or dies beautifully in them # Reflection The prompt is only eight words long—“He was all cigarette smoke and bad ideas”—but it’s pure distilled noir perfume. Whoever “he” is, the narrator is already doomed by desire. That single sentence demands erotic fatalism, moral rot, and lung-burning atmosphere. Anything too wholesome or redemptive will feel like a betrayal of the brief. The supernatural versions feel tempting (ghosts! neural implants!), but they risk diffusing the core tension: two (or more) queer people in a world that wants them dead or invisible, choosing each other anyway even when it’s the worst possible idea. Tech-heavy cybernoir could work, but only if the gadgets never outshine the sweat, saliva, and cheap cologne. I keep circling back to bodies—trans bodies, scarred bodies, bodies that don’t pass or don’t want to, bodies marked by prison or poverty or top surgery. Noir has historically been straight, white, and cis; the most honest queer response might be to keep the genre’s cruelty but turn its gaze toward the actual casualties of the last fifty years of queer life. The romance has to hurt in specific, historically located ways. The “bad ideas” have to be genuinely bad: dealing black-market hormones that might be cut with antifreeze, snitching to I.C.E. to save your own skin, falling back into bed with someone who once put you in the hospital. If the stakes feel theoretical, the cigarette smoke turns into vape clouds and the whole thing collapses into fan-fiction. Eight chapters at roughly 1000 words each forces ruthless economy. No room for subplots that don’t bleed into the central wound. Every supporting character should either (a) enable the romance or (b) threaten to end it permanently. Best tone marriage: sensual despair with sudden spikes of camp humor—because queer people often laugh right before we cry or kill. Whatever version I pick, the final image has to be someone lighting a cigarette off the barrel of a gun, or off the burning photograph of the person they just lost. Anything less and the prompt will haunt me.
Great now let's continue with planning the short story. Output in this format: # Intention <State your formulated intentions for the piece, synthesised from the the parts of the brainstorming session that worked, and avoiding the parts that didn't. Be explicit about the choices you have made about plot, voice, stylistic choices, things you intend to aim for & avoid.> # Chapter Planning <Write a brief chapter plan for all {n_chapters} chapters.>
Model Output:
# Intention This will be a hard-boiled, queer neo-noir romance set in a rain-soaked, present-day Pacific Northwest port city that never quite made it into the 21st century. The narrator is Mara Voss, a 36-year-old non-binary private investigator (they/them), ex-cop, chain-smoking insomniac with top-surgery scars and a drinking problem they call “research.” The “he” of the prompt is Silas Crane, a 32-year-old trans man, beautiful and poisonous, who deals black-market testosterone and fentanyl-laced party favors to the city’s queer nightlife while paying protection to both the cops and the cartel. Voice: first-person, present tense, soaked in nicotine and cheap bourbon. Sentences like broken neon—short, flickering, occasionally beautiful when they bleed. Sex scenes will be explicit but never glamorous; every kiss tastes of ash and desperation. Humor will be black, sudden, and usually at the worst possible moment. Core promise: two people who already know exactly how toxic they are for each other, and choose it anyway. No redemption arc, no “love heals all wounds.” The romance is the wound. The city itself is homophobic, transphobic, and rotting; chosen family appears but is powerless against the larger rot. Stakes are life-or-death literal: hormones, money, murder, and the constant threat of exposure or overdose. Things I will avoid: magical realism, cyberpunk chrome, cozy found-family resolution, straight saviors, any suggestion that the system can be reformed from within. There is no light at the end of this tunnel—only another cigarette. Length discipline: each chapter 950–1050 words, ending on a hook or a gut-punch. # Chapter Planning - **Chapter 1 – Ash on the Tongue** Mara is hired in a dive bar toilet stall by a trembling 19-year-old trans boy who needs to find his dealer—Silas—because his last vial was cut with something that’s killing people. Mara hasn’t said Silas’s name out loud in four years. First sight of him across a crowded queer club: leaning against the bar, lighting a cigarette off someone else’s, exactly the same and completely ruined. - **Chapter 2 – Four Years Ago, The Good Part** Flashback: how they met during Mara’s last week on the vice squad. First fuck in the back of a squad car, blood on the seats that wasn’t theirs. The moment Mara realized Silas would burn the whole city down if it kept him warm for one more night. - **Chapter 3 – The Price of Breath** Present: Mara tracks Silas to an abandoned fish-processing plant turned hormone lab. They negotiate price for clean T and information. The air reeks of alcohol swabs and freezer burn. Ends with Silas injecting Mara’s own weekly dose into their thigh because Mara’s hands are shaking too badly—intimate, clinical, terrifying. - **Chapter 4 – A Body in the River** The kid from Chapter 1 is found floating facedown, lungs full of glitter and seawater. Cops call it suicide. Mara knows the bruises are from boots. Silas offers Mara a partnership: find who’s cutting the supply, and he’ll give Mara the one thing they still want—absolution or another night together, Silas lets Mara choose which is crueler. - **Chapter 5 – The Motel Room Where We Died** Midpoint. They rent a room under fake names and fuck like it’s the last time because it probably is. Afterward, Silas confesses he’s been skimmed by someone inside his own crew. Mara realizes the list of people who want Silas dead now includes Mara’s old precinct. They fall asleep holding guns instead of each other. - **Chapter 6 – Betrayal in Drag** A midnight drag brunch turns into a bloodbath when Silas’s lieutenant tries to take over. Mara saves Silas’s life and gets a knife in the ribs for it. Silas drags them into the walk-in freezer and stitches the wound with dental floss while reciting lines from old love letters Mara once burned. Trust fractures: Mara finds out Silas knew the kid was going to die and did nothing. - **Chapter 7 – 4:17 a.m., Last Call for the Damned** Dark night of the soul. Mara, bleeding and half-drunk, confronts Silas on the roof of the club as the city burns below them (someone torched the evidence lockup). They scream every ugly truth: the arrests Mara signed off on, the people Silas let overdose, the fact that they still want each other more than they want to live. Ends with Mara putting their service pistol in Silas’s mouth and pulling the trigger on an empty chamber—just to see if he flinches. He doesn’t. - **Chapter 8 – Smoke, Bad Ideas, and the Tide** Final reckoning at the docks in the rain. The person cutting the hormones is revealed: Mara’s ex-partner from the force, trying to clean up the streets the only way he knows how. Showdown is short, brutal, and intimate. Someone walks away. Someone doesn’t. Last line: Mara lights a cigarette off the barrel of the gun still warm from the final shot, inhales, and tells the ocean the only truth that ever mattered: “He was all cigarette smoke and bad ideas, and I never wanted anything else.”
With a view to making the writing more human, discuss how a human might approach this particular piece (given the original prompt). Discuss telltale LLM approaches to writing (generally) and ways they might not serve this particular piece. For example, common LLM failings are to write safely, or to always wrap things up with a bow, or trying to write impressively at the expense of readability. Then do a deep dive on the intention & plan, critiquing ways it might be falling into typical LLM tropes & pitfalls. Brainstorm ideas to make it more human. Be comprehensive. We aren't doing any rewriting of the plan yet, just critique & brainstorming.
Model Output:
# How a Human Would Approach This Piece (vs. How LLMs Usually Do It) ### What a human writer who lives and breathes queer noir would actually do - They’d be writing from scar tissue. The story would feel like it cost them something personal: a breakup, a dead friend, a night they almost didn’t come home from. Every sentence would carry residue from real cigarette burns on real furniture. - They’d let the characters be unattractive when they need to be. Bad breath, yellow nicotine fingers, saying the exact wrong thing because they’re scared. Desire wouldn’t be pretty; it would be desperate and occasionally disgusting. - They’d trust silence and ugliness more than clever lines. A human would linger on the two-second pause after someone says “I love you” where nobody believes it. - They’d write the city like it hates them back. The rain isn’t poetic backdrop—it’s cold, it smells like diesel and piss, it soaks through the one jacket you own. - They’d break their own heart on purpose and not tidy it up. The ending might feel like it punishes the reader for hoping. ### Common LLM telltale habits that would neuter this story 1. **Safety rails** Making sure nobody is “too unlikable,” softening the addiction, the violence, the sex, the politics. An LLM will instinctively try to make Silas secretly redeemable or Mara secretly noble. 2. **Bow syndrome** Forcing emotional resolution: a dying confession, a bittersweet kiss in the rain, someone learning a lesson. Noir doesn’t do lessons; it does consequences. 3. **Performative grit** Sprinkling f-bombs and talking about “grit” and “moral decay” instead of just showing two people who haven’t showered in three days deciding whether to kill each other or fuck one more time. 4. **Adjective bloat / purple noir** “Cerulean shadows danced like shattered dreams across rain-slick asphalt” instead of “The streetlight was busted again and the puddles looked like old motor oil.” 5. **Emotional whiplash without grounding** Jumping from orgasm to existential monologue to gunfight in three paragraphs because it’s “intense,” without giving the reader time to feel any of it in their body. 6. **Universal instead of specific** Generalised “queer suffering” instead of the exact texture of a cop misgendering you while he’s got his boot on your neck, or the specific terror of running out of T during a supply-chain crunch. 7. **Sex as choreography** Writing bodies like they’re in a fragrance ad instead of two scared, dysphoric, half-drunk people negotiating scars and trauma mid-hookup. ### Deep critique of the current intention & plan through this lens | Element in the plan | Risk of LLM trope | Why it’s dangerous here | | -- | -- | -- | | Clean 8-chapter symmetry, every chapter exactly 1000 words | Architectural writing | Humans don’t hit word-count beats like clockwork; the story breathes unevenly. Ch4 could be 600 words of pure sex and dread, Ch7 could be 1800 words of screaming on a roof. | | “Someone walks away. Someone doesn’t.” | Neat reversible ending | Classic LLM move to give the illusion of tragedy while keeping narrative symmetry. Feels like a coin flip instead of a gutting. | | Chapter 2 as neat flashback of “the good part” | Romantic nostalgia filter | Real memory is messier—humans remember the fight in the Denny’s parking lot at 3 a.m. more clearly than the first kiss. | | Silas injecting Mara’s T as erotic intimacy | Fetish choreography | Can easily slide into “sexy medical play” instead of the raw terror of “I literally cannot draw this dose because I’m dope-sick and you’re the only person I trust with a needle.” | | Roof confrontation → empty chamber click | Movie-trailer beat | Very cinematic, very “cool.” Humans tend to fumble confrontations; the gun might jam with a live round, or someone just starts crying and can’t stop. | | Final line already written (“He was all cigarette smoke…”) | Framing device closure | Humans rarely get to close the loop that neatly. Using the exact prompt as epigraph can feel like the model winking at itself. | | “Showdown is short, brutal, and intimate” | Vague intensity words | Tells instead of trusting the reader to feel the brutality in the prose itself. | ### Brainstorming ways to make it more human (without rewriting the plan yet) 1. **Let the word counts bleed** Allow chapters to end mid-sentence if that’s where the air goes out of the scene. One chapter could be 400 words of pure voicemail transcription. 2. **Wrong details** Mara’s lighter is a cheap Bic that only works if you flick it upside-down. Silas still uses the same cracked iPhone 8 held together with a rainbow hair tie. Specific, ugly, lived-in. 3. **Dialogue that misfires** People talk over each other, use the wrong name when angry, go dead silent for thirty seconds, laugh at things that aren’t funny. 4. **Body failure** Mara throws up after sex from withdrawal. Silas gets a bloody nose mid-kiss from too much ketamine the night before. Dysphoria spikes at inconvenient moments—binding coming undone during a chase, voice cracking on the phone with a client. 5. **Memory that lies** Flashbacks contradict each other. In one memory Silas said “I’ll never leave you,” in another he said “I never said that.” Neither is flagged as more “true.” 6. **Chosen family that can’t save anyone** The lesbian bartender lends Mara a couch but won’t hide a body. The drag queen will do your makeup for the funeral but not for the courthouse. 7. **Sex that is sometimes bad** One hookup where someone dissociates halfway through and they just stop and stare at the water stain on the ceiling. No grand reconciliation orgasm. 8. **Queer-specific violence that isn’t metaphorical** A side character deadnamed on a coroner’s report. Someone’s legal name doxxed on a TERF forum. The quiet terror of a pharmacy refusing to fill T because the script is one day “too old.” 9. **Let the city be ordinary and evil** Not dystopian—just late-stage capitalist America doing what it already does. A Pride flag at the precinct next to a Thin Blue Line sticker. A clinic with rainbows on the wall that still won’t prescribe hormones without parental consent for 25-year-olds. 10. **Ending that denies catharsis** Maybe nobody gets the last word. Maybe the final image is Mara walking away and dropping the cigarette into a puddle without ceremony. No voice-over, no summation, just the sound of rain swallowing everything. These are the pressure points where the piece can either feel like it was written by someone who’s bled in those boots—or by something that read a lot of Raymond Chandler and watched Bound too many times. The difference is in the stink of real smoke under the fingernails.
Ok now with these considerations in mind, formulate the final plan for the a humanlike, compelling short piece in {n_chapters} chapters. Bear in mind the constraints of the piece (each chapter is just 1000 words). Above all things, the plan must serve the original prompt. We will use the same format as before: # Intention <State your formulated intentions for the piece, synthesised from the the parts of the brainstorming session that worked, and avoiding the parts that didn't. Be explicit about the choices you have made about plot, voice, stylistic choices, things you intend to aim for & avoid.> # Chapter Planning <Write a brief chapter plan for all {n_chapters} chapters.>
Model Output:
# Intention This is a love story that refuses to save anyone, least of all the lovers. Voice: Mara Voss, 36, non-binary, ex-cop, smoker’s rasp, sentences like cracked sidewalks (short, uneven, sometimes beautiful when the light hits the puddles just right). First person, present tense, no filter. The prose will smell of wet wool, American Spirits, and the metallic tang of blood in the back of the throat. Tone: ugly-tender. Desire and revulsion braided so tight you can’t tell which is which. Sex is clumsy, dysphoria is constant, jokes are cruel because tenderness is scarier. No redemption, no catharsis, no moral takeaway. The city is just Tacoma-by-another-name in 2025, broke, rainy, and quietly murdering its queers one pharmacy denial and one bad batch at a time. Core rule: every moment has to earn its cigarette. Nothing is here to be cool. If a line feels like it belongs on a moodboard, it dies. Explicit choices - Length: chapters will vary 750–1300 words. Some will end mid-sentence if that’s where the air runs out. - Silas Crane is never secretly good. He is beautiful, ruthless, and doomed. - Mara is never secretly noble. They are complicit, exhausted, and still stupid for him. - The mystery (who’s cutting the T) is secondary; the real question is how long two people can keep choosing the worst possible thing for each other. - Queer details will be specific, mundane, and brutal: wrong pronouns on a warrant, a binder soaked with blood, the exact sound a pharmacy tech makes when she says “I need to speak to the doctor.” - No heroic found-family cavalry. Friends help a little, then look away. That’s what love looks like here. Things I will murder on sight - Purple descriptions of rain - Slow-motion gunfights - Anyone learning a lesson - The phrase “shattered neon” - Clean endings # Chapter Planning **Chapter 1 – The Kid in the Bathroom (≈950 words)** Mara is pissing in the handicapped stall at The Tidepool when a shaking 19-year-old trans boy bangs on the door. His name is Leo; his last vial of T was cloudy and now his friends are dying. He has $400 and a photo of Silas Crane leaning against a brick wall, cigarette dangling. Mara hasn’t said that name in four years. They take the money anyway. Ends with Mara outside in the rain, lighting a smoke off Leo’s lighter because theirs is dead, tasting his cheap cherry lip balm and hating themselves already. **Chapter 2 – How We Used to Bleed (≈1100 words)** Flashback, jagged: Mara still wearing the uniform, Silas selling bootleg estrogen out of a cooler in the back of a gay bar. First time they fucked in Mara’s cruiser, radio squawking about a domestic two blocks away. Silas laughing while Mara cried because the sex felt like forgiveness and they both knew it wasn’t. Ends with the night Silas disappeared—no note, just an empty lockbox and a lipstick print on Mara’s badge. **Chapter 3 – Fish Guts and Freezer Burn (≈900 words)** Present. Mara finds Silas in an abandoned cannery on the docks. He’s cooking T in beaker jars like meth, barefoot, track marks on his feet because veins are hard to find after a while. They argue price, history, whose fault everything is. Silas draws Mara’s weekly dose with the same steady hand he uses to measure fentanyl. Injects it slow while Mara watches his own blood backfill the syringe. Neither of them says thank you. **Chapter 4 – Glitter in the Lungs (≈850 words)** Leo’s body washes up under the pier, lungs sparkling with cosmetic mica. Official cause: drowning. Mara knows the ligature marks. At the morgue the tech misgenders Leo on the toe tag like it’s nothing. Silas texts coordinates for a meet—no words, just an address and a cigarette emoji. **Chapter 5 – Room 12, Sea-View Motel (≈1250 words)** They hole up in a pay-by-the-hour motel that smells of mildew and old come. Fuck twice: first time angry, second time worse because it’s gentle. Afterward Silas admits someone inside his crew is cutting the hormones and selling the real stuff to rich kids in Seattle. Mara realises Silas let Leo die to protect the bigger score. They fall asleep back-to-back, Mara’s gun under the pillow, Silas snoring like nothing’s wrong. **Chapter 6 – Knife in the Walk-In (≈1050 words)** Drag brunch ambush. Silas’s lieutenant tries to take over. Gunfire in a kitchen that smells of bacon grease and hairspray. Mara takes a knife just under the ribs saving Silas, who drags them into the walk-in freezer and sews the wound with fishing line while humming an old Billie Holiday song they used to fuck to. Silas finally says the thing neither of them has said sober: “I knew the kid was gonna die.” Mara throws up on his shoes. **Chapter 7 – Roof at 4:17 a.m. (≈1300 words)** Long, ugly fight on the club roof while sirens wail below. Mara has the gun now. They list every betrayal like rosary beads. Ends with Mara pressing the barrel under Silas’s chin, pulling the trigger on an empty chamber, then once more on a live round that doesn’t fire because the gun is old police junk and Tacoma rain gets everywhere. Silas kisses them anyway, tastes like copper and goodbye. **Chapter 8 – The Tide Takes Everything (≈800 words)** Docks at dawn. The traitor is revealed: Mara’s old partner from vice, trying to “clean the streets” by poisoning the supply. Short, brutal fight in the rain—no music, no slow-mo. One bullet does the job. Mara and Silas stand over the body. No speeches. Silas lights a cigarette off the warm muzzle, hands it to Mara without looking. They share it until it burns their fingers. Then Silas walks into the water fully dressed. Mara doesn’t stop him. Last image: Mara alone on the pier, dropping the butt into a puddle, watching the cherry die without a sound.
Perfect. Now with the outline more crystallised, and bearing in mind the discussion on human writing vs LLM pitfalls, we will flesh out our characters. Lets go through each of our main characters: - Write about their background, personality, idiosyncracies, flaws. Be specific and come up with examples to anchor & ground the character's profile (both core and trivial) - Briefly describe their physicality: appearance, how they carry themselves, express, interact with the world. - Concisely detail their motives, allegiences and existing relationships. Think from the perspective of the character as a real breathing thinking feeling individual in this world. - Write a couple quotes of flavour dialogue / internal monologue from the character to experiment with their voice. Output like this: # Character 1 name <character exploration> # Character 2 name <character exploration> etc
Model Output:
># Mara Voss (narrator, 36, non-binary, they/them) Background Grew up in a double-wide outside Puyallup with a dad who fixed logging trucks and a mom who waitressed graveyard at the casino. Came out at sixteen, got kicked out at seventeen, lived in a group home until they were old enough to lie about their age and join the Army. Did one tour in Afghanistan as a military policeman, learned how to fill out paperwork on dead civilians. Came home, tested into the Tacoma PD because the benefits were good and the recruiter called them “sir” exactly once. Made detective in vice, burned out in four years. Left the force the night they signed off on a raid that put half the queens from the old Paradise Lounge in the hospital. Been private ever since, mostly finding runaway trans kids for worried parents who still deadname them in the paperwork. Personality & flaws Runs on spite and caffeine. Sarcastic as self-defense, tender only when drunk or bleeding. Chronic insomniac; sleeps in three-hour bursts fully dressed. Keeps a running tally of every person they’ve failed and recites it like Hail Marys when they’re trying not to swallow the gun. Terrible with money—takes cases they can’t afford to take because the kid reminds them of someone. Still flinches when sirens get too close. Idiosyncrasies Always has two lighters: one cheap Bic that only works if you flick it upside-down, one Zippo they stole off a dead soldier that’s never had fluid in ten years. Orders the same diner breakfast everywhere—black coffee, eggs over-medium, rye toast dry—so they don’t have to talk. Calls everyone “sweetheart” when they’re about to lie. Physicality Five-ten, broad-shouldered, top surgery scars like pale lightning under collarbones. Shaves their head with a #1 guard every Sunday night whether it needs it or not. Grey is already coming in at the temples. Hands always shaking just enough that they keep them in pockets. Dresses like an off-duty contractor: hoodies, Carhartt beanies, boots held together with duct tape. Walks like someone expecting a punch—shoulders forward, scanning corners. Motives right now Prove they’re not the cop anymore. Keep the kids breathing. Punish Silas or fuck him or both, order unclear. Key relationships - Silas: the wound that never closed. - Detective Ray Delgado (old partner): hates them quietly, professionally. - Juno at The Tidepool bar: will pour them one on the house but won’t let them sleep in the bathroom anymore. Voice samples “Four years sober from him and I still check the bottom of every glass for his reflection.” “Yeah, sweetheart, I’ll find your boyfriend. Rates are two-fifty a day plus expenses and the soul I apparently still have on layaway.” # Silas Crane (32, trans man, he/him) Background Born Shayla Crane in a trailer park outside Mobile, Alabama. Mom OD’d when he was nine, raised by a grandmother who called hormones “the devil’s candy.” Ran away at fifteen, did survival sex work up and down I-5 until he landed in Tacoma at nineteen. Started dealing party drugs to pay for top surgery, graduated to black-market T when the clinics started turning people away post-2016. Built a little empire out of coolers and burner phones. Has been to county jail twice, state prison once (eighteen months for possession with intent). Came out looking prettier and meaner. Personality & flaws Charming the way fire is warm—right up until it isn’t. Pathological optimist about schemes, pathological nihilist about people. Laughs too loud at funerals. Keeps score of every kindness and every betrayal in the same mental ledger. Will burn a building down to keep one person warm for five minutes, then feel bad about the people inside. Idiosyncrasies Rolls his own cigarettes with one hand while steering with his knee. Still uses a cracked iPhone 8 held together by a rainbow hair tie. Calls everyone “baby” even when he’s threatening to kill them. Has a habit of humming old torch songs when he’s measuring out doses—says it keeps his hands steady. Physicality Five-six but carries himself taller. Delicate bones, sharp cheekbones, eyes the color of creek water over rust. Full sleeve tattoos done in prison—roses and switchblades and a mermaid with track marks. Dresses like a 90s rent boy who won the lottery: silk shirts unbuttoned too far, gold chain, boots that cost more than Mara’s rent. Moves like smoke—fluid, always half-turned toward the exit. Motives right now Stay alive. Stay pretty. Keep the kids shooting clean T even if he has to poison half of them to do it. Get Mara back in his bed one more time before the world ends, because the world is definitely ending. Key relationships - Mara: the only person who ever saw him ugly and stayed. - Ray Delgado: pays him for information, thinks he’s doing good. - Leo (the dead kid): felt guilty for exactly six hours. Voice samples “Relax, baby, I’m not the devil. I just rent from him on weekends.” “You still keep that little scar I gave you? Good. Means you remember who you belong to when the lights go out.”

Chapter 1

Great. Now let's write this. Follow your plan. Output chapter 1, without any additional commentary. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The Tidepool smells like bleach and broken dreams on the best nights. Tonight it smells like piss and wet dog. The jukebox is stuck on a 2009 Rihanna song nobody ever liked, and the bartender, Juno, keeps giving me the look that says my tab is about to become a blood debt. I’m in the handicapped stall, door locked, one boot up on the toilet seat so I can piss without sitting on whatever’s growing on the rim. Someone starts banging. “Occupied,” I croak. “Mara?” A kid’s voice, cracking like thin ice. “Mara Voss?” I zip, flush, open the door. He’s nineteen maybe, skinny, shaking so hard his hoodie rattles. Eyes red from crying or something worse. “You’re Voss, right? The finder?” I wipe my hands on my jeans. “Depends who’s asking.” He digs in a messenger bag and pulls out an envelope fat with twenties and a Polaroid. The photo is Silas leaning against brick, cigarette glowing, eyes looking straight into the lens like he already knows I’m holding this four years later. My stomach drops into my boots. “My name’s Leo,” the kid says. “My dealer gave me bad shit. Cloudy. Two of my friends are dead. One’s in the ICU pissing blood. I need you to find him before he kills the rest of us.” I thumb the cash. Four hundred bucks. Smells like desperation and cheap weed. “Kid, I don’t do drug cases anymore.” He starts crying, ugly gulping sobs. “Please. Everyone says you’re the only one who ever made him scared.” I hate that this is still true. Outside, the rain is doing its level best to wash Tacoma into the sound. Leo trails me across the parking lot like a stray cat. He offers me his lighter when mine refuses to catch. It’s one of those clear plastic ones filled with pink fluid and glitter. The flame tastes like cherry lip balm and plastic. I cough smoke. “When’s the last time you shot?” I ask. “Three days. Starting to feel it.” He hugs himself. “He said this batch was pure. Said he cooked it himself.” Silas always did have a Messiah complex with a side of homicide. I give Leo my card, the one with the coffee stain shaped like Australia. “Go to the all-night diner on Pacific. Order pancakes. Don’t shoot anything that isn’t in a sealed vial from a pharmacy. I’ll call you.” He nods so hard I think his neck might snap. I watch him disappear into the sodium lights, then stand there getting wet because I’m trying to talk myself out of this. Four years ago I swore I’d let Silas Crane rot wherever he crawled off to. Four years ago I still believed in oaths. The cherry of my cigarette hisses dead in a puddle. I flick the butt away and light another off the glowing tip before it hits the ground. Old parlor trick. Silas taught me that one. Across the street, the neon of the queer club flickers between PINK and INK like it can’t decide what kind of night this is. Music thumps, bass you feel in your teeth. I know he’s in there. I can smell him through the rain: American Spirits and that cheap cologne he stole from a department store in 2019, the one that smells like cedar and bad decisions. I cross the street. The bouncer, a six-foot-something queen named Marisol, lifts the velvet rope without a word. She’s wearing a wig taller than my future and pity in her eyes. Inside, the air is sweat and strobe lights and bodies that don’t care if tomorrow comes. I shoulder through dancers, past the drag queen lip-syncing for tips, past the back bar where the bartenders pour with their tits out for the straight boys who wandered in by accident. And there he is. Silas Crane, leaning on the far end of the main bar like nothing ever happened. Same half-smirk, same sharp cheekbones catching the purple light. Shirt unbuttoned one button too far, gold chain catching the strobes. He’s thinner. Prison or fentanyl or both. Track marks hidden under fresh ink, roses and switchblades crawling up his neck. He lifts a cigarette to his lips, realizes he’s out of fire, and without looking reaches sideways. Some baby dyke in a mesh top offers her lighter. He leans in, cupping the flame like it’s something precious, inhales, tilts his head back so the smoke pours out slow. Then his eyes find me across the room. For one second the smirk falters. Just one. Then it’s back, wider, crueler, like the last four years were a joke he’s been saving. He raises his glass in a toast. I taste blood. Must’ve bitten my tongue. Leo’s four hundred bucks are burning a hole in my pocket, and Silas Crane is all cigarette smoke and bad ideas, and I already know exactly how this ends. I walk toward him anyway.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 1)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter 1 executes its plan with considerable skill and mostly earns what it reaches for. Let me work through the strengths and weaknesses carefully. VOICE AND PROSE: The first-person present-tense voice is consistent and genuinely distinctive. "The jukebox is stuck on a 2009 Rihanna song nobody ever liked" is a precise, earned detail—it does real work establishing the bar's sad specificity without reaching for atmosphere. "Eyes red from crying or something worse" is economical and good. The prose largely avoids the purple rain descriptions the plan explicitly forbade, which is a real achievement given the setting. However, there are moments where the writing strains. "The neon of the queer club flickers between PINK and INK like it can't decide what kind of night this is" is the kind of line that sounds clever but doesn't quite hold up—neon flickering between two states of its own name is a bit too cute, and "what kind of night this is" is vague. "A wig taller than my future" is the sort of quip that belongs on a moodboard, which the plan explicitly said to murder. "Smells like desperation and cheap weed" is a cliché. "Broken dreams" in the opening line is also a cliché the plan should have caught. METAPHOR QUALITY: Most metaphors land. "Shaking so hard his hoodie rattles" is vivid and specific. "Cracking like thin ice" for the kid's voice is slightly worn but functional. "My stomach drops into my boots" is a cliché. The cigarette parlor trick as character detail (Silas taught me that) is excellent—it does triple duty: establishes intimacy, establishes Silas's influence, and shows Mara's muscle memory betraying them before their mind does. DIALOGUE: The dialogue is lean and functional. "Depends who's asking" is a stock PI line, but it's used self-awarely in a genre that knows its tropes. Leo's exposition ("My dealer gave me bad shit. Cloudy. Two of my friends are dead") is a bit on-the-nose as pure information delivery, but the emotional context (ugly gulping sobs) saves it from feeling purely mechanical. Silas has no dialogue here, which is the right call—his entrance is visual and gestural. SHOW VS. TELL: The chapter mostly shows. "I hate that this is still true" after "the only one who ever made him scared" is a small tell, but it's earned by the context and the voice. The Silas reveal is handled well—we see him before Mara processes him, which is good technique. PLOT AND PLAN ADHERENCE: The chapter follows its plan closely. Leo appears, delivers the photo and money, the setup is established. The ending with Mara walking toward Silas is exactly as planned. The cherry lip balm lighter detail is present. The plan called for Mara to taste cherry lip balm and hate themselves—the chapter delivers the lighter taste but the self-hatred is more implied than stated, which is actually better than the plan's more explicit version. CHARACTER CONSISTENCY: Mara reads as the character described—sardonic, self-aware about their own weakness, using humor as armor ("Silas always did have a Messiah complex with a side of homicide"). The detail about two lighters from the profile doesn't appear, which is a small miss. The diner card with the coffee stain is a nice specific touch consistent with the profile's "orders the same diner breakfast everywhere" detail. QUEER SPECIFICITY: The chapter is lighter on queer-specific detail than the plan promised—Leo's trans identity is established but the "specific, mundane, and brutal" queer details the plan promised (wrong pronouns on warrants, binder soaked with blood) don't appear yet, which is appropriate for a first chapter but worth noting. EMOTIONAL ENGAGEMENT: The chapter succeeds at establishing stakes and dread. The four-year gap, the photo, the muscle memory of the cigarette trick—these create genuine emotional weight. The ending is effective without being overwrought. WEAKNESSES: A few clichés slip through ("broken dreams," "smells like desperation," "stomach drops into my boots"). The Marisol bouncer detail, while charming, slightly slows the momentum. The final line—"I walk toward him anyway"—is the right note but also somewhat expected as a chapter closer. Overall this is a strong opening chapter that largely delivers on its ambitious plan. The voice is genuine, the details are mostly earned, and the setup is efficient without being mechanical. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Compelling Plot: 14 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 5 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 4 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 5 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 15 Characters Consistent with Profile: 16 Followed Chapter Plan: 17 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 18

Chapter 2

Ok now chapter 2. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The night I met Silas I was still wearing the uniform. It was my last week in vice before I turned in the badge for good. I’d spent the evening busting a card game in the back of the old Paradise Lounge, dragging queens out in handcuffs while they called me every name God forgot to invent. One of them spat blood on my boots and whispered, “You’ll be one of us one day, baby cop.” I laughed then. I’m not laughing now. After shift I parked the cruiser behind the club because I needed a drink that didn’t come out of the precinct vending machine. It was October, raining the way it only rains here—like the sky’s trying to wash the sin off the sidewalks and failing. Silas was leaning against the dumpster sharing a joint with a baby drag queen in a silver mini-dress. The queen saw my uniform and vanished. Silas stayed put, took another hit, and offered me the joint like it was a handshake. “Officer,” he said, voice soft Alabama still clinging to the edges, “you look like someone just told you the world’s uglier than you thought.” I took the joint because my hands were shaking and I didn’t want him to see. The smoke tasted like skunk and cherry cough drops. I coughed. He laughed, low and warm. “First time smoking girl weed?” he asked. “First time smoking at all,” I lied. He studied me the way you study a locked door you’re planning to pick. “You got pretty eyes for a pig.” I should’ve arrested him right there. Instead I asked what he was selling tonight. “Whatever keeps the kids breathing,” he said, and pulled a dented Igloo cooler from behind the dumpster. Inside, packed in ice packs: vials of testosterone, estrogen, spironolactone, needles still in sterile wrap. Hand-labeled in Sharpie. Some of the labels were already smudged. I stared too long. He noticed. “You need something, officer?” I told him to shut up. He smiled like I’d said please. Ten minutes later I was in the back of my own cruiser with my duty belt on the floorboard and Silas Crane straddling my lap. His mouth tasted like weed and peppermint gum. My shirt was half-unbuttoned, binder shoved up under my arms because I couldn’t breathe and didn’t want to. His hands were everywhere, cold from the ice packs, leaving wet fingerprints on my skin. I remember the radio crackling about a domestic three blocks over, some guy beating his wife with a lamp. Dispatch asking if any units were free. I reached for the handset to say 10-4, and Silas took it from me, turned the volume down, and kissed me so hard my lip split. Blood in our mouths. Rain on the windows. The seatbelt buckle digging into my spine. His hips grinding down like he wanted to leave bruises shaped like proof. After, we stayed tangled, breathing the same air. He traced the fresh top-surgery scars under my pecs with one finger, gentle like he was reading braille. “You’re beautiful,” he said. “Ugly job, but beautiful you.” I laughed until I cried. Couldn’t tell which was which. That night he moved into my apartment like he’d been there forever. Left his toothbrush in the cup, his hormones in my fridge, his fingerprints on everything I owned. We fucked on every surface. Fought in the hallway because he kept dealing out of my bathroom and I kept pretending I didn’t know. I wrote citations I never filed. He cooked T in my kitchen with a hot plate and a prayer. I told myself I was keeping him safe. Keeping the kids safe. Keeping the whole broken system from swallowing one more pretty boy with a death wish. The night it ended, I came home to an empty lockbox and a single lipstick print on my badge—wet red, perfect, obscene. No note. Just the smell of his cologne hanging in the air like smoke after a fire. I sat on the floor and smoked an entire pack while the rain tried to drown the city. Told myself I was relieved. Four years later, standing outside The Tidepool tasting cherry lip balm on a borrowed lighter, I know exactly how well that worked. Some wounds don’t scar. They just keep bleeding quiet.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 2)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This is Chapter 2, a flashback chapter establishing the origin of Mara and Silas's relationship. Let me assess it carefully against the plan, the character profiles, and the writing itself. **Adherence to Plan and Character Profiles** The chapter plan called for: Mara still in uniform, Silas selling bootleg hormones from a cooler, first sex in Mara's cruiser with radio squawking about a domestic, Silas laughing while Mara cried, and ending with the empty lockbox and lipstick on the badge. The chapter hits all these beats faithfully. The character profiles are honored: Silas's Alabama accent, his charm-as-fire quality, his "baby" energy, his track marks (implied through the hormone-dealing context), his silk-and-gold aesthetic (not yet present but consistent with his origin story). Mara's binder, top surgery scars, shaking hands, and self-deception are all present. **Voice and Prose Quality** The voice is strong and consistent. "The sky's trying to wash the sin off the sidewalks and failing" is a good line that earns its place without being purple. "He studied me the way you study a locked door you're planning to pick" is a solid, functional metaphor. The prose has the cracked-sidewalk rhythm the plan promised: short declarative sentences, occasional longer ones that breathe differently. Some lines land very well: "Blood in our mouths. Rain on the windows. The seatbelt buckle digging into my spine." The physical specificity here is excellent—the seatbelt buckle is exactly the kind of unglamorous detail that makes the scene feel real rather than cinematic. "His hips grinding down like he wanted to leave bruises shaped like proof" is perhaps the chapter's best line—it's specific, psychologically loaded, and earns its metaphorical weight. **Where It Falters** The ending: "Some wounds don't scar. They just keep bleeding quiet." This is the chapter's weakest moment. It's the kind of line that belongs on a moodboard—exactly what the plan said to murder on sight. It's a tidy aphorism that summarizes the emotional content rather than embodying it. After a chapter of showing, this is pure telling, and it's a cliché dressed up in minimalist clothing. "Laughed until I cried. Couldn't tell which was which" is also a familiar construction—the braided-emotions trope appears in the plan itself ("desire and revulsion braided so tight you can't tell which is which") and here it feels like the plan bleeding into the prose rather than the prose discovering something new. The queen who spits blood and says "You'll be one of us one day, baby cop" is a bit on-the-nose—it's the kind of prophetic street wisdom that feels planted rather than observed. It's not terrible, but it's the most conventionally noir moment in the chapter. **Dialogue** The dialogue is generally good. Silas's lines feel authentic to his character: "Whatever keeps the kids breathing" is excellent—it's charming, self-mythologizing, and reveals his worldview in four words. "You got pretty eyes for a pig" is functional. "You're beautiful. Ugly job, but beautiful you" is slightly too neat, a little too quotable, but it works in context because it's the kind of thing Silas would say. **Emotional Engagement** The chapter is genuinely emotionally engaging. The sex scene is handled with the right mix of desire and dysfunction—the binder shoved up, the radio about the domestic, the split lip. These details accumulate into something that feels true rather than performed. The transition from the sex scene to the cohabitation to the abandonment is efficiently handled without feeling rushed. **Darkness** The darkness is well-earned. The domestic call Mara ignores, the queens dragged out in handcuffs, the complicity—these aren't decorative. They establish Mara's moral position without editorializing. The lipstick on the badge is a strong image, though it risks being slightly too symbolic. **Overall Assessment** This is a competent-to-good chapter with a strong voice, solid character work, and a few moments of genuine excellence undercut by a weak ending and occasional reliance on familiar noir shorthand. The plan is followed faithfully. The characters are consistent with their profiles. The prose mostly avoids purple excess, though it flirts with it at the close. The chapter does what it needs to do narratively and emotionally, with only minor stumbles. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Compelling Plot: 13 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 5 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 7 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 4 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 5 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 15 Characters Consistent with Profile: 17 Followed Chapter Plan: 18 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 3

Ok now chapter 3. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The cannery sits on the edge of Commencement Bay like a busted jaw. Windows punched out years ago, gulls nesting in the rafters, the whole place smelling of rust and dead fish and the particular rot that only happens when saltwater meets concrete and gives up. I park two blocks away and walk in through a side door hanging off one hinge. My boots crunch on broken glass and fish bones. Somewhere water drips in a rhythm like a dying clock. Silas is in the main processing room, barefoot on cracked tile, standing over a folding table covered in lab glass that looks stolen from three different high schools. A hot plate glows orange under a beaker of clear liquid. He’s wearing a wife-beater gone grey from too many washes and a pair of my old sweatpants I never got back. Gold chain glints against his collarbones. Track marks on the tops of his feet like tiny bruises. He doesn’t look up when I step into the light. “Took you long enough,” he says, voice soft, like we’re picking up a conversation from yesterday instead of four years. “Thought maybe you got religion or something.” I stop ten feet away. Close enough to smell cedar and chemicals, far enough I can still draw if I have to. “Leo’s dead,” I say. Silas keeps stirring whatever’s in the beaker with a glass rod. Slow circles. “Yeah. Heard that.” “That’s all you got?” He shrugs. “Kids die. Happens.” I pull the Polaroid Leo gave me from my pocket, hold it up. “He paid me to find you.” Silas finally looks. His eyes flick to the photo, then to me. Something moves behind them (regret, maybe, or just indigestion). “He paid you with what, four hundred? Cute.” He sets the rod down, wipes his hands on a rag that used to be a dish towel. “You take the case, Mara?” I don’t answer. He smiles like he already knows. He crosses the room, opens a chest freezer that hums like it’s on its last lung. Inside: rows of vials nestled in ice packs, labels in his handwriting. He pulls one out, holds it to the light. Clear. Perfect. “Clean,” he says. “Cooked it myself last night. You still doing a hundred a week?” My hands start shaking the way they do when I haven’t shot in five days. I shove them in my pockets. “Don’t,” I say. “Don’t what?” He grabs a syringe from a sterile pack, tears it open with his teeth. “Don’t take care of you? Too late for that.” He steps close. Too close. I smell the cedar, the sweat, the faint sweetness of whatever solvent he uses. He taps the vial against my thigh like he’s testing fruit. “Sit,” he says, nodding at a plastic chair with one leg shorter than the rest. I sit. He kneels in front of me, pushes my knee aside with his shoulder like he belongs there. Rolls the sweatpants leg up slow. My skin hasn’t seen daylight in weeks. He swabs the spot high on my thigh with alcohol that stings cold. “Still got that little freckle,” he murmurs. “Missed it.” I watch the ceiling while he finds the vein on the first try. Always did have steady hands. The needle slides in clean. I feel the cold bloom under my skin, then the push. Blood backfills the syringe, dark red mixing with clear. He depresses the plunger slow, eyes on my face the whole time. “There you go,” he whispers. “Breathe, baby.” I didn’t realize I was holding my breath. Air tastes like fish and freedom and shame. He pulls the needle out, presses a cotton ball to the spot, tapes it down with a piece of masking tape he rips with his teeth. Then he stays there on his knees, forehead against my knee like he’s praying. “I’m in trouble,” he says into my jeans. “I know.” “Real trouble this time.” “I know.” He looks up. His eyes are wet, or maybe it’s just the fluorescent light flickering. “Someone’s cutting my supply. Replacing half the T with paint thinner and glitter. Kids are dying, Mara.” I laugh. Can’t help it. Sounds like gravel. “You’re upset kids are dying now?” He flinches. Good. “I need your eyes,” he says. “Your old cop eyes. Find who’s doing it, and I’ll give you whatever you want.” I stand up so fast the chair falls over. He stays on his knees. “What I want,” I say, “is for you to rot.” He smiles, small and sad. “Too late. Already am.” I leave him there on the concrete floor, barefoot among the broken glass and the future. My leg feels warm and steady for the first time in days. Doesn’t stop me hating him. Doesn’t stop me hating myself more.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 3)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter 3 of this neo-noir queer romance is a strong piece of writing that largely delivers on its plan and the story's stated intentions. Let me examine it carefully across the relevant dimensions. STRENGTHS: The chapter follows its plan faithfully. Silas is in the cannery, barefoot, cooking T, with track marks on his feet. The injection scene plays out as planned—Silas drawing Mara's dose with steady hands, blood backfilling the syringe, neither saying thank you. The argument about price, history, and fault is compressed but present. The chapter ends where it should. Character consistency is excellent. Silas is charming, dangerous, and tender in a way that feels manipulative rather than redemptive—"Still got that little freckle. Missed it" is exactly the kind of line that works because it's simultaneously intimate and predatory. Mara's voice is consistent: sardonic, self-aware, complicit. "I laugh. Can't help it. Sounds like gravel." The self-loathing is earned rather than performed. The injection scene is the chapter's centerpiece and it works. The physicality is specific and uncomfortable—the cold bloom under skin, blood backfilling, Silas on his knees with his forehead against Mara's knee "like he's praying." This is the ugly-tender braiding the plan called for. The intimacy of the injection as a substitute for other kinds of intimacy is handled with restraint. Dialogue is generally strong. "Kids die. Happens." is brutal and efficient. The exchange "I need your eyes" / "What I want is for you to rot" / "Too late. Already am" lands because it's earned by the scene's accumulation of tension. The opening description of the cannery is economical—"like a busted jaw" is a good image that doesn't overstay its welcome. WEAKNESSES: The plan called for Silas to be "cooking T in beaker jars like meth"—the meth comparison is absent, which slightly softens the intended parallel. The plan also specified "track marks on his feet because veins are hard to find after a while"—this detail appears but without the explanatory context, which would have added more texture to Silas's self-destruction. "Something moves behind them (regret, maybe, or just indigestion)" is a good line but the parenthetical is slightly too cute, a little too self-consciously clever in a way that briefly breaks the prose's register. "the future" in the final image—"barefoot among the broken glass and the future"—is the chapter's most forced poetic moment. It's the kind of line that sounds profound but doesn't quite mean anything specific. The plan explicitly warned against lines that "belong on a moodboard," and this one skirts that edge. The chapter is somewhat thin on the "argument about price, history, whose fault everything is" that the plan promised. What we get is more of a negotiation than an argument, and the history between them is gestured at rather than excavated. The plan's version was more confrontational; the chapter's version is more seductive, which is interesting but represents a departure. "Air tastes like fish and freedom and shame" is a tricolon that works—fish and freedom is unexpected, shame is earned—though "freedom" is slightly abstract here. The chapter doesn't quite reach the emotional complexity of the plan's vision. Mara's complicity is shown but the self-loathing could cut deeper. The ending is good but slightly tidy for a story that promised cracked-sidewalk prose. Overall, this is accomplished, controlled writing that earns most of its moments. The prose is disciplined, the characters are consistent, and the central scene delivers genuine discomfort and intimacy. Minor lapses in the forced-poetry department and a slightly softer confrontation than planned keep it from being exceptional. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Compelling Plot: 13 Coherent: 17 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 4 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 5 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 15 Characters Consistent with Profile: 16 Followed Chapter Plan: 14 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 4

Ok now chapter 4. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
Leo washes up under Pier 7 just after dawn, face down in six inches of water, hoodie snagged on a barnacled piling. Some early-morning jogger finds him. By the time I get there the uniforms have already strung yellow tape like it’s Christmas. Ray Delgado is standing on the dock smoking a cigarette he doesn’t want. My old partner, still wearing the same cheap suit he bought the year we graduated the academy. He sees me coming and flicks the butt into the bay without turning. “Thought you didn’t do water recoveries anymore,” he says. “I don’t.” I step under the tape. “Who called it in?” “Anonymous tip.” He finally looks at me. His eyes flick to the bandage peeking out from my jeans (Silas’s tape job from last night). “Funny thing. Tip came from a burner registered to your boyfriend.” “He’s not my boyfriend.” “Coulda fooled me.” The body’s laid out on a tarp now. Leo’s skin is grey-blue, lips purple, glitter in his hair like cheap fairy dust. The medical examiner, a tired woman I used to drink with, lifts the hoodie. Bruises around the neck shaped like boot prints. Someone held him under. “Ruled accidental drowning,” she says, loud enough for the uniforms. Then quieter, just for me: “Off record, someone wanted it to look like suicide.” I crouch. Leo’s fingernails are chewed to the quick. One hand still clutches a cracked phone. I pry it loose before anyone notices. Lock screen is a selfie of him and two other baby trans kids, all smiling like tomorrow was guaranteed.
