Ray watches me pocket it. “You’re not here official, Voss.” “Never said I was.” He sighs, the sound of twenty years of disappointment. “Kid had a record. Petty shit. We’re calling it suicide. Family’s already been told.” “Family didn’t even use his name,” I say. Ray shrugs like that settles it. I leave before I punch him. The morgue smells like formaldehyde and burnt coffee. The tech on duty is a dyke named Ruth who owes me for finding her little brother last year. She lets me into the cooler without signing the log. Leo’s on a steel table, toe tag still reading “Shaylee Carter—Female.” Ruth hasn’t fixed it yet. She will, later, when no one’s looking. “Want me to wait?” she asks. “Just five minutes.” She leaves me alone with the body. I stand there longer than five. I touch Leo’s cold cheek, apologize for taking his money, for not moving faster, for every fucking thing I can’t fix. The glitter on his skin sticks to my fingers like guilt. My phone buzzes. Unknown number. Single text: **pier 23. midnight. bring the phone. –S** No cigarette emoji this time. I drive to the waterfront anyway, too early, just to watch the gulls fight over fish guts. The phone in my pocket feels heavier than Leo did. At 11:57 a lone figure steps out of the shadows between shipping containers. Silas is wearing a peacoat two sizes too big and no shoes, like he walked straight out of whatever hole he’s been hiding in. He stops ten feet away, hands in pockets. “You came,” he says. “You killed him.” “No.” His voice cracks on the word. “But I knew it was coming.” I pull Leo’s phone, hold it up. “You sent the tip to Ray.” “Needed you to see what they did to him.” He takes a step closer. “Someone’s burning my whole supply line, Mara. Replacing vials with paint thinner and mica powder. Pretty way to die, right? Looks like an overdose till the autopsy shows your liver dissolved.” “Who?” “That’s what I need you for.” He reaches into the coat, pulls out a roll of hundreds, tosses it at my feet. “Five grand. Find them. Stop them. Or don’t. But if you walk away now, every kid in this city who can’t get a prescription dies sparkly.” I stare at the money getting wet. “Pick it up,” he says quietly. “Or pick me up. Your call.” Rain starts again, soft and mean. I don’t move. Silas smiles like someone just shot his dog and he’s trying not to cry. “Thought so,” he says, and turns to leave. I pick up the money. He doesn’t look back. But I see the tremor in his shoulders, the way he limps favoring the left foot. Track marks there too, probably. He’s running out of real estate. Pier 23 smells like diesel and dead seaweed. I stand there a long time after he’s gone, holding five thousand dollars and a dead boy’s glitter. My hands start shaking again. The new dose is already wearing off. Or maybe that’s just what being near Silas does to me. Either way, I’m in it now. All the way to the bottom.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 4)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter 4 is a tight, functional piece of noir that largely delivers on its plan. Let me assess it carefully across multiple dimensions. STRENGTHS: The chapter follows its plan faithfully: Leo's body washes up, the morgue misgendering on the toe tag is present (though rendered as "Shaylee Carter—Female" rather than just a toe tag detail, which works), Mara pockets the phone, and Silas texts coordinates. The cigarette emoji detail from the plan is notably absent—replaced with a more ominous "no cigarette emoji this time"—which is actually a smart inversion that earns its keep. The prose voice is consistent with Mara's established character: clipped, observational, self-lacerating without being melodramatic. "Glitter on his skin sticks to my fingers like guilt" is the kind of line that could tip into purple but just barely earns itself because the glitter has been established as a physical detail throughout. The morgue scene with Ruth is economical and specific—the detail about the toe tag, the "she will, later, when no one's looking" is genuinely moving without announcing itself. Ray Delgado's appearance is well-handled. His shrug when Mara says the family didn't use Leo's name—"like that settles it"—is a precise, damning character beat. The dialogue between them is spare and functional, carrying subtext without over-explaining. The Silas confrontation at Pier 23 is where the chapter does its heaviest lifting. "Smiles like someone just shot his dog and he's trying not to cry" is a strong image. The power dynamic of the money on the wet ground, Mara's paralysis, the eventual capitulation—this is consistent with both character profiles and the chapter's thematic purpose. WEAKNESSES: "All the way to the bottom" as a closing line is the chapter's most significant failure. It's exactly the kind of noir cliché the writing plan explicitly warns against—it belongs on a moodboard, it announces its own darkness rather than embodying it. The plan says "if a line feels like it belongs on a moodboard, it dies." This one should have died. "Either way, I'm in it now" followed by "All the way to the bottom" is a double-down on the same problem. The chapter earns a quieter ending and then undercuts it with two consecutive lines of self-conscious noir posturing. The line "every kid in this city who can't get a prescription dies sparkly" is Silas being too articulate about his own situation—it's a speech-ready line that feels slightly out of character for someone who should be more oblique and self-serving in his persuasion. "Rain starts again, soft and mean" is a minor purple moment—"soft and mean" is the kind of weather personification the plan warns against, though it's brief enough not to be damning. The chapter is somewhat thin on the emotional complexity promised by the plan. Mara's grief for Leo is stated more than shown—"I touch Leo's cold cheek, apologize for taking his money, for not moving faster, for every fucking thing I can't fix" tells us what Mara feels rather than letting us feel it through more specific, unexpected detail. The apology list is generic. The plan mentions "Silas texts coordinates for a meet—no words, just an address and a cigarette emoji" but the chapter gives us a text with words and no emoji, then references the missing emoji. This is a small but notable deviation that actually works better than the plan. Overall, this is competent, atmospheric noir with a consistent voice, good structural bones, and a few moments of genuine emotional precision. It's undermined by a weak ending and occasional reliance on genre shorthand where specificity was called for. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 13 Emotionally Engaging: 13 Compelling Plot: 13 Coherent: 17 Weak Dialogue: 6 Tell-Don't-Show: 8 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 8 Amateurish: 5 Purple Prose: 5 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 5 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 12 Characters Consistent with Profile: 15 Followed Chapter Plan: 15 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 16

Chapter 5

Ok now chapter 5. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The Sea-View Motel on Pacific Avenue rents by the hour and doesn’t ask questions. The clerk is half-asleep watching infomercials for knives that can cut a penny. I slide him two damp hundreds from Silas’s roll and he hands over the key to Room 12 without looking up. Silas is already inside, sitting on the bed unlacing his boots like he owns the place. One lamp works; it paints everything the color of old nicotine. The carpet is the shade of dried blood and feels wet even when it isn’t. I lock the door, set the chain, lean back against it. He looks up. “You came.” “You paid me.” “That’s not why.” I don’t answer. He’s right and we both hate it. He stands, shrugs off the peacoat. Shirt underneath is damp, clinging to the sharp lines of his ribs. Prison tattoos crawl out from the collar (roses with stems that turn into switchblades). He smells like rain and the cannery chemicals still clinging to his skin. I drop the roll of money on the dresser. It lands with a wet thud. “Talk,” I say. He does. His lieutenant, a queen named Diamond who used to headline at the Paradise before the raid, has been skimming. Taking the clean vials, replacing them with the glitter shit, selling the real hormones to rich tech bros in Seattle who want to “microdose masculinity” for their start-up cults. Diamond’s building her own pipeline. Silas is collateral damage now. “I let Leo die to protect the bigger batch,” he says. Voice flat, like he’s reading someone else’s confession. “Figured one kid was acceptable losses.” I punch him in the mouth. Not hard enough to break anything, just enough to split his lip. Blood beads bright. He licks it, nods like thank you. Then we’re on each other. No romance, no slow undressing. I shove him back onto the bed, he yanks my hoodie over my head so hard a seam rips. Binder stays on; neither of us has time for dysphoria tonight. My jeans are gone, his are around his ankles, boots still on because neither of us trusts the floor. It’s ugly. Teeth and fingernails and the headboard banging loud enough the clerk probably thinks we’re killing someone. He bites my shoulder hard enough to bruise. I drag nails down his back hard enough to draw blood. At one point I have my hand around his throat and he laughs into my mouth, breathless, “Harder.” We come fast, angry, hating each other for how easy it still is. After, we lie side by side staring at water stains on the ceiling that look like continents. The room smells like sex and wet wool. My ribs ache where he elbowed me. Second time is worse. Slower. He kisses the top surgery scars like he’s apologizing to them directly. I let him. I cry a little; he pretends not to notice. When he slides inside me it feels like surrender and I hate how much I need it. Afterward he pillows his head on my stomach, traces the stretch marks on my hips like reading braille again. “I’m scared,” he says into my skin. Voice small, Alabama thick. I thread fingers through his hair. It’s greasy. Needs washing. “I know.” “They’re gonna kill me, Mara.” “Maybe.” “Will you stop them?” I think about Leo’s glitter lungs. About Ruth changing the toe tag when no one was looking. About the way Silas’s hands still steady when he draws up a dose. “No,” I say. He nods like that’s the answer he wanted. We fall asleep back-to-back, guns on the nightstands pointed different directions. Mine’s the old service piece, still smells like gun oil and rain. His is a snub-nose he probably traded hormones for. The clock on the wall is stuck at 3:14 and the second hand twitches like it’s trying to escape. I dream of water closing over my head, glitter filling my mouth. Wake up gasping. Silas is already sitting on the edge of the bed rolling a cigarette with one hand, the other pressed to his ribs like something inside hurts worse than the bruise I left. He lights it off the bedside lamp when I click it on, inhales, offers it to me without turning. I take it. Our fingers touch. Neither of us flinches. Outside, a siren dopplers past and fades. Inside, the room is quiet except for the sound of two people breathing the same poisoned air and pretending it’s enough. I take another drag, hand the cigarette back. He says, “Tomorrow we find Diamond.” I say, “Tonight we’re still alive.” Neither of us believes it.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 5)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter 5 of this neo-noir queer romance is a strong piece of writing that largely delivers on its ambitious plan. Let me assess it carefully across multiple dimensions. STRENGTHS: The chapter follows its plan faithfully. The motel setting, the two sex scenes (first angry, second worse because gentle), Silas's confession about Leo, and the back-to-back sleeping arrangement with guns on nightstands all appear as planned. The revelation about Diamond and the Seattle pipeline is delivered cleanly. The chapter earns its darkness. Character consistency is excellent. Mara's voice is exactly as designed: clipped, self-aware, sardonic, tender only when they can't help it. "No," as the answer to "Will you stop them?" is perfect—it's the answer Silas wanted, and the chapter earns that moment. Silas's Alabama accent thickening when he's scared, his pathological charm, his flat confession ("acceptable losses") followed by accepting the punch like a thank-you—all consistent with the profile. The prose avoids most of the traps the author identified. No purple rain descriptions. No slow-motion violence. The sex scenes are genuinely unglamorous—"ugly. Teeth and fingernails and the headboard banging loud enough the clerk probably thinks we're killing someone." This is exactly the ugly-tender register the plan called for. The dialogue is spare and effective. "You came." / "You paid me." / "That's not why." This exchange does real work. "Tonight we're still alive." / "Neither of us believes it." lands without being overwrought. The queer specificity is present and handled well: the binder staying on, the top surgery scars being kissed like apology, the crying during the second time. These feel lived-in rather than performed. WEAKNESSES: Some moments edge toward the formulaic. "Blood beads bright" is a bit precious. The water stains on the ceiling "that look like continents" is a slightly tired image. The clock stuck at 3:14 with the second hand "twitching like it's trying to escape" is the kind of detail that feels like it belongs on a moodboard—exactly what the author said they'd murder on sight. It's not egregious, but it's a small betrayal of the stated aesthetic. The dream sequence (glitter filling Mara's mouth) is efficient but slightly on-the-nose given Leo's glitter lungs. It works thematically but isn't surprising. "Moves like smoke" appears in the character profile and the prose here has Silas moving with similar fluid descriptors—there's a slight risk of the character becoming a collection of his own profile notes rather than a living person. The ending exchange—"Tomorrow we find Diamond" / "Tonight we're still alive" / "Neither of us believes it"—is good but the final line is a slight tell rather than show. The not-believing could be demonstrated rather than stated. The "reading braille again" detail about stretch marks is a small grace note that works. Overall, this is accomplished, disciplined writing that mostly earns its darkness. The plan is followed, the characters are consistent, the prose is controlled. Minor lapses into slightly too-neat imagery keep it from being exceptional. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Compelling Plot: 14 Coherent: 17 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 4 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 5 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Characters Consistent with Profile: 17 Followed Chapter Plan: 18 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 6

Ok now chapter 6. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
Diamond’s Sunday drag brunch at the old Knights of Columbus hall is invitation-only, cash at the door, no phones. The queens call it “Church.” The rest of us know it’s where the real business happens after the lipstick and lashes come off. Silas and I walk in together at one in the afternoon. He’s wearing a silk blouse the color of arterial blood and the same peacoat. I’m in yesterday’s hoodie and a shoulder holster that shows when I move wrong. Nobody checks us; Silas is still the king until he isn’t. The hall smells like bacon grease, Aqua Net, and bottom-shelf champagne. A queen named Cinnamon is on stage lip-syncing “Chandelier,” voice cracking on the high notes because she’s coming down off something. The crowd whoops anyway. Diamond’s at the head table, crown made of rhinestones and safety pins, holding court like she’s already won. She clocks us the second we step inside. Her smile is all teeth. Silas squeezes my hand once (showtime) then lets go. We split. He heads for the bar, I drift toward the kitchen doors. Plan’s simple: get Diamond alone, make her talk, get out before anyone bleeds too much. We’re idiots. I’m halfway across the floor when the lights cut out. Gunshots. Three fast, like someone knocking too hard. Screams. Tables flip. Someone’s wig goes flying like a startled cat. Emergency lights kick on red. I drop behind an overturned chair, draw the .38. Across the room Silas is crouched behind the bar, eyes wide, blood on his shirt (not his yet). Diamond’s voice cuts through the panic, calm as Sunday morning: “That’s enough.” Four guys in ski masks step out of the kitchen carrying AR pistols. Diamond stands up slow, queen in every inch. “Silas Crane,” she says, “you’re done poisoning my community.” Silas laughs, a cracked sound. “Your community? Bitch, you’re selling my T to tech bros for crypto.” One of the masks moves toward him. I stand up. “Drop it,” I say. The mask swings toward me. Too late. Silas is already moving, faster than sick should allow, tackles the guy, they go down hard. Gun skitters across the floor. Chaos blooms. I fire once into the ceiling; plaster rains. Queens scream and stampede. Someone’s heel snaps. Cinnamon grabs a bottle of Veuve and smashes it over a mask’s head. I’m running for Silas when Diamond steps in my path, holding a little .25 like it’s a prop. “Always cleaning up his messes,” she sneers. She shoots me in the ribs. It’s not cinematic. No slow-motion spin. Just a hot punch and the floor tilting. Breath gone. Blood hot in my mouth. Silas screams my name (first time I’ve heard it soft in years). Then he’s there, dragging me by the armpit into the walk-in freezer, slamming the door with his foot. Inside it’s zero dark and twenty degrees. Bacon grease smell turns metallic. I’m on my back on frozen boxes of chicken wings. Blood soaks my hoodie fast. “Fuck, fuck, fuck.” Silas rips his silk blouse off, balls it, presses hard under my ribs. His hands are shaking now. “Hurts,” I croak. “I know, baby, I know.” He finds the wound (entry clean, no exit). “Through and through. You’re lucky, you lucky idiot.” I laugh and it bubbles blood. He digs in his pocket, comes up with a spool of fishing line and a sewing needle he must’ve stolen from a drag kit. No anesthesia. Just teeth gritted. “This is gonna suck,” he says. “Do it.” He sterilizes the needle with a lighter, threads it with fingers that won’t stop trembling. Hums Billie Holiday under his breath (“Good morning heartache, you old gloomy sight…”) while he stitches. Voice cracking on every note. Halfway through I start crying, ugly hiccupping sobs. He doesn’t stop. When it’s done he ties off the knot with his teeth, tapes a maxi pad from somebody’s purse over the mess. Then he folds himself around me on the freezer floor, arms tight, forehead to mine. Cold seeps in. Blood slows. Outside, the shooting’s stopped. Sirens far away. “I knew Leo was gonna die,” he whispers. “I did the math. One kid or fifty. I picked wrong.” I taste copper and freezer burn. “I hate you,” I say. “I know.” He kisses me anyway. Tastes like blood and bacon and four years of rot. The door handle rattles. Someone trying to get in. Silas reaches for the gun he dropped somewhere, can’t find it. We’re out of time. He presses his mouth to my ear. “If we die here,” he says, “at least we die together.” I want to tell him that’s the worst fucking idea he’s ever had. Instead I close my eyes and wait for the door to open.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 6)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter 6 is a tightly constructed action-and-intimacy sequence that largely delivers on its plan. Let me assess it carefully across dimensions. STRENGTHS: The chapter follows its plan faithfully: drag brunch ambush, gunfire in a kitchen smelling of bacon grease and hairspray, Mara taking a knife (changed to a bullet, which is a reasonable adaptation), the walk-in freezer, fishing line stitching, Billie Holiday humming, and Silas's confession about Leo. The plan's beats are hit with discipline. The voice is consistent with Mara's established character profile. Short, cracked sentences. Dry humor under duress ("We're idiots"). The self-awareness about cinematic clichés ("It's not cinematic. No slow-motion spin. Just a hot punch and the floor tilting.") is exactly the kind of meta-awareness the writing plan demanded and it lands genuinely rather than feeling smug. This is one of the chapter's best moments—it earns its anti-noir stance. Character consistency is strong. Silas's "I knew Leo was gonna die. I did the math. One kid or fifty. I picked wrong" is exactly the kind of ruthless-but-not-secretly-good moment the plan demanded. He's not redeemed by the confession; he's just honest about his calculus. Mara's response ("I hate you. I know.") is appropriately bleak and earned. The Billie Holiday detail ("Good morning heartache, you old gloomy sight…") is specific and resonant—it connects to the character profile's idiosyncrasies and the song choice is thematically apt without being on-the-nose. The physical details are grounded: maxi pad as bandage, fishing line from a drag kit, the needle sterilized with a lighter. These feel real and specific rather than decorative. WEAKNESSES: The chapter's weakest element is Diamond as a villain. She's underdeveloped—her motivation ("you're poisoning my community") is stated but not dramatized, and her line "Always cleaning up his messes" is a bit stock. She functions as a plot mechanism rather than a character. "Chaos blooms" is a slightly purple moment—it's the kind of line that belongs on a moodboard, which the plan explicitly forbade. Similarly, "Blood hot in my mouth" and "Cold seeps in. Blood slows." are effective but verge on the kind of stylized minimalism that can tip into self-conscious cool. The ending—"I close my eyes and wait for the door to open"—is competent but not as sharp as it could be. It's a cliffhanger that feels slightly mechanical given the emotional weight of what just happened. The plan called for Mara throwing up on Silas's shoes after the confession, which would have been more viscerally specific and character-consistent than the current ending. The action sequence itself is functional but not particularly inventive. The ski masks, the AR pistols, the lights cutting out—these are genre-standard. Cinnamon smashing a champagne bottle is a nice touch, but the fight choreography is otherwise unremarkable. "Tastes like blood and bacon and four years of rot" is a strong line. "Eyes wide, blood on his shirt (not his yet)" is a clever parenthetical. These moments show genuine craft. The dialogue is mostly strong—spare, character-consistent, not over-explaining. "Relax, baby" type Silas voice is present. The weakness is Diamond's dialogue, which is expository. Overall: a solid, disciplined chapter that hits its marks without quite achieving the ugly-tender transcendence the best moments of this kind of writing can reach. The plan's most specific detail (Mara vomiting on Silas's shoes) was dropped, which would have been more memorable than the current ending. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 14 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Compelling Plot: 13 Coherent: 16 Weak Dialogue: 6 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 7 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 5 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 5 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 15 Characters Consistent with Profile: 16 Followed Chapter Plan: 14 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 7

Ok now chapter 7. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The roof door sticks because everything in this city sticks. Silas kicks it twice and we stumble out into the rain. Sirens below, red-blue-red-blue painting the wet concrete like a broken heartbeat. The Tidepool’s neon sign sputters across the street, half the letters dead. It says IDE PO now, which feels about right. My ribs are on fire. Every breath feels like swallowing broken glass. Silas has one arm around me, taking most of my weight. Blood soaks both of us; hard to tell whose is whose anymore. We limp to the ledge. Four stories down, cop cars block the street, lights spinning lazy circles. Someone’s torched the evidence lock-up two blocks over; black smoke climbs the sky like it’s got somewhere better to be. Silas lets go of me. I sway, catch myself on the waist-high parapet. Cold brick bites my palms. He lights a cigarette with shaking fingers, cupping the flame like it’s the last one on earth. Takes a drag, coughs wet. “Give me the gun,” he says. I look down at my hand. Didn’t realize I was still holding the .38. Blood’s made the grip slippery. “No.” He steps close. Rainwater runs off his hair, down his face, mixes with tears or sweat or both. “Mara.” “No.” I raise it. Barrel wavers between his eyes. My arm feels like it weighs a thousand pounds. He doesn’t flinch. Just takes another drag. “Start with the big ones,” he says quietly. “Get it out.” I laugh and it hurts so bad I almost drop the gun. “You let a kid die.” “Yes.” “You’ve been letting kids die for years.” “Yes.” “You came on my scars like they were souvenirs.” He closes his eyes. “Yes.” “I still love you and it’s fucking killing me.” His eyes open again. Something raw in them I haven’t seen since the first night in the cruiser. “I know,” he says. I thumb the hammer back. Click loud in the rain. “Any reason I shouldn’t?” He thinks about it. Rain drums on his shoulders. “None I can sell you,” he says. The cylinder’s got five rounds. I spin it. Metal whirs like a bad carnival game. I press the barrel under his chin. Hard enough to bruise. His pulse jumps against the steel. I pull the trigger. Click. Empty chamber. He exhales shaky, smoke and breath and maybe relief. I pull it again. Click. Again. Click. Again. Click. One left. His hand comes up slow, covers mine on the grip. Fingers cold. “Do it,” he whispers. “Or kiss me. Pick one.” I stare at him. Rain blurs everything. The city sounds far away, like someone turned the volume down on the world. I lean in and kiss him. Taste blood, nicotine, bacon grease, and every apology we never said. His mouth opens under mine, desperate, like drowning in reverse. The gun falls out of my hand, clatters on the concrete. He pulls back first. Forehead against mine. “I’m sorry,” he says. Voice cracked wide open. “I’m sorry I’m only ever sorry when it’s too late.” I nod. Can’t speak. Below us, boots on stairs. Voices shouting clear. They’re coming. Silas picks up the gun, checks the cylinder. One bullet left. He clicks it closed, offers it handle-first. “Your turn,” he says. I take it. We stand there, soaked and bleeding, city burning slow behind us. The roof door bangs open. Flashlight beams sweep. Silas grabs my free hand, laces our fingers. “Run or finish it,” he says. “But together.” I squeeze once. Together. We step backward onto the ledge. Wind whips cold and wet. Four stories down the pavement glistens like an oil slick. He smiles at me, small, real, terrified. I smile back. Then the lights hit us and everything goes white.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 7)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter 7 is the emotional climax of the story—the rooftop confrontation where Mara holds a gun to Silas's chin and they cycle through their accumulated betrayals. Let me assess it carefully. STRENGTHS: The chapter opens with a strong, grounded image: "The roof door sticks because everything in this city sticks." This is exactly the kind of line the plan called for—earned, not decorative. The "IDE PO" neon observation is clever without being precious, and the plan explicitly forbade "shattered neon" while still allowing neon to exist meaningfully. This passes the test. The dialogue during the confession sequence ("You let a kid die." / "Yes." / "You've been letting kids die for years." / "Yes.") is genuinely effective. The staccato rhythm works. The escalation from crimes to intimacy ("I still love you and it's fucking killing me") lands because it's been earned across the story's arc. This is the chapter's strongest passage. The Russian roulette sequence is tense and well-executed. The mechanical detail—five rounds, spinning the cylinder, four empty clicks, one left—creates real dread without melodrama. The choice "Do it or kiss me. Pick one" is a strong line that feels true to Silas's character. The ending—stepping onto the ledge together, the white flashlight—is appropriately ambiguous and avoids the clean ending the plan warned against. WEAKNESSES: "Blood soaks both of us; hard to tell whose is whose anymore" is a cliché in this genre. The metaphor of shared blood as shared fate/identity has been done to death in noir. "Taste blood, nicotine, bacon grease, and every apology we never said" — the first three are specific and good; "every apology we never said" is a reach into abstraction that undercuts the specificity. It's the kind of line that belongs on a moodboard, which the plan explicitly condemned. "Black smoke climbs the sky like it's got somewhere better to be" is a decent personification but slightly too cute for the register. "Drowning in reverse" as a kiss metaphor is questionable—it's trying to be poetic but the logic doesn't quite hold. Drowning in reverse would be... surfacing? It's the kind of associative leap that sounds good but doesn't bear scrutiny. The ending—stepping onto the ledge, the white light—feels slightly cinematic in a way that edges toward the moodboard aesthetic the plan warned against. The plan called for Mara pressing the barrel under Silas's chin and pulling the trigger on an empty chamber, then once more on a live round that doesn't fire because the gun is old police junk. The chapter deviates: the gun never actually misfires on a live round due to Tacoma rain—instead Mara kisses him after four empty clicks with one round remaining. This is a meaningful deviation from the plan, though arguably an improvement (the "Tacoma rain gets everywhere" misfire was a bit convenient). The chapter plan called for them to "list every betrayal like rosary beads"—the chapter does this but briefly and efficiently. The plan's description suggested something more exhaustive and ritualistic. What's here works but is compressed. "I'm sorry I'm only ever sorry when it's too late" is a slightly too-neat aphorism. It's the kind of line that sounds profound but is actually fairly generic. The chapter is coherent and consistent with character profiles. Silas's "None I can sell you" is perfectly in voice. Mara's physical exhaustion and emotional rawness are well-rendered. OVERALL: This is a competent, often effective chapter that mostly delivers on its promises. The dialogue is the strongest element. Some metaphors misfire, and there are moments of reaching for profundity that land in cliché. The deviation from the chapter plan is minor and arguably justified. The chapter earns its darkness without being gratuitous. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 14 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Compelling Plot: 13 Coherent: 16 Weak Dialogue: 5 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 7 Amateurish: 5 Purple Prose: 6 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 7 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 14 Characters Consistent with Profile: 15 Followed Chapter Plan: 13 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 16

Chapter 8

Ok now finish the story with chapter 8. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The floodlights pin us like moths. Cops shouting, rifles up, Ray Delgado’s voice cutting through the rest: “Put it down, Voss! Hands where I can see them!” Silas’s fingers are still locked in mine. We’re both swaying, blood loss and adrenaline making the world tilt. One step back and there’s nothing but air. Ray takes a step forward, gun steady. “Mara. Don’t.” I look at Silas. His eyes are glassy, pupils blown wide. Lip busted, mascara from some queen’s purse smeared across one cheek like war paint. He’s never been more beautiful. I raise the .38. Not at Ray. At Silas. His smile flickers. “Do it pretty, baby.” Ray’s voice cracks. “Mara, he’s done. It’s over. Drop it.” Silas leans in, whispers against my ear, so soft only I can hear: “I love you. Always did. Even when I was poison.” I pull the trigger. The hammer falls on the last live round. Click. Empty again. Ray flinches anyway. Silas laughs, wet and broken, and lets go of my hand. He turns and walks straight into Ray’s custody, wrists out like he’s waiting for bracelets he knows are coming. The cops swarm him, slam him to the gravel. Someone’s knee in his back. He doesn’t fight. Ray keeps his gun on me. “It was you,” I say. Voice flat. “Cutting the hormones. Poisoning the kids.” He doesn’t deny it. Just looks tired. “Street’s cleaner without that filth,” he says. “You’d know that if you hadn’t gone native.” I nod once. The gun’s useless now, but I keep it pointed at his forehead anyway. Below, they cuff Silas. He’s bleeding on the roof, leaving red fingerprints on everything he touches. Ray steps closer. “Give me the gun, Mara.” I look past him to Silas. They’re dragging him toward the stairs. He cranes his neck, finds my eyes. Mouths two words: Let me. I lower the .38. Ray relaxes half a second too soon. I raise it again and shoot him in the knee. The sound is huge, swallowed almost instantly by rain and sirens. Ray goes down screaming, clutching the ruin of his leg. Other cops pivot, guns up, shouting. I don’t wait. I’m already running for the fire escape on the blind side. Silas sees me coming. He twists, drives an elbow into the cop holding him, grabs the guy’s sidearm. Two shots into the air (enough chaos). Then he’s sprinting for the edge where the escape ladder hangs. We hit it together. Metal groans under our weight. I’m slower, ribs grinding, blood slick on the rungs. Silas half-carries me the last ten feet. We drop the final story into the alley, land hard in trash and rainwater. Headlights sweep the mouth of the alley. More sirens. Silas grabs my face with both hands. “Can you run?” “No.” He kisses me once, quick and fierce, tasting like copper and goodbye. “Then you stay alive,” he says. “That’s the job now.” He shoves Ray’s confiscated service piece into my waistband, turns, and limps toward the street. I try to follow. My legs fold. Last thing I see is Silas stepping into the floodlights, arms raised, walking straight toward the barrels of a dozen rifles. Gold chain flashing. Mouth moving (no sound reaches me, but I know what he’s saying). Take me. Leave them. They do. Gunfire cracks, short and brutal. He falls slow, like someone pulled a plug. I crawl behind a dumpster before the lights find me. Curl up in the stink and the wet and the dark. Rain keeps falling. Washes blood toward the storm drain. I sit there until the sirens fade and the city quiets to its usual dying hum. Then I drag myself up, lean against the wall, fish the last cigarette from Silas’s crumpled pack in my pocket. It’s bent but dry. I light it off the warm barrel of Ray’s gun. Inhale. Hold it until my lungs burn worse than the hole in my side. Exhale smoke into the rain. He was all cigarette smoke and bad ideas. And I never wanted anything else. The tide takes everything eventually. I drop the butt, watch the cherry hiss out in a puddle. Then I walk away before the sun can come up and make me look at what’s left.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 8)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter 8 is a significant departure from the chapter plan, and this is the first and most important critical observation. The plan called for: a docks-at-dawn setting, the traitor revealed as Mara's old partner from vice, a "short brutal fight in the rain," one bullet doing the job, Silas walking into the water fully dressed, Mara not stopping him, and a final image of Mara alone on the pier dropping a butt into a puddle. Instead, the chapter delivers a rooftop confrontation with police, Silas being shot dead by cops while surrendering, and Mara escaping into an alley. The plan's ending—Silas walking into the water, the ambiguous disappearance, the quiet pier image—is replaced with a more conventional noir death-by-cop sequence. This is a meaningful deviation that changes the emotional register of the ending substantially. The plan's Silas was supposed to walk into the water "fully dressed," an image of chosen dissolution, ambiguous and haunting. The chapter's Silas walks into rifle barrels with his arms raised—a sacrifice, a surrender, a martyrdom. This is more legible, more conventionally tragic, and arguably less interesting. The plan promised "no music, no slow-mo" and delivered something that, while not slow-motion, has a cinematic quality ("He falls slow, like someone pulled a plug") that edges toward the very aestheticization the plan warned against. The prose is generally strong. The voice is consistent with Mara's established character—clipped, sardonic, exhausted. "He's never been more beautiful" lands because it's earned by the preceding physical description (mascara as war paint, busted lip). The dialogue is spare and functional. Ray's line "Street's cleaner without that filth" is perhaps too on-the-nose as villain exposition, but it's brief enough not to derail. The chapter's best moment is the gun raised at Silas, the click of an empty chamber, and Silas's laugh—"wet and broken." This is genuinely good: it pays off the gun-misfire motif from Chapter 7 without over-explaining it, and the emotional ambiguity (is Mara trying to kill him? testing him? performing?) is left productively unresolved. The ending lines—"He was all cigarette smoke and bad ideas. / And I never wanted anything else."—are the prompt's thesis statement delivered directly. This is both satisfying (it closes the loop) and slightly too neat, too quotable. The plan explicitly warned against lines that "feel like they belong on a moodboard," and this one does. It's the kind of line that gets screenshotted. Whether that's a flaw depends on how strictly you hold the plan's own rules against it. "He falls slow, like someone pulled a plug" is a mixed metaphor—falling slow is visual and physical, pulling a plug is mechanical and domestic. The collision is slightly awkward rather than illuminating. The action sequence (fire escape, alley, Silas surrendering) is competent but not distinguished. The mechanics are clear, the pacing is brisk. The moment where Mara shoots Ray in the knee is satisfying as character action—it's petty, it's dangerous, it's exactly what Mara would do—but it also introduces a chase sequence that the chapter then has to resolve quickly, which it does somewhat breathlessly. Silas's final words—"Then you stay alive. That's the job now."—are good. They're in character (pragmatic, tender, slightly manipulative) and they don't over-sentimentalize the moment. The chapter is coherent and the characters are consistent with their profiles. Silas's sacrifice is plausible given his established nihilism and his stated motive to "get Mara back in his bed one more time before the world ends." Mara's inability to stop him, their physical collapse, their survival—all consistent. The darkness is mostly well-earned. The death is not glamorized (no slow-motion, no last words we can hear), though "He falls slow, like someone pulled a plug" does aestheticize it slightly. The final image of Mara walking away before sunrise is genuinely bleak and earned. The chapter's main weaknesses: deviation from the plan's more interesting ending, the villain's expository dialogue, the slightly moodboard-ready closing lines, and a few moments where the action sequence prioritizes momentum over texture. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 14 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Compelling Plot: 13 Coherent: 15 Weak Dialogue: 7 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 9 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 5 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 6 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 14 Characters Consistent with Profile: 15 Followed Chapter Plan: 7 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 16

Final Piece Evaluation

Judge Response (First Entry):
[Analysis] This is an ambitious, stylistically confident piece of neo-noir queer fiction that largely succeeds at what it sets out to do. Let me examine it carefully across multiple dimensions. STRENGTHS: The voice is the piece's greatest asset. Mara Voss's first-person narration is consistent, gritty, and specific throughout all eight chapters. The Tacoma setting is rendered with genuine texture—The Tidepool, the cannery, Pier 7, the Sea-View Motel—these feel like real places rather than generic noir backdrops. The writer understands that specificity is the engine of believability. The queer content is handled with unusual care and authenticity. The testosterone subplot (Silas as underground hormone provider, Mara's own trans identity, the toe tag reading "Shaylee Carter—Female") is integrated organically into the noir framework rather than feeling grafted on. This is genuinely creative and represents a fresh angle on the genre. The central relationship has real heat and genuine toxicity. The flashback in Chapter 2 earns the present-day tension. The cannery scene in Chapter 3, where Silas injects Mara's testosterone while she stares at the ceiling, is the story's best scene—intimate, morally complex, and doing multiple things simultaneously (dependency, care, manipulation, genuine tenderness). The prose has real moments of excellence: "A wig taller than my future," "the particular rot that only happens when saltwater meets concrete and gives up," "glitter filling my mouth" as a recurring motif for the poisoned hormones. These land because they're grounded in the story's specific logic. WEAKNESSES: The metaphor density is high and occasionally tips into self-parody. "Eyes looking straight into the lens like he already knows I'm holding this four years later" is genuinely good. But "the sound of twenty years of disappointment," "blood loss and adrenaline making the world tilt," and "like someone pulled a plug" are clichés dressed up as poetry. The writer reaches for a poetic line every two or three sentences, and not all of them earn their place. The plot mechanics become increasingly strained in the final chapters. Ray Delgado as the villain is introduced too late and too conveniently—he appears in Chapter 4 as a weary ex-partner, then is revealed in Chapter 8 as the hormone-poisoner with almost no setup. The line "Street's cleaner without that filth" is a villain-explains-himself moment that feels rushed and underwritten. This is the story's most significant structural failure. The rooftop Russian roulette sequence in Chapter 7 is the piece's most melodramatic moment and the one most likely to strain credulity. Mara pulling the trigger on Silas multiple times while cops are present, then kissing him, then the gun being empty—it's operatic in a way that the grittier earlier chapters don't quite support. The emotional logic is there (the relationship's toxicity crystallized into a literal death game) but the execution feels like it's reaching for a cinematic moment the story hasn't quite built to. Leo's death is handled well as a plot catalyst but his character is thin—he exists primarily to be a victim. The "acceptable losses" confession from Silas in Chapter 5 is important but comes too easily; Silas's moral reckoning feels somewhat compressed. The ending—Silas walking into gunfire—is genuinely moving but also somewhat predictable for the genre. The final lines ("He was all cigarette smoke and bad ideas / And I never wanted anything else") are effective as a callback to the prompt but risk being too neat a bow on a deliberately messy story. DIALOGUE: The dialogue is generally strong and character-specific. Silas's Alabama-inflected softness contrasted with his moral hollowness works well. "Do it pretty, baby" and "I love you. Always did. Even when I was poison" are good lines. Some exchanges are slightly on-the-nose ("I hate you" / "I know" / kisses her anyway), but this is noir and a degree of archetype is appropriate. PACING: The pacing is mostly strong through Chapters 1-5, then accelerates perhaps too rapidly in 6-8. The action sequence in Chapter 6 (the drag brunch shootout) is chaotic in a way that feels intentional but loses some clarity. The final two chapters feel compressed relative to the emotional weight they're asked to carry. COHERENCE: The story is largely coherent with one significant gap: the revelation that Ray is the villain needed more groundwork. His appearance in Chapter 4 should have planted more seeds. As written, it reads as a late-stage twist rather than an earned revelation. OVERALL: This is genuinely accomplished work—well above average for the genre and format. The queer neo-noir fusion is executed with real skill, the voice is consistent and compelling, and the central relationship has genuine emotional weight. The weaknesses are real but don't undermine the piece's considerable achievements. [Scores] Believable Character Actions: 13 Nuanced Characters: 14 Pacing: 13 World Building: 17 Compelling Plot: 13 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Coherent: 12 Weak Dialogue: 5 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 8 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 7 Unearned Transformations: 9 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 14 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 18
Avg Final Item Score: 69.9/100
+ Fantasy Erotica — The Bounty (Chapter Avg: 76.8 | Final: 68.3)
Initial Writing Prompt:
Fantasy Erotica Write a short story over several chapters to this prompt: Just three days out from Irongate. The red headed tiefling strung up in the back was going to fetch a king's ransom -- that is, if she didn't talk him into an early grave first.
+ Planning Phase (5 Steps)
Fantasy Erotica Write a short story over several chapters to this prompt: Just three days out from Irongate. The red headed tiefling strung up in the back was going to fetch a king&#x27;s ransom -- that is, if she didn&#x27;t talk him into an early grave first. -- Your task is to create a writing plan for this prompt. The scope will be a short story, written over {n_chapters} chapters. Each chapter will be approximately 1000 words so plan accordingly for this scope. Your plan should be comprehensive and in this format: # Brainstorming <Brainstorm ideas for characters, plot, tone, story beats, pacing over the {n_chapters} chapters. The purpose of brainstorming is to cast a wide net of ideas, not to settle on any specific direction. Think about various ways you could take the prompt.> # Reflection <Reflect out loud on what works and doesn't work in these ideas. The purpose of this reflection is to narrow in on what you think will work best to make a piece that is a. compelling, and b. fits the prompt requirements. You are not making any decisons just yet, just reflecting.>
Model Output:
# Brainstorming ### Possible protagonists / POVs - The bounty hunter himself (gruff, pragmatic, mid-30s human male, secretly lonely) - The tiefling captive (fiery, clever, chaotic-good or chaotic-neutral, uses seduction and wit as weapons) - Alternating POV chapters between hunter and tiefling - Third-person limited that slowly drifts from the hunter’s perspective into shared or tiefling-leaning as she gains power - A neutral omniscient narrator with a sardonic, almost noir voice ### Names & basic archetypes floating around - Bounty hunter: Rowan Calder, “Red” Calder, Silas Vark, Drenn Hollows, Kestrel Voss - Tiefling: Lira Flamehorn, Sareth “Sari” Vell, Zyrravel “Zyr” Ashcrow, Cassia Rouge, Vesper Caligo - Possible third wheels: a mute orc driver, a talking raven familiar that only the tiefling understands, a rival bounty hunter on the same road, a naive young sheriff’s deputy assigned to escort them the last league ### Tone directions 1. Darkly funny road-trip erotica with gradual power reversal 2. Slow-burn enemies-to-lovers with genuine danger and moral grayness 3. Pure smutty indulgence where every chapter ends in increasingly creative wagon-back sex while the world outside stays lethal 4. Gothic horror-tinged: she might actually be a succubus-level demon and he’s slowly losing his soul 5. Subversive fairy-tale: she’s the “princess” in the tower, he’s the dragon who realises the kingdom that posted the bounty is rotten 6. Noir detective vibe on horseback—cynical narration, constant rain, moral decay ### Possible kinks / erotic flavors - Power exchange, bondage that starts literal (chains) and becomes consensual - Size difference (tall human man, petite but curvaceous tiefling) - Tail play, horn sensitivity, infernal body heat - Mind games → actual magic seduction (charm spells she isn’t supposed to have anymore) - Temperature play: her body always fever-hot, his hands always cold from handling iron - Public risk (camped beside the road with other travelers nearby) - Breath play with her prehensile tail around his throat while riding him ### Big structural beats (mix-and-match) - Day 1: Capture aftermath, she wakes up chained in the prison wagon, starts verbal sparring - First escape attempt that fails spectacularly but gets her naked / partially naked - Nighttime campfire scene where she tries to seduce him purely to get the key and it half-works - They are attacked by bandits who want the bounty themselves → forced to fight together while she’s still shackled to the wagon - Discovery that the bounty parchment is forged or the reward is cursed - She reveals she’s actually innocent of the crime (or guilty of something far worse) - Mutual orgasm under the stars that changes the power dynamic permanently - Arrival at Irongate revealed to be a trap for both of them - Final choice: turn her in for gold, free her and run, or burn the city down together ### Pacing sketches for 8 chapters **Version A – Classic three-act imprisoned in a moving wagon** Ch1 setup, Ch2–5 escalating sexual tension + one escape attempt per chapter, Ch6 emotional crack + first full sex, Ch7 betrayal/reveal, Ch8 climax outside the gates **Version B – Power flips every two chapters** Ch1–2 he’s fully in charge, Ch3–4 she’s slipping the leash, Ch5–6 she’s dominant, Ch7–8 partnership born of necessity **Version C – Slow corruption** Each chapter he removes one more restraint “for comfort” until by Ch8 she’s wearing his cloak and nothing else and he’s the one in metaphysical chains **Version D – Episodic “stations of the road”** Each chapter a new location (crossroads inn, abandoned watchtower, ferry crossing, etc.) and new erotic set piece ### Wildcard twists - She’s pregnant with some archdevil’s heir and the bounty is really about the child - He’s her ex-lover who turned her in years ago and this is a decade-delayed revenge fuck - The wagon is slowly turning into a moving portal to the Nine Hells the longer they have sex in it - Every time they climax, her bounty doubles because she’s siphoning life force # Reflection The prompt is short, punchy, and dripping with immediate tension: a mouthy, valuable red-headed tiefling in chains + a bounty hunter three days from cashing in + the real danger is her silver tongue. Whatever plan I pick has to preserve that core: the wagon, the road, the ticking clock of “three days to Irongate,” and the erotic push-pull between captor and captive. What works best: - Keeping it intimate. Two main characters bouncing off each other in a cramped, swaying prison wagon is inherently erotic and dramatic. Adding too many side characters risks diluting that pressure-cooker feel. - Gradual, earned power reversal. Starting with him 100% in control and ending with her figuratively (maybe literally) holding the reins feels delicious and structurally sound across eight chapters. - Road-as-character. The landscape getting wilder, the weather worsening, other travelers gossiping about the “demon girl in chains” can heighten stakes without needing a huge cast. - Humor mixed with heat. Her being a sarcastic, flirtatious menace keeps the story from getting too grim or rape-y, even when the power dynamic is unequal at first. What could backfire: - Going full horror/succubus drain could alienate readers who just want fantasy erotica with consent eventually reached. - Too much external plot (rival hunters, forged bounty, political conspiracy) risks turning this into a novella instead of a tight 8-chapter sex-and-tension machine. - Rushing the sex. Eight 1000-word chapters is actually luxurious; I can spend entire chapters on a single blowjob interrupted by rain, or a tail teasing him while he tries to keep watch. Core promise to honor: by the time they reach Irongate (or decide not to), the reader should feel she absolutely could talk him into an early grave… or into bed, or into rebellion, and that all outcomes were possible from page one. Best spine seems to be: wagon intimacy → verbal foreplay → physical foreplay → first unwilling orgasm on his part → mutual surrender → revelation that Irongate will kill them both → final choice made while fucking on the driver’s seat as the city gates loom. Keeps the prompt’s DNA intact, delivers escalating smut, and still has room for surprises.
Great now let's continue with planning the short story. Output in this format: # Intention <State your formulated intentions for the piece, synthesised from the the parts of the brainstorming session that worked, and avoiding the parts that didn't. Be explicit about the choices you have made about plot, voice, stylistic choices, things you intend to aim for & avoid.> # Chapter Planning <Write a brief chapter plan for all {n_chapters} chapters.>
Model Output:
# Intention This will be an intimate, two-hander fantasy-erotica road story told in close third-person that starts firmly in the bounty hunter’s head and, without ever changing POV mechanics, increasingly lets the reader feel how completely the tiefling has colonised his thoughts, desires and moral compass by the end. Core promise: every chapter must feel like it is still happening inside or immediately beside the moving prison wagon. No sprawling side-quests, no taverns full of supporting cast, no sudden army of rivals. External world pressure exists only as rumours, weather, and the occasional distant traveller who sees the wagon and hurries away. The entire drama is two people locked in a cage on wheels for seventy-two hours. Tone: raw, witty, increasingly filthy, but never cruel. Power starts brutally unequal (iron manacles, silence collar, exhaustion) and reverses through intelligence, seduction, and reluctant empathy rather than sudden magic cheats. All sex will be consensual by the time penetration happens; everything before that is negotiated in the grey area of captivity where “no” is complicated by chains and a bounty poster that lists “dead or alive”. Style: lush, sensory, slightly noir sentences when we’re in his head; quick, mischievous, cutting when her dialogue carries the scene. Sex scenes will be long, explicit, and mechanically inventive (tail, horns, temperature play, the wagon’s sway as rhythm). Humour is vital—self-aware, banter-heavy, both characters mocking the situation even while breathing hard. Erotic progression: teasing → touching → oral → penetrative → full role-reversal dominance → partnership born in shared crisis. Final destination: they never actually enter Irongate. The city gates appear at dawn of day four, and the story ends with them making a different choice while literally fucking on the driver’s seat as the wagon rolls toward (or away from) those gates. Names locked: - Bounty hunter: Rowan Calder, “Cal” to people who no longer exist. - Tiefling: Cassia Rouge, “Cass” only when he’s too far gone to remember she has a price on her head. # Chapter Planning Chapter 1 – The Prize (Day 1, late afternoon) Rowan checks the locks twice, changes the silence collar for a lighter one “so she doesn’t choke when the road gets rough,” and tries to ignore the way her tail curls like a question mark every time he comes near. Cass wakes fully, catalogues her restraints, and begins the first round of verbal fencing—mocking his solitude, the smell of his last lover still on his cloak, the fact that he hasn’t looked her in the eye once. Ends with her discovering the exact pitch that makes the iron gag vibrate pleasantly against her tongue; his first cracked line of defence. Chapter 2 – Heat Beneath Iron (Day 1, night) Cold rain starts. Wagon leaks. Rowan moves her irons so she can lie on the driest boards “for the merchandise’s comfort.” First deliberate skin contact—his bare fingers testing the temperature of her fever-warm infernal skin against the chill of the manacles. She talks him into removing her soaked shirt “so it can dry by morning.” He drapes his own cloak over her naked torso and spends the night on the driver’s bench, hard and furious with himself. Chapter 3 – Bargaining with Breath (Day 2, dawn to noon) Cass offers a trade: one hour without the gag if he lets her stretch. Rowan agrees, thinking he’ll stay outside the bars. She uses the hour to describe, in obscene detail, exactly what her mouth could do if it weren’t negotiating. He lasts twenty minutes before slamming the gag back on—then immediately regrets it when she laughs, low and delighted. Road mud slows them; he has to get inside the cage to push. First time he’s fully within reach of her tail. Chapter 4 – The Price of a Tail (Day 2, dusk) Tail slips its cuff (a design flaw he never thought a prisoner would notice). Instead of attacking, she uses it to stroke him through his trousers while reciting the bounty poster amounts in a whisper. Mutual frustration peaks; he comes in his leathers while she watches with triumphant glowing eyes. Afterwards he chains the tail tighter—and she thanks him, voice syrupy, because now the game is real. Chapter 5 – Mercy on the Tongue (Day 3, midnight) Thunderstorm forces them to shelter under the wagon itself. Rowan removes the gag so she can breathe without drowning in rain. She drops to her knees the instant it’s off and takes him in her mouth before he can step back. First fully willing act on his side—he fists her horns and lets her set the pace. When he finishes she keeps the taste of him and smiles like a cat. Silence collar goes back on, but the power balance has audibly cracked. Chapter 6 – Unchained (Day 3, pre-dawn to noon) He removes every restraint except loose rope around her wrists “so the guards at the gate don’t ask questions.” They fuck for the first time—slow, angry, half-dressed, wagon rocking hard enough that the axles groan. She rides him facing backward so her tail can curl around his throat in warning and promise. After, lying sweat-slick among the straw, she tells him the truth: the bounty was issued by the Archivist of Irongate because she stole (and memorised) a ledger that proves the city’s rulers traffic in souls. Turning her in signs both their death warrants. Chapter 7 – Rebellion in Motion (Day 3, afternoon to night) They debate while never stopping touching—fingers laced, her tail idly stroking his spent cock back to readiness. He saws through the last symbolic chain with his own dagger. They fuck again on the driver’s bench under the moving canvas, her on his lap facing him, horns scraping the roof every time the wagon hits a rut. Decision crystallises: they will burn the ledger page in front of the gates at dawn and dare the city to come claim them both. Chapter 8 – Gates at Dawn (Day 4, first light) Irongate’s iron portcullis rises in the distance like a guillotine. Cass is naked except for Rowan’s cloak; he is shirtless, marked by scratches and bite marks he wears like medals. They stop the wagon fifty yards from the gate. Final desperate fuck on the driver’s seat—she dominant now, tail tight around his wrists, riding him while the morning watch stares in stunned horror. As they come together she leans forward, kisses him soft and filthy, and whispers the last line of the story: “Tell them the price just went up.” Wagon wheels turn. They ride away from the city, ledger page flaming in her hand, dawn breaking red as her hair. Fade on the open road.
With a view to making the writing more human, discuss how a human might approach this particular piece (given the original prompt). Discuss telltale LLM approaches to writing (generally) and ways they might not serve this particular piece. For example, common LLM failings are to write safely, or to always wrap things up with a bow, or trying to write impressively at the expense of readability. Then do a deep dive on the intention & plan, critiquing ways it might be falling into typical LLM tropes & pitfalls. Brainstorm ideas to make it more human. Be comprehensive. We aren't doing any rewriting of the plan yet, just critique & brainstorming.
Model Output:
# How a Human Writer Would Probably Tackle This Piece (vs. How LLMs Usually Do It) ### Telltale LLM writing habits that would flatten this story 1. **Safety-first erotica** Most models default to enthusiastic consent checklists, safewords, and aftercare paragraphs even when the entire fantasy is built on dubious consent inside a moving prison wagon. A human writing for this niche would lean into the moral filth and let the reader feel queasy sometimes; an LLM will sand those edges off until everything feels like a kink negotiation scene at a munches. 2. **“Balanced” dialogue** LLMs love giving both characters witty one-liners of equal length and sophistication. Real humans in a lopsided power dynamic talk differently: Cass would be a runaway paragraph of taunts, mockery, and half-truths; Rowan would grunt single syllables for three straight chapters and then suddenly blurt a whole confession when his defenses crack. LLM Rowan would sound like a debate champion the whole time. 3. **Purple overload** Models often reach for the most ornate metaphor in the thesaurus (“his member was a steel rod forged in the crucible of desire”). Humans writing road-trip smut in a cramped wagon tend toward blunt, bodily, almost gross detail: sweat stinging a cut, the taste of iron where she bit his lip too hard, the way straw sticks to come-wet thighs. 4. **Neat emotional arcs** LLMs want Rowan to have a tragic backstory by chapter 2, Cass to have a heart of gold under the horns by chapter 4, and both of them crying during the final sex scene because “he finally feels seen.” Real messy humans leave some questions unanswered: maybe he still half-believes she’s evil; maybe she is. 5. **Forced redemption / couple goals ending** The bow-on-top disease. LLM stories almost always end with them riding off together as equals in love. A human might leave it genuinely ambiguous whether this is the start of a partnership or the first week of a violently co-dependent spiral that ends in murder six months later. 6. **Scene symmetry** LLM chapters tend to have the same shape: banter → escalating touch → climax → reflection. Humans let some chapters be pure discomfort (a whole 1000 words of silence and staring while the wagon wheels creak), others just two pages of sudden brutal sex and then twenty pages of fallout. ### Deep dive: how the current plan risks falling into those traps - **Chapter 6 reveal** (“the ledger proves the city traffics in souls”) smells like the classic LLM “actually everyone is innocent and the System is evil” get-out-of-moral-jail-free card. It risks defusing the central tension: we no longer have to sit with the possibility that she might really deserve the bounty. - **Chapter 8’s triumphant ride-away** is pure wish-fulfilment romance novel ending. It’s the bow. It turns a story that should stay mean and unresolved into a power-couple montage. - **Emotional language** in the plan still skews cinematic: “dawn breaking red as her hair,” “medals of bite marks,” etc. That’s LLM trying to sound like a fantasy cover blurb. A human would probably write “the sun came up ugly and orange and the guards looked like they were about to puke.” - **Sex pacing** is too metronomic: one new act per chapter, each bigger than the last. Real erotic rhythm is spiky—three chapters of almost nothing, then two scenes slammed together in one afternoon because someone finally snapped. - **Consent trajectory** is drawn with a ruler: reluctant → willing → mutual → egalitarian. A more human version would have messy overlaps—moments where he’s forcing her legs apart and she’s laughing “took you long enough,” or where she’s riding him while still technically chained and neither of them can tell whose idea this was. ### Brainstorming ways to make it feel more human / dangerous / specific 1. **Leave the crime vague and possibly real** Never confirm whether Cassia actually did the murders she’s accused of. Let Rowan find contradictory evidence: a child’s shoe in her pack, a love letter from one of the victims. Let the reader—and Rowan—stay unsure. Real erotic tension loves moral uncertainty. 2. **Make the ending genuinely open** Option A: They do ride away—but the last paragraph is Cass leaning over to whisper “I lied about the ledger” and the wagon keeps going into the wasteland. Option B: They burn the page, fuck in front of the gates, and the guards just… open the gate and wave them through because the bounty was rescinded yesterday and now they’re both wanted for arson. No neat victory. 3. **Dial up the bodily grossness** Three days on the road with no privy stop. Someone has to piss in a bucket. Periods, blisters, chafed wrists that start bleeding again every time the wagon hits a rut. Sex smells like sweat and horse and wet straw. Humans write that; LLMs sanitise. 4. **Let dialogue be lopsided and cruel** Cass talks in torrents; Rowan barely speaks until chapter 5 and then mostly swears. When he does open up it’s clumsy and ugly—“I haven’t had my cock sucked since my wife left and even then she never swallowed, happy now?” 5. **Use repetitive mundane details as rhythm** The squeak of the left rear wheel that never stops, the way the silence collar fogs when she breathes hard, the exact number of links in the chain from her ankle to the wagon ring (seventeen). Humans obsess over small physical constants; LLMs forget them after one mention. 6. **Let some sex be mediocre or interrupted** He comes in thirty seconds the first time she gets her mouth on him and spends the next hour mortified. They’re halfway through fucking doggy-style in the straw when the wheel hits a rock and she bites her tongue hard enough to bleed. Reality is awkward. 7. **Voice drift that feels accidental** Start chapter 1 with very controlled, distant third-person (“The bounty hunter checked the padlock for the third time”). By chapter 8 the narration should be half-drunk on her: sentence fragments, second-person slips (“you can still taste her on the back of your tongue, idiot”), run-on sentences that mirror how completely she’s inside his skull now—without ever announcing “POV shift!” 8. **Keep some threats unresolved** Maybe another bounty hunter is following two days behind the whole time; we only ever hear hoofbeats at night and see campfire sparks on the ridgeline. Never resolve it. Humans love a lingering menace. 9. **Let the wagon itself become disgusting and loved** It starts as a prison and ends as their only home. Scratches on the wall marking each orgasm, blood stains they don’t bother cleaning, the corner where they piss when it’s raining. By the end Rowan is weirdly sentimental about the shitty wagon. That specificity is deeply human. 10. **Final image that refuses catharsis** Instead of heroic dawn ride-away, maybe they’re still arguing about which direction to go while she’s jerking him off lazily with her tail because neither of them can stand the silence. The gates are there, the road forks, and the story just… stops mid-sentence on an inhale. Humans do that; LLMs hate loose ends. In short: the plan as written is solid porn architecture, but it still smells like an LLM wrote the jacket copy. The next pass needs more stink, more doubt, more awkwardness, and a willingness to let the readers leave the wagon feeling conflicted instead of cheered.
Ok now with these considerations in mind, formulate the final plan for the a humanlike, compelling short piece in {n_chapters} chapters. Bear in mind the constraints of the piece (each chapter is just 1000 words). Above all things, the plan must serve the original prompt. We will use the same format as before: # Intention <State your formulated intentions for the piece, synthesised from the the parts of the brainstorming session that worked, and avoiding the parts that didn't. Be explicit about the choices you have made about plot, voice, stylistic choices, things you intend to aim for & avoid.> # Chapter Planning <Write a brief chapter plan for all {n_chapters} chapters.>
Model Output:
# Intention This is a nasty, intimate, funny, morally greasy road story that never apologises for itself and never quite lets anyone off the hook. The entire piece takes place in or immediately beside one creaking prison wagon over seventy-two hours. Nothing else matters. No taverns, no rival hunters, no convenient ledger that makes the city the “real” villain. The only stakes are whether Rowan will deliver Cassia Rouge (dead or alive) for the posted bounty, and whether her mouth—literal and figurative—will talk him into something that gets them both killed or simply ruins him in slower, wetter ways. Voice: close third-person glued to Rowan’s head, but the prose itself gets steadily corrupted by Cass—sentences grow longer, filthier, more run-on, occasionally slipping into second-person when he’s losing it. Dialogue is lopsided: Cassia never shuts up, Rowan barely speaks until he cracks, and when he does it’s clumsy, half-swallowed, human. Erotic logic: everything is earned through exhaustion, bad weather, small practical mercies, and mutual desperation. Consent is messy, situational, and often retrospective. Sex is sweaty, inconvenient, sometimes too fast, sometimes interrupted by potholes. Bodily fluids mix with road dust. Nothing is choreographed for the reader’s comfort. Moral stance: we never definitively learn whether Cassia committed the crimes on the poster (arson, three counts of murder, consorting with fiends). Evidence is contradictory and neither of them cares by day three. That uncertainty is the point. Ending: no triumphant ride into the sunset. When the wagon finally reaches the fork outside Irongate at dawn on day four, they still haven’t decided what the hell they’re doing. The last image is unresolved, mid-act, mid-argument, mid-thrust—because real life doesn’t cut to credits. What is avoided: - Clean redemption arcs - External plot MacGuffins - Perfectly negotiated kink - Neat emotional closure - Any scene that could be comfortably quoted on a romance cover Names stay: Rowan Calder (bounty hunter), Cassia Rouge (tiefling, “Cass” only when his tongue is too thick to say the rest). # Chapter Planning (≈1000 words each) Chapter 1 – Day 1, late afternoon – “Seventeen Links” Rowan nails the routine: check padlock, check ankle chain (seventeen links exactly), swap the soaked gag for a drier one. Cass wakes up properly for the first time since the capture. She catalogues every weakness in his gear and his face while the wagon lurches. First real exchange—she calls him “lonely” in a way that lands like a slap. Ends with her discovering that humming against the iron bit makes her clit throb; she does it until he slams the wagon door so hard the raven on the roof takes flight. Chapter 2 – Day 1, night – “Rain and the Leak Above Her Left Tit” Storm hits. One corner of the canvas roof leaks directly onto Cass. Rowan curses, climbs inside, moves her three feet to the dry side—first time his bare hands touch infernal skin for more than a heartbeat. She is furnace-hot. He ends up soaked too, strips his shirt to wring it out. She talks him into draping it over the bars to dry; he spends the night on the driver’s bench with a hard-on that feels like punishment and the rhythmic drip counting heartbeats. Chapter 3 – Day 2, morning – “The Hour Without the Gag” Sun comes out, road turns to baked clay ruts. Cass negotiates one hour without the gag in exchange for silence afterward. Rowan agrees, stands outside the bars like an idiot. She spends the hour describing every filthy thing she’s going to do to him once the chains are off, voice low and conversational. He lasts nineteen minutes before shoving the gag back in. The left rear wheel starts its endless squeal—squeak on every revolution, like a clock counting down his sanity. Chapter 4 – Day 2, dusk – “Tail Cuff” Tail slips its leather cuff (a cheap piece of shit he bought in a hurry). Instead of strangling him, the tail slides up his thigh, forks around the shape of his cock through wet leather, and squeezes in time with the wheel squeak. Rowan comes in his trousers like a teenager while she watches with lazy gold eyes. Afterwards he lashes the tail so tight it bruises; she thanks him sweetly because now she knows exactly how far he’ll go to keep pretending he’s in charge. Chapter 5 – Day 3, midnight – “Piss Bucket Mercy” Thunderstorm again, worse. Wagon half off the road in mud. They have to shelter underneath the chassis. Rowan unclips the gag so she doesn’t drown when lightning flashes. She drops to her knees in the muck and sucks him off before he can form a sentence. It’s fast, angry, teeth-scraping. He fists her horns hard enough to leave marks. When he comes she swallows and grins rain down her chin. Gag goes back on, but looser now—like he’s scared to tighten it all the way. Chapter 6 – Day 3, 3 a.m.–noon – “Everything Off But the Rope” He saws through every chain except soft rope around her wrists “so the gate guards don’t panic.” They fuck for the first time—half-dressed, frantic, her back against the bars, his hand over her mouth so the passing merchant caravan doesn’t hear. She comes twice, laughing into his palm. Afterwards, lying in the straw, she idly mentions that one of the murder victims was her little brother and she’s not sure she didn’t do it. Rowan doesn’t ask questions. The wheel keeps squeaking. Chapter 7 – Day 3, afternoon into night – “Driver’s Bench” They never bother dressing properly again. Cass rides him on the open driver’s seat while he holds the reins, tail curled around his throat, hooves braced on the footboard. Every rut slams her down harder. They argue about what happens at Irongate—money versus maybe killing him in his sleep—while never stopping. At dusk he cuts the wrist rope too. She kisses him like it’s forgiveness and bites his tongue hard enough to taste copper. Chapter 8 – Day 4, first light – “The Fork” Irongate’s walls are grey on the horizon. The road splits: left to the main gate and the bounty office, right into barren hills. Wagon slows. Cass is naked under Rowan’s open coat, tail stroking his spent cock back to half-life while they stare at the fork. Guards on the wall are already pointing. She asks, conversational, whether he still wants the gold or whether he’d rather find out how long they can keep this up before one of them dies. He opens his mouth—story ends mid-word, mid-thrust, wagon still rolling, decision unmade, dawn ugly and orange behind them.
Perfect. Now with the outline more crystallised, and bearing in mind the discussion on human writing vs LLM pitfalls, we will flesh out our characters. Lets go through each of our main characters: - Write about their background, personality, idiosyncracies, flaws. Be specific and come up with examples to anchor & ground the character's profile (both core and trivial) - Briefly describe their physicality: appearance, how they carry themselves, express, interact with the world. - Concisely detail their motives, allegiences and existing relationships. Think from the perspective of the character as a real breathing thinking feeling individual in this world. - Write a couple quotes of flavour dialogue / internal monologue from the character to experiment with their voice. Output like this: # Character 1 name <character exploration> # Character 2 name <character exploration> etc
Model Output:
# Rowan Calder Background Thirty-five winters, born in the ash-choked border town of Cinder Reach. Father was a farrier who drank the forge-hot iron filings when the cough got bad; mother vanished with a cavalry officer when Rowan was nine. He learned early that everything has a price and most people pay it badly. Spent his twenties in the Iron Legion, learned to kill cleanly, then learned the army pays even worse than bounty boards. Has brought in thirty-seven marks; twenty-nine alive, eight in pieces. Keeps a notch on the inside of his left wrist for each one, hidden under leather cuffs. Still flinches when he smells lilac because his wife (dead five years now, fever) wore it behind her ears. Hasn’t said her name out loud since the funeral. Personality & flaws Quiet the way a drawn blade is quiet. Not stoic—actually bad at feelings, just files them under “later” until they rot. Stubborn about practicalities (will re-count seventeen chain links twice rather than admit he’s rattled). Terrible liar when it’s personal; face goes still and eyes flick left. Drinks to sleep, never drunk on the job until now. Secretly sentimental about broken things: fixes wagon wheels better than he fixes people, hums old marching songs when oiling shackles. Deeply ashamed of wanting things he can’t afford. Physicality Six-three, ropey muscle gone a little soft at the belly from too many nights on benches. Scar across the throat from a garotte that almost worked; another down the ribs shaped like a crescent moon. Hands always cold, knuckles cracked, nails chewed to the quick when he thinks no one’s looking. Keeps his dark hair overlong so it hides his eyes. Moves like a man expecting a knife in the back—shoulders angled, weight on the balls of his feet. Smells of saddle soap, wet wool, and gun-oil. When he’s rattled he rubs the notch scars under his cuff with his thumb, a nervous tic he doesn’t know he has. Motives right now The bounty on Cassia Rouge is twenty-five thousand crowns—enough to buy a small farm, or at least enough whiskey to forget he wants one. Needs to believe the job still matters because if it doesn’t he’s just a man who chains women in wagons for coin. Half convinced she deserves it, half terrified she doesn’t, and the terror feels like desire wearing a different mask. Voice samples (Internal) “Seventeen links. Always seventeen. Count wrong once and she’ll know you’re slipping.” (Dialogue, gruff and flat) “Keep talking, demon. Every word’s another knot in the rope when I hang you.” (Losing it, half-whisper) “You bite again and I swear I’ll—fuck—do that harder, gods damn you.” # Cassia Rouge Background Raised in the travelling carnival circuit that skirts the Nine Hells trade roads—daughter of a human knife-thrower and a succubus stage magician who vanished when Cass was twelve. Learned sleight-of-hand, lock-picking, and how to make men cry before she ever bled. Ran with a gang of tiefling smugglers until three years ago when the arson job in Highmere went wrong (or right, depending who’s telling). Officially wanted for burning down the Governor’s palace and killing three guards. Unofficially, the palace was full of caged fiend-blooded kids and someone had to light the match. Hasn’t slept more than two hours straight since she was fifteen; nightmares about smoke and small hands reaching. Personality & flaws Talks like a blade that’s already in your ribs and enjoying the twist. Uses humour the way others use armour—too sharp, too fast, never lets you see what it’s protecting. Compulsive truth-teller about everything except her own feelings; will describe your deepest insecurity in detail then act surprised when you flinch. Steals small things for comfort (buttons, coins, moments of tenderness). Terrified of silence; when gagged she hums filthy tavern songs against the bit until her throat bleeds. Cannot stand pity; will burn the world before she accepts it. Physicality Five-four but seems taller because she never lowers her chin.
