She told herself she was angry. She was. She was furious that he had not come, that he had left her with a ledger full of ruin and a handful of crumbs and no word. She was furious that she cared. The house slept uneasily. Her father had taken to his bed with a bottle and a headache; the servants crept about like mice. Cresswell’s letters arrived twice daily now (thick, cream paper, seal the size of a sovereign), each one more proprietorial than the last. Eleanor burned them in the schoolroom fire and watched the wax bubble. On the seventh night the rain thinned to a mist. She heard the scrape first, then the soft curse in Yorkshire. Relief hit her so hard she nearly laughed aloud. Gabriel hauled himself over the rail dripping, coat black with water, hair stuck to his forehead. He looked half-drowned and wholly furious. “Tha’s daft,” he said before he was even properly inside. “Standing out here in nowt but thy shift. Tha’ll catch thy death and then I’ll have to carry thee, and I’m not in t’mood.” “I thought you were dead,” she snapped. “Or dismissed. Or both.” “Thought about it,” he said, shutting the casement with a shoulder. “Decided dying were less trouble.” He was limping worse; the bruise on his jaw had gone yellow-green. When he peeled off his coat it made a wet slap on the floorboards. Underneath his shirt clung to him, outlining every rib. Eleanor fetched the towel she had kept ready for three nights and thrust it at him. He took it, rubbed his face, then handed it back without thinking. She could smell the rain on him, and something sharper (fear, maybe). “The window stuck,” she said. “I noticed.” “I pried it with a knife.” “Tha’ll break the frame.” “I don’t care about the bloody frame.” They glared at each other, breathing hard. Then Gabriel laughed once, a short surprised bark, and the fight went out of them both. He sat heavily on the chair. Eleanor stayed standing, arms wrapped round herself to stop the shivering. “I can’t come every night,” he said at last. “Not with the weather. Ivy’s rotten in places. Nearly came down on my head twice.” “I’m not asking you to risk your neck for sport.” “No,” he said quietly. “I know what tha’s asking.” Silence again, softer this time. The candle had burned low; shadows jumped across the books and the unmade bed. He reached into his pocket and drew out a small twist of paper. Opened it: three sugared violets, damp but whole. “Bought ’em in the village,” he muttered. “Thought they’d be crushed. Didn’t want to come empty-handed.” Eleanor stared at the tiny purple things. Violets. Her mother’s funeral flowers. She felt her throat close. “You hate violets,” he said, reading her face. “I remember now. Damn it.” “No,” she managed. “I—thank you.” She took one, put it on her tongue. Sweet, cloying, perfect. She hated that it was perfect. Gabriel watched her, wary. “Teach me something, then.” “What?” “Anything. Latin. How to hold a fork proper. Owt that isn’t mucking out stables or dodging Sir Robert’s temper.” Eleanor almost smiled. “You want a lesson at two in the morning?” “I want,” he said, “to sit here a bit longer without feeling like a trespasser.” So she fetched volume three of Gibbon (because it was heaviest and would hurt if she needed to throw it) and opened it at random. Read aloud in the low husky voice she used when the house was asleep. He listened, head propped on one fist, eyes half-closed. When she stumbled over a passage he corrected her pronunciation without thinking, then looked horrified. “Forgot meself,” he said. “Don’t,” she said. “Do it again.” She read on. He followed the line with a blunt finger, tracing the Latin like it was braille. At one point he murmured, “That’s not how you say imperator round here,” and she laughed (actually laughed) before she could stop herself. The sound startled them both. Footsteps in the corridor. Mrs Haworth, bladder or conscience keeping her awake. They froze. Eleanor blew out the candle in one breath. Darkness swallowed them. The footsteps passed. Neither moved. In the black Gabriel whispered, “Test the ivy proper before tha trusts it. Thick stem, green under the bark. If it’s brown, it’s dead. Don’t lean all thy weight at once.” “I’m not planning on climbing anywhere.” “Might have to,” he said, so quietly she almost missed it. When the candle was relit they were sitting closer than before. Her knee brushed his wet trouser leg; neither pulled away. He taught her three safe handholds and the difference between living ivy and dead. She taught him the first line of Catullus 5 (Vivamus, mea Lesbia, atque amemus) and refused to translate it. He repeated it after her, stumbling, stubborn, until it sounded almost right. The clock struck four. Time to go. At the window he paused. “Tomorrow night the moon’s brighter. I’ll bring rope if the ivy’s worse.” “Don’t be an idiot.” “Too late.” He was over the rail before she could answer, descending slow, testing every grip. Halfway down a branch cracked loud as a gunshot. Eleanor’s heart stopped. He hung by one arm, swore ripely, found another hold. When his boots touched gravel he looked up once (just once) and lifted two fingers to his forehead in a small, crooked salute. She stayed on the balcony long after he was gone, sugared violet melting on her tongue, rain misting her face, repeating the Latin under her breath like a charm. Vivamus, mea Lesbia… The words tasted of danger and sugar and something perilously close to hope.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 3)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter is a genuinely accomplished piece of romantic fiction that largely delivers on its ambitious plan. Let me work through its strengths and weaknesses carefully. STRENGTHS: The dialogue is the chapter's greatest achievement. Lines like "Thought about it. Decided dying were less trouble" and "I want to sit here a bit longer without feeling like a trespasser" are precisely calibrated—they reveal character, carry emotional weight, and feel earned rather than constructed. Gabriel's Yorkshire dialect is deployed with restraint and consistency; it roughens at the right moments without becoming a caricature. Eleanor's voice ("I don't care about the bloody frame") has the clipped fury the character profile demands. The violet incident is the chapter's masterstroke. The plan called for sensuality in accidents, and this delivers: Gabriel brings violets not knowing they're Eleanor's funeral flowers, she eats one anyway, and the moment becomes simultaneously tender and painful without any authorial underlining. This is showing at its best—the reader feels the complexity without being told what to feel. The Catullus 5 choice is inspired. "Vivamus, mea Lesbia, atque amemus" (Let us live and love) is the perfect text for this scene—a poem about seizing life against disapproval, about counting kisses before death intervenes. Eleanor refusing to translate it is a small, perfect act of cowardice and desire simultaneously. The plan specified she would "quote Catullus at him to prove she is not helpless," and the execution exceeds the plan by making the Latin itself do emotional work. The physical comedy of hiding behind the chimney (from the plan) is replaced here by the footsteps-in-the-corridor scene, which works better—it's more intimate, more charged, and the darkness that follows brings them physically closer without melodrama. The ending image—"sugared violet melting on her tongue, rain misting her face, repeating the Latin under her breath like a charm"—is genuinely lovely and earns its slight lyricism because the violet and the Latin have been established as emotionally loaded objects. WEAKNESSES: The chapter plan specified they would both laugh too loudly and hide behind the chimney when a maid looks out. The execution changes this to a candle-blowing scene, which is fine, but the plan's chimney hiding had a specific physical comedy that would have been charming. This is a minor deviation. The opening paragraph's "steady, spiteful drumming" is the chapter's most conventional metaphor—"spiteful" weather is a cliché. Similarly, "shadows jumped across the books" is workmanlike rather than precise. The line "She told herself she was angry. She was. She was furious" is slightly over-insistent—the repetition feels like the author nudging the reader rather than trusting the scene. The Gibbon reading scene is slightly convenient—it's a bit too neat that he corrects her pronunciation, then looks horrified, then she says "Don't. Do it again." The beats are slightly telegraphed, though the execution is warm enough to carry it. The plan called for Eleanor to be "soaked" on the balcony and for Gabriel to be "furious she risked pneumonia"—this is delivered, but the quarrel about "who is rescuing whom" from the plan is somewhat muted. The chapter softens this confrontation into something gentler than planned, which slightly reduces the friction the plan promised. The final line's "tasted of danger and sugar and something perilously close to hope" is the chapter's weakest moment—"perilously close to hope" is a romance-novel construction that the rest of the prose is too good for. It's the one moment where the writing reaches for a conventional emotional summary rather than trusting the scene. OVERALL: This is high-quality literary romance writing. The characters are consistent with their profiles, the dialogue is strong, the emotional beats are mostly earned, and the central conceit (the window, the ivy, the Latin) is handled with intelligence. The chapter follows its plan closely while making smart adjustments. The weaknesses are real but minor—a few conventional phrases, one slightly over-explained emotional moment, one slightly soft ending line. This is the work of a writer who understands restraint and specificity. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Compelling Plot: 15 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 4 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 4 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Characters Consistent with Profile: 17 Followed Chapter Plan: 15 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 18

Chapter 4

Ok now chapter 4. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
Christmas came to Aldridge Manor the way bailiffs did: noisily, with forced cheer, and far too much brass. Mr Peregrine Cresswell arrived on the 23rd in a yellow-wheeled phaeton that splashed mud to the second-storey windows. He filled the house with cigar smoke, hunting talk, and the certainty that everything would soon belong to him. The servants’ ball was his idea (good for morale, he said, slapping backs and spilling negus). Eleanor watched from the minstrels’ gallery as the kitchen and stable folk were herded into the Long Gallery for country dances. Someone had found a fiddle with three strings and a bow that shed horsehair like dandruff. Gabriel was playing it. He stood near the fireplace in his one decent coat, sleeves too short, face closed tight. The tune was “The Rogue’s March,” fast and mocking. Servants stamped and laughed; Cresswell roared approval and tried to lead a reeling maid who wanted none of him. Eleanor felt the music like a hand round her throat. She should have stayed upstairs. Instead she slipped down the servants’ stair and hovered in the scullery doorway, half-hidden by steam and hanging hams. The heat was thick with onions and sweat and ale. Gabriel saw her. His bow faltered for one beat (just one) then steadied. Their eyes met across the smoke and stamping feet. Something raw flashed between them, gone before anyone else noticed. Cresswell noticed everything else. He caught Eleanor’s wrist as she turned to flee. “There’s my girl! Come and dance, show these folk how it’s done in polite circles.” His breath was brandy and tooth powder. “I have a headache.” “Nonsense. Fresh air and exercise.” He dragged her into the set before she could protest. She danced like a marionette, cheeks burning. Gabriel’s music grew sharper, almost vicious. When the dance spun her past him she caught the shine of sweat at his open collar, the way his jaw worked as though biting down on something furious. At eleven the gentry retired. Cresswell pinched her cheek and promised “a proper kiss under the mistletoe tomorrow.” Eleanor fled upstairs, pulse hammering louder than the drums had downstairs. She paced until the house quieted, then opened the casement wide. Frost glittered on the balcony rail; the sky was iron-hard, stars like nail-heads. She waited in her thinnest nightgown and her mother’s old wool shawl, teeth chattering. He came late, moving stiffly, coat unbuttoned against the cold. The music still clung to him (resin, smoke, sweat). When he swung over the rail he smelled of the kitchens and something darker (ale, maybe, or anger). “Thought you’d be asleep,” he said. “I was at your concert.” His mouth twisted. “Not my choice.” “I saw.” Silence stretched, thin and bright as ice. “I hate him,” she said suddenly, voice cracking. “I hate the way he looks at the house. At me, like we’re already catalogued and priced.” Gabriel stepped closer. Close enough that she felt the warmth coming off him, ale and pine and furious life. “He’ll never have you,” he said. Low, certain. “He already does. It’s settled.” “Nothing’s settled.” He reached out, slow enough she could have moved away. She didn’t. His hand settled at the nape of her neck, thumb brushing the fine hairs that had escaped her plait. His palm was hot, calloused, trembling faintly. “Eleanor.” She kissed him first. It was not gentle. She caught his lower lip between her teeth, tasted blood and ale and the faint sweetness of stolen sugar from the kitchens. He made a shocked sound, hands coming up to grip her waist hard enough to bruise through the thin lawn. For one blazing moment the world narrowed to mouths and breath and the scrape of his stubble against her chin. Then terror slammed into her like cold water. She shoved him back so hard he stumbled against the rail. “No,” she said. “No. We can’t.” “Eleanor—” “I said no!” She was shaking (rage, want, terror all tangled). Tears stung, useless and humiliating. Gabriel’s chest rose and fell like he’d been running. Blood beaded where she’d bitten him. He touched it with two fingers, looked at the red, then at her. “I’m sorry,” she whispered. “Don’t be.” His voice was raw. “I’m not.” He swung over the balcony without another word. The descent was fast, almost reckless; ivy cracked under his weight. Halfway down he slipped (one boot skidded clean off a branch) and for a heartbeat he hung by his hands alone. Eleanor’s scream lodged in her throat. He found purchase, dropped the last eight feet, landed hard and off-balance. When he straightened he did not look up. Just walked away across the frost, coat flapping like a broken wing. Eleanor slammed the casement shut and shot the bolt for the first time in weeks. The click sounded final. She stood there a long time, palms pressed to the cold glass, tasting blood where she’d bitten her own lip in sympathy. The shawl slipped from her shoulders and pooled on the floor like spilled milk. Outside, the ballroom windows still glowed faintly, music long over but the house too drunk on Cresswell’s brandy to sleep. Somewhere a clock struck one. Eleanor crawled into bed fully dressed, shoes and all, and pulled the counterpane over her head like a child hiding from ghosts. The taste of him stayed on her tongue (ale and resin and something that felt like ruin). She hated him for it. She hated herself more. The window stayed locked. She told herself it would stay that way.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 4)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter is a strong piece of Victorian romantic fiction that largely delivers on its ambitious plan. Let me work through its qualities and weaknesses systematically. **Strengths:** The opening line is genuinely good: "Christmas came to Aldridge Manor the way bailiffs did: noisily, with forced cheer, and far too much brass." This is a well-earned metaphor that does real work—it characterizes Cresswell, establishes the economic anxiety, and sets tone simultaneously. It doesn't feel forced. The sensory texture is consistently strong and specific: "resin, smoke, sweat," "ale and pine and furious life," "the scrape of his stubble against her chin." These are the grubby, physical details the plan promised, and they land. The writing avoids panoramic Victorian description and stays at body-height, as intended. Character consistency is excellent. Eleanor's pride, her tendency to act then panic, her self-recrimination ("She hated him for it. She hated herself more") all track precisely with the profile. Gabriel's compressed fury, his one-beat falter on the fiddle, his refusal to apologize ("I'm not") are perfectly calibrated. Cresswell is used economically—he's present enough to be threatening without becoming cartoonish. The kiss scene is handled with real skill. Eleanor initiating, the violence of it ("caught his lower lip between her teeth"), the immediate terror—this is psychologically honest and avoids the romance-novel cliché of the swooning heroine. The aftermath, Gabriel touching the blood and looking at it, is a strong visual beat. The chapter follows its plan faithfully: servants' ball, Gabriel playing fiddle, Eleanor watching through the scullery door, the kiss, the bite, the shove, the locked window. The plan's note about him "climbing down bleeding" is honored. **Weaknesses:** Some metaphors are slightly overworked. "Coat flapping like a broken wing" is a touch predictable for this quality of prose. "The click sounded final" is telling rather than showing—the finality is already in the action. "Thin and bright as ice" for silence is competent but not distinctive. The parenthetical asides (ale, maybe, or anger) are a stylistic tic that appears several times and occasionally feels like a hedge rather than a choice. The plan called for prose that is "plain when they're frightened, ornate when they're pretending not to be"—this chapter is fairly consistently mid-register throughout, missing some of that dynamic range. The ending, Eleanor crawling into bed "like a child hiding from ghosts," is slightly soft—it's a familiar image. The final line "She told herself it would stay that way" is competent but predictable; we know it won't, and the chapter doesn't quite find a more surprising way to land that irony. The dialogue is mostly strong but occasionally functional rather than distinctive. "Nothing's settled" is a bit of a stock line. The Catullus-quoting Eleanor of the plan doesn't quite appear here—she's reactive rather than intellectually combative, which is a slight missed opportunity. The scream that "lodged in her throat" during his near-fall is a cliché that the otherwise careful prose doesn't earn. **Overall:** This is accomplished, emotionally honest work that successfully executes a difficult tonal balance. The characters feel real, the physical world is vividly rendered, and the central scene delivers genuine tension. Its weaknesses are minor—occasional cliché, slightly flat ending, missed dynamic range—rather than structural failures. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Compelling Plot: 15 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 5 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 4 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 5 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Characters Consistent with Profile: 17 Followed Chapter Plan: 18 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 5

Ok now chapter 5. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The bolt stayed shot for fourteen nights. Fourteen nights of Eleanor lying rigid in the dark, counting the hours by the church clock across the valley. One, two, three… sometimes four before exhaustion dragged her under. She woke with jaw clenched so tight it ached. The casement glared at her like a blind eye. Downstairs Cresswell hunted, drank, and measured the silver with a weighing scale he’d brought himself. He kissed her cheek in passing, smelling of wet dogs and triumph, and called her “my little nun” when she flinched. Her father avoided her eyes. The house felt like a coffin being nailed shut from the inside. She saw Gabriel every day, of course. He came to the estate office at nine, coat brushed, hair slicked down, voice flat as a bailiff’s. He read figures to her father while she sat with her embroidery hoop and stabbed the linen until her fingers bled. Once their eyes met across the breakfast table; his flicked away first. She hated him for that. At night she walked the corridors barefoot to keep from screaming. Sometimes she stood outside the green baize door and listened to the servants laughing in the hall below—his laugh, low and sudden, like a match struck. She hated them all. On the ninth night she opened the casement just to breathe and found the balcony strewn with frost-flowers. Someone had climbed up while she slept and left nothing else. She swept them off with shaking hands and shut the window again. By the fourteenth night the house was empty. Sir Robert and Cresswell had gone to York for a cattle fair and taken the carriage, most of the indoor servants, and every drop of decent brandy. The few left behind celebrated with gin in the kitchen. Eleanor heard them singing until past midnight. She sat on the bed in her oldest gown, hair down her back like a schoolgirl’s, and stared at the bolt until her eyes watered. Then she crossed the room, turned the key in the lock with fingers that would not stop trembling, and opened the casement wide. Cold air rushed in, sharp as forgiveness. She stepped onto the balcony in nothing but stockinged feet. The frost burned. Somewhere an owl called, mournful and ridiculous. She stood there until her teeth chattered so hard she tasted blood where she’d bitten her tongue. Footsteps on gravel—hesitant, then quick. Gabriel came up the ivy slowly, testing every hold as though the plant had betrayed him before. When his head cleared the rail he saw her and stopped dead, one hand gripping a thick stem, the other braced against the wall. Moonlight silvered the new scar through his eyebrow and the hollows under his eyes. “Eleanor,” he said, voice cracked from disuse. She could not speak. If she spoke she would scream or cry or beg, and she had done enough of all three. He finished the climb like an old man, knees creaking. When both boots were on the balcony he simply looked at her—looked and looked, as if memorising the damage fourteen days had done. She was thinner. There were bruised shadows under her eyes and a cold-sore at the corner of her mouth. Her hands were raw from tearing at embroidery silk. He took one step. Another. Stopped just short of touching. “I near killed mysen coming tonight,” he said quietly. “Slipped twice. Thought if I fell it’d serve me right for a coward.” “You are a coward,” she whispered. “Aye.” Another step. Close enough now that she smelled horses and frost and the faint metallic trace of blood from a fresh cut on his knuckles. “I locked it,” she said. “I noticed.” “I hated you.” “I know.” The silence stretched, thin enough to cut. Then she moved—small, helpless—leaning forward until her forehead rested against his coat collar. He smelled exactly the same and it undid her. A sound escaped her, half-sob, half-laugh, ugly and raw. His arms came round her slowly, as though she might still bite. She did not bite. She buried her face in the damp wool and held on with both hands, fingers twisted in the fabric like she was drowning. He held her up when her knees went. They stood locked together on the freezing balcony, two people trying to fit fourteen nights of rage and longing into one embrace. His coat was wet through; hers was thin cambric. Neither cared. Minutes or hours later—he never knew—he lifted her. She weighed nothing. Carried her inside and kicked the casement shut with his heel. The room was black-dark; the fire long dead. He sat on the floor with his back to the bedpost and settled her in his lap like a child, her legs across his thighs, her head tucked under his chin. She was shaking so hard her teeth rattled. He pulled the counterpane down and wrapped it round them both, rocking slightly, the way you quiet a frightened horse. Neither spoke. Words felt too small. Her fingers found the new scar on his knuckles, traced it. He pressed his lips to her hair, once, twice, tasting salt and frost and the faint sweetness of sugared violets gone stale in her pocket weeks ago. Outside, the church clock struck three, then four. The house stayed mercifully asleep. When the sky beyond the curtains turned the colour of pewter they were still on the floor, tangled in blankets and each other, breathing the same small pocket of air. Her hand rested over his heart; his thumb drew slow circles on her wrist where the pulse fluttered. The window stayed unlatched. Frost feathered the inside of the panes like lace. Neither of them had slept. Neither wanted morning.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 5)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter is a genuinely accomplished piece of romantic fiction that largely delivers on its ambitious plan. Let me work through its strengths and weaknesses carefully. The prose voice is controlled and distinctive. The plan called for prose that is "plain when they're frightened, ornate when they're pretending not to be," and the chapter largely honors this: the sentences during the reunion are stripped down, almost monosyllabic in the dialogue ("Aye." "I noticed." "I know."), while the descriptive passages allow slightly more texture. This is a real craft choice, not an accident. The dialogue is one of the chapter's strongest elements. The exchange on the balcony—"I locked it." / "I noticed." / "I hated you." / "I know."—is exactly the kind of compressed, loaded exchange the plan promised. It avoids melodrama while carrying enormous emotional weight. Gabriel's Yorkshire dialect surfaces naturally ("I near killed mysen") without being performed or overdone. The dialogue earns its brevity. The sensory details are specific and grounded: "frost-flowers," "the faint metallic trace of blood from a fresh cut on his knuckles," "sugared violets gone stale in her pocket weeks ago." The violet detail is particularly good—it connects to Eleanor's character profile (she hates violets, her mother's funeral flowers) in a way that rewards attentive readers without announcing itself. This is showing, not telling. The chapter follows its plan faithfully: the fourteen-day silence, Eleanor watching from behind curtains, the house emptying, the opening of the window, Gabriel's near-fall, the reunion without a kiss, holding on until dawn. The plan's instruction that "they do not kiss; they just hold on until the sky goes grey" is honored precisely and effectively. Where the chapter is slightly weaker: a few metaphors strain. "Cold air rushed in, sharp as forgiveness" is the most questionable—forgiveness is not typically sharp, and the comparison feels like it's reaching for profundity rather than earning it. "The casement glared at her like a blind eye" is a competent but not especially fresh image. "The silence stretched, thin enough to cut" is a cliché. These are minor but real lapses in an otherwise disciplined prose style. The "coffin being nailed shut from the inside" is a strong image. The frost-flowers left on the balcony is a lovely, ambiguous detail—did Gabriel leave them? The text implies it but doesn't confirm, which is exactly right. The ending—"Neither of them had slept. Neither wanted morning"—is clean and earned. It doesn't overreach. Cresswell's brief appearance ("my little nun") is well-deployed: he's present enough to feel threatening without dominating a chapter that isn't his. Sir Robert's averted eyes are economical characterization. One small concern: "He lifted her. She weighed nothing." The "she weighed nothing" is a slightly worn romantic trope. Similarly, "like a child" and "the way you quiet a frightened horse" are both effective but the horse comparison is perhaps too on-the-nose given Gabriel's background—it risks feeling like a planted character note rather than organic observation. The chapter is emotionally engaging, coherent, and consistent with both character profiles. Eleanor's pride, her compulsive counting (fourteen nights, fourteen days), her inability to speak without screaming—all consistent. Gabriel's stubborn return, his self-recrimination ("serve me right for a coward"), his physical tenderness—all consistent. The darkness is well-earned: fourteen days of genuine suffering, not manufactured. The lightness (the owl called "mournful and ridiculous") is brief but real. Overall this is a high-quality chapter that mostly avoids the pitfalls of the genre while delivering genuine emotional resonance. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 18 Compelling Plot: 15 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 4 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 6 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Characters Consistent with Profile: 18 Followed Chapter Plan: 18 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 6

Ok now chapter 6. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The beating happened on a Tuesday. Eleanor heard it from the library window: her father’s voice raised in fury, the crack of a riding crop, a single choked sound that was not quite a cry. She ran barefoot down the servants’ stair and reached the stable yard in time to see Gabriel on his knees in the mud, arms wrapped round his head while Sir Robert laid about him with the crop like a man flogging a gatepost. The grooms stood frozen. No one dared intervene. Eleanor screamed her father’s name. Sir Robert turned, face purple, eyes wild with drink and terror. “Go inside, girl! This is none of your affair!” Gabriel lifted his head long enough for her to see the blood running from his temple, the split lip, the eye already swelling shut. Then the crop came down again and he curled tighter, silent. She would remember the silence most. Sir Robert flung the crop away at last, chest heaving. “Get up, you bastard,” he spat. “You’ll find those missing pages or I’ll have the constable on you for theft.” He stormed back to the house, boots squelching. The grooms melted away. Eleanor knelt in the filth without caring that her gown soaked through. Gabriel tried to push her off. “Don’t. Mud.” “Shut up.” Her voice shook. She got an arm round his shoulders; he was heavier than he looked, all bone and stubborn muscle. Together they staggered to the tack-room and shut the door. Inside it smelled of leather and liniment and horse. Weak afternoon light came through one grimy window. Eleanor fetched the brandy flask kept for colic and poured a mouthful down him. He coughed, spat blood, took another swallow. She tore strips from her petticoat with shaking hands. When she touched the cut above his eye he flinched. “Hold still.” “Can’t.” His voice was thick. “Ribs.” She eased his coat off, then the shirt. Bruises bloomed across his side like spilled wine (old yellow ones layered beneath fresh purple). One rib grated when he breathed. She made a sound she did not recognise as hers. “He found the ledger pages gone,” Gabriel said. “Thinks I took ’em to blackmail him.” “Did you?” He gave a cracked laugh. “Wanted to. Burned ’em instead.” She cleaned the blood with brandy and the petticoat strips. He watched her face, not the wounds. “I’ll manage the books,” he said suddenly. “Shift what’s left. Cresswell’ll still have the house, but you’ll not be part of the bargain. I swear it.” “Stop swearing things you can’t keep.” “I keep what matters.” Night came early, sleet ticking against the tack-room window. The house settled into its usual drunk slumber. Gabriel could not climb with one arm cradled to his side, so Eleanor led him up the servants’ stair, past the snoring boot boy, to her room. He moved like an old man, breath whistling through broken teeth. When the door shut behind them he simply slid down the wall and sat on the floor, head back, eyes closed. Eleanor lit one candle. The room looked smaller with him in it (larger, too, as though the walls had learned a new dimension). She knelt again. “Lie down before you fall.” “Can’t. Bed’s too good for the likes of me.” “Then the floor’s too cold. Choose.” He chose the floor. She dragged the mattress off the bedstead and shoved it beside him. He looked at her as if she’d performed a miracle, then eased himself down with a groan. Eleanor fetched the brandy, the last clean petticoat, a basin of water gone tepid. She cleaned the dirt from his knuckles, bound the worst cuts. When she reached the bruise over his ribs he caught her wrist. “Enough.” “Not nearly.” His fingers tightened. “Eleanor.” She met his eye (the good one, swollen half-shut). “I will marry him,” she said. The words tasted of rust. “In Lent. To keep the roof. You know that.” He said nothing. Just looked at her until she had to look away. She laid her head on his chest, careful of the bruises. His heart hammered beneath her ear, fast and alive and furious. Outside, sleet turned to snow. The candle burned lower. “I hate this house,” she whispered against his shirt. “I hate what it does to people.” His good arm came round her, slow and painful. “Then leave it.” “I can’t.” “You can.” His voice cracked. “With me.” She laughed, a broken sound. “And live on what? Your wages? Two rooms over a stable?” “If that’s what it takes.” She wanted to believe him. Wanted it so violently her bones hurt. Snow hissed against the window. The candle guttered. Gabriel’s fingers found the nape of her neck, stroked once, twice, like gentling a horse. She turned her face into his throat and breathed him in (blood, horses, brandy, snow). He smelled like the only real thing left in the world. They stayed like that until the candle drowned in its own wax. Until the snow stopped and the house creaked around them like an old ship taking on water. He slept first, breath evening out, the bruised hand still tangled in her hair. Eleanor lay awake listening to his heart and the wind and the slow drip of melting snow from the eaves. At some point she pressed her lips to the pulse in his wrist, just once, tasting salt and iron. Morning would come with Cresswell’s laughter and her father’s shame and the smell of wet wool and defeat. But for now the mattress was wide enough for two broken people, and the window (unlatched, as always) let in a blade of cold moonlight that cut straight across them both like a promise neither dared speak aloud.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 6)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter is a strong piece of Victorian-inflected literary fiction that largely delivers on its ambitious plan. Let me work through its qualities and weaknesses systematically. STRENGTHS: The prose voice is controlled and distinctive. The chapter opens with brutal economy: "The beating happened on a Tuesday." This is exactly the kind of plain declarative sentence the plan called for when characters are frightened, and it works. The violence is rendered without melodrama—the detail of Gabriel "silent" under the crop, and Eleanor noting "She would remember the silence most," is genuinely affecting and shows rather than tells the horror. Character consistency is high. Eleanor's pride manifesting as action rather than speech ("Shut up. Her voice shook"), her compulsive care despite herself, her use of Latin-inflected precision ("Not nearly") all track with the profile. Gabriel's stubborn dignity ("Can't. Bed's too good for the likes of me"), his flat Yorkshire vowels roughening under stress, his tendency to trust action over words—all consistent. The line "I keep what matters" is exactly the kind of compressed, action-oriented declaration his profile predicts. The sensory texture is well-managed. The tack-room smelling of "leather and liniment and horse," the brandy for colic, the petticoat strips, the bruises "like spilled wine (old yellow ones layered beneath fresh purple)"—these are specific and grounded. The parenthetical about old bruises beneath new ones is quietly devastating, implying a history of violence without stating it. The chapter plan called for Gabriel arriving with split knuckles and a black eye, climbing one-handed, Eleanor cleaning cuts with brandy, ending up on the floor because the bed is too soft for shaking, and Eleanor telling him she'll marry Cresswell while he presses his forehead to hers until dawn. The chapter follows this faithfully but with intelligent modifications: the beating is witnessed rather than just reported, which raises the stakes; the floor scene is rendered with more texture than the plan suggested; the ending substitutes moonlight and a pulse-kiss for the forehead-press, which is arguably more restrained and effective. The dialogue is largely excellent. It's clipped, functional, full of half-finished thoughts. "Can't. Ribs." is perfect. The exchange about the ledger pages ("Did you?" / "Wanted to. Burned 'em instead.") is economical and character-revealing. The marriage declaration—"I will marry him. In Lent. To keep the roof. You know that."—lands with appropriate weight. WEAKNESSES: There are moments where the prose tips toward the slightly overwrought. "The room looked smaller with him in it (larger, too, as though the walls had learned a new dimension)" is the most egregious example—it's the kind of paradoxical observation that sounds profound but doesn't quite mean anything concrete. Walls don't learn dimensions; this is the sort of poetic riffing the scoring notes warn against. "She smelled like the only real thing left in the world" (applied to him) is a cliché of the genre, even if it's rendered with some specificity in the preceding list of smells. The candle burning down is a well-worn symbol, and "the candle drowned in its own wax" while evocative is not surprising. Similarly, "the house creaked around them like an old ship taking on water" is a competent but familiar metaphor. The ending paragraph—"Morning would come with Cresswell's laughter and her father's shame and the smell of wet wool and defeat"—is slightly too neat in its summarizing function, telling us what morning will bring rather than trusting the scene to carry that weight. The plan specified Gabriel climbing "one-handed because the other arm is in a sling." In the chapter, he can't climb at all and is led up the servants' stair instead—a reasonable adaptation, but it loses the specific image of the one-handed climb that would have been visually striking and thematically resonant with the window motif. The window appears only at the end as an unlatched casement letting in moonlight, which is present but less central than the plan intended. The "Well-earned Lightness or Darkness" criterion: the darkness here is genuinely earned through specific detail and consistent characterization. The tenderness of the ending—the pulse-kiss, the sleeping together without sex—feels appropriate and not sentimental. Overall, this is accomplished literary fiction with a few moments of reaching too hard for profundity. The characters are vivid, the emotion is real, the prose is mostly disciplined. It falls short of masterpiece territory primarily because of occasional metaphorical overreach and the slight underuse of the window as structural element. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Compelling Plot: 15 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 5 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 6 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Characters Consistent with Profile: 17 Followed Chapter Plan: 14 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 16

Chapter 7

Ok now chapter 7. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
Shrove Tuesday was fixed for the wedding. A special licence, Cresswell boasted; no need to wait for banns when a man could buy time like everything else. The village seamstress had been up three nights running, turning an old court dress of Eleanor’s mother into something “suitable.” The house stank of beeswax and panic. Gabriel came to her room three nights before the day, climbing one-handed because his ribs were still taped. Snow had turned to sleet again; his coat steamed in the warm room. He carried a small bundle wrapped in oilskin. “Tickets,” he said without greeting. “Liverpool to Quebec. Third class on the *SS Moravian*, sailing Friday week. Cattle boat, but she’s sound.” He laid two pasteboard stubs on the table as if they were holy relics. “I’ve fifty pounds saved, and passage paid. Enough to start somewhere they don’t know the name Aldridge.” Eleanor stared at the tickets. They looked absurdly small. “Under false names,” he added. “Mr and Mrs Thorne. Easier that way.” She laughed once (sharp, incredulous). “You think I can simply vanish? My father will send riders. Cresswell will have the ports watched.” “Only if they wake in time.” He pulled a second, smaller packet from his pocket: a valise no larger than a saddlebag. Inside: one plain wool dress, a shawl, stout boots two sizes too big (stolen from the housemaids’ cupboard), a purse with twenty sovereigns, and a single silver hairpin that had once been her mother’s. “I can’t,” she said. The words scraped her throat raw. Gabriel’s face did not change, but something behind his eyes died a little. “Right,” he said. “Thought I’d ask.” He turned to the window. “Wait.” She caught his sleeve. “I didn’t say never. I said—” “I heard what tha said.” Silence fell between them, heavy as wet wool. Outside, the sleet hissed against the panes. The house was quiet; Cresswell had taken her father to York again to sign final papers. Only the kitchen cat and Mrs Haworth’s snores moved below. Gabriel sat on the edge of the straight chair, elbows on knees, staring at the tickets as if they might fly away. Eleanor paced. The carpet was worn to threads beneath her bare feet. “If I go,” she said at last, “I leave my mother’s books to be sold for brandy. I leave the tenants to Cresswell’s bailiffs. I leave the dogs in the kennel to starve because no one else feeds them.” He looked up. “And if tha stays?” She had no answer that did not taste of ash. The clock struck two. Gabriel stood. “I’ll come at four on the morning of the wedding,” he said quietly. “Carriage’ll be waiting beyond the west gate. If tha’s there, we go. If not—” He shrugged, the movement stiff with pain. “I’ll not ask again.” He swung over the sill. She watched him descend slowly, one arm cradling ribs, boots slipping on the icy ivy. Halfway down he stopped, looked up. Moonlight caught the bruise still yellow on his cheek. “I love thee,” he said, so low the wind nearly stole it. “That’s all I’ve got left to give.” Then he was gone. Eleanor did not sleep. She sat on the roof instead, climbed out when the house was darkest, and crouched among the chimney pots in her nightgown and borrowed trousers, hair whipping in the wind. The slates were glazed with ice; her toes went numb. She watched the sky pale from black to pewter to the colour of dirty wool. At half past three she heard the casement creak behind her. Gabriel again, breathing hard. He had come back. “I couldn’t leave thee to choose alone,” he said. He sat beside her, careful not to touch. They watched the park whiten with frost. “I’m frightened,” she whispered. “Me too.” “I’ll miss the library.” “I’ll miss the horses.” “I’ll miss my mother’s grave.” “I’ll miss nowt but thee if tha stays.” She turned to him then. His face was drawn with cold and sleeplessness, the swollen eye still half-shut. A streak of blood where the ivy had torn his cheek again. “I can’t leave her,” Eleanor said, voice cracking. “Not without—” She stopped. Could not say goodbye. Gabriel nodded once, as though he had expected it. He started to rise. Eleanor caught his hand. “Wait.” She looked at the sky, at the black windows of the house that had raised her and broken her in equal measure. At the thin gold band of dawn beginning to show above the trees. “One more night,” she said. “Let me say goodbye properly. Then—if you still want me—I’ll come.” He searched her face for lies and found none. “Aye,” he said at last. “One more night.” They sat there until the cold drove them back inside. He kissed her once (slow, deliberate, tasting of blood and sleet and farewell) then climbed down again. This time she did not watch him go. She spent the day like a ghost: touched the spines of books she would never read again, fed the kennel dogs scraps of wedding cake meant for guests, stood a long time at her mother’s grave in the chapel yard while the sexton pretended not to see her crying. At dusk she burned her childhood diaries in the schoolroom fire and watched the pages curl like black wings. Night came again. She left the casement unlatched. She did not know if he would return, or if she would have the courage to follow when he did. She only knew that tomorrow Cresswell would arrive with the bishop and the ring and the smug smile of a man who had bought a baronet’s daughter along with the timber rights. Eleanor sat on the edge of the bed in the dark, fully dressed beneath her nightgown (the wool dress, the stolen boots, the silver hairpin clutched in her fist like a talisman). Outside, the wind rattled the ivy against the wall, impatient.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 7)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter represents a strong execution of the plan, with several genuinely accomplished moments and a few places where the writing strains or settles for the obvious. The chapter follows its plan closely: Gabriel arrives with tickets, Eleanor hesitates, they argue on the roof, he starts to leave, she follows. The plan called for "voices cracking" and "him starting down alone" before she climbs after him. The chapter modifies this slightly—making it a two-night structure rather than a single 4 a.m. scene—which actually enriches the emotional texture by giving Eleanor a day of farewell. This is a creative improvement on the plan, not a deviation. Character consistency is strong. Eleanor's pride, her compulsive cataloguing of what she'll lose (books, tenants, dogs, mother's grave), her use of delay as a form of courage—all ring true to the profile. Gabriel's flat delivery ("Thought I'd ask"), his return because he "couldn't leave thee to choose alone," his Yorkshire dialect roughening under emotion—these are well-handled. The dialect is deployed sparingly and effectively: "Tha's daft" would be too on-the-nose here, but "I'll miss nowt but thee if tha stays" lands with real force. The dialogue is largely excellent. The exchange "I'll miss the library / I'll miss the horses / I'll miss my mother's grave / I'll miss nowt but thee if tha stays" is the chapter's best moment—a genuine piece of compressed emotional writing that earns its parallelism. The "I love thee" declaration, delivered halfway down an icy wall in moonlight, risks sentimentality but is saved by its brevity and the physical context. Where the chapter falters: "something behind his eyes died a little" is a cliché that the prose elsewhere avoids. "Pages curl like black wings" is the kind of poetic reach that doesn't quite land—it's trying too hard for a moment that doesn't need the flourish. "Heavy as wet wool" is used twice in the story (it appears in the plan's tone notes) and feels recycled here. The burning of diaries is a slightly too-neat symbolic gesture—the chapter plan didn't include it, and while it's not egregious, it tips toward the expected. The two-night structure is genuinely clever: it gives Eleanor agency (she chooses to spend a day saying goodbye rather than simply failing to act at 3:45 a.m.) and makes her eventual decision feel more earned. The detail of her sitting "fully dressed beneath her nightgown" in the dark, waiting, is excellent—specific, physical, and emotionally precise without being explained. The chapter's ending is strong: the ivy rattling "impatient" is a light personification that works because it's restrained. The uncertainty about whether he'll return, and whether she'll have courage, is appropriately unresolved for a penultimate chapter. The prose is generally clean and controlled. The plan called for "plain when frightened, ornate when pretending not to be"—this chapter is mostly plain, which is right for the emotional register. There's no purple prose to speak of, and the metaphors that appear are mostly earned. One weakness: the chapter tells us Eleanor "did not sleep" and then shows her on the roof, which is fine, but the transition from the first night to the second is handled a little abruptly. The day-of-farewell section (books, dogs, grave, diaries) is slightly rushed—a list of gestures rather than a scene. This is the chapter's main structural weakness. Overall, this is accomplished work: emotionally engaging, character-consistent, well-paced, with dialogue that genuinely serves the story. The flaws are real but minor. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Compelling Plot: 15 Coherent: 16 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 3 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 5 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Characters Consistent with Profile: 17 Followed Chapter Plan: 15 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 8

Ok now finish the story with chapter 8. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
Dawn crept in pale and sour, the colour of dishwater. The wedding was set for eleven; the bishop had been promised breakfast and a fat purse. Already the kitchen clattered with pans and the smell of kippers drifted up the back stairs. Eleanor heard it all through the floorboards: the ordinary, merciless noise of a house preparing to sell her. She had not slept. The wool dress clung to her skin beneath the cambric nightgown; the stolen boots pinched. Her mother’s silver hairpin was twisted so tightly into her fist the edges had cut half-moons into her palm. At half past five she opened the casement for the last time. Frost lay thick on the balcony rail. The ivy looked black and brittle, treacherous with ice. Far below, the gravel glinted like broken glass. Gabriel was already there. He stood on the roof tiles to the left of the balcony, coatless despite the cold, breath pluming white. His ribs were bound tight beneath the shirt; one sleeve was torn where the ivy had fought him on the way up. He looked as though he had been waiting for hours. Their eyes met. No words. None left. Eleanor stepped over the sill. The nightgown caught on the latch and ripped; she left it flapping like a white flag. The wind cut through the wool dress at once, sharp enough to make her gasp. Gabriel reached out, steadying her as her stockinged feet slid on the frozen slates. “Careful,” he said, voice rough from cold and sleeplessness. “Tiles are worse than ivy.” She nodded. Her teeth had started to chatter. Together they moved along the ridge, two small figures against the great blind face of the house. Behind them the west-wing window stood open, banging gently whenever the wind caught it. A maid would find it later and scream about ghosts. They reached the corner where the old drainpipe ran down to the scullery yard. Gabriel went first, testing each bracket with his weight. Halfway he slipped; slate shattered under his boot and rained down like hail. Eleanor’s heart stopped. He caught the pipe, hung a moment by one arm, then found footing again. “Your turn,” he said without looking up. She followed. The wool dress rode up; her knees scraped raw. Cold burned her lungs. When she reached the pipe her hands were too numb to grip properly. Gabriel braced below her, took her weight on his shoulders until her feet found the brackets. Together they slid the last ten feet and dropped into the soft muck of the kitchen garden. They landed hard. Eleanor’s ankle turned; pain shot white-hot up her leg. For a moment she could only cling to him, face buried in his coat, breathing him in—sweat, horses, frost, terror. “Can tha walk?” he asked against her hair. “I’ll run if I have to.” He laughed once, breathless and incredulous, then took her hand. They limped across the herb beds, past the glasshouse where the wedding flowers waited in buckets (hothouse lilies already drooping). The kennel dogs set up a furious barking; Eleanor whistled the low note her father used and they fell silent, confused. At the west gate a dog-cart waited, horse blanketed against the cold. Old Josiah the groom stood holding the reins, face creased with worry and something like approval. “Thought you’d never come, Miss,” he muttered. “Road’s clear to the station if you’re quick.” Gabriel helped her up. The seat was hard and smelled of oats and leather. He climbed in beside her, took the reins. Josiah slapped the horse’s rump and they lurched forward. Behind them the manor woke slowly. A window slammed. Someone shouted. The open casement on the west wing banged once more, like a mouth trying to speak. Eleanor looked back only once. The great house stood pale against the rising sun, every window catching the light like astonished eyes. Smoke began to curl from the kitchen chimney; somewhere a bell began to ring in frantic alarm. The torn fragment of nightgown still fluttered from the balcony rail, white and forlorn. She turned away. Gabriel’s hand found hers on the seat between them. Their fingers were both filthy, both bleeding. Neither cared. He clicked to the horse and they rattled down the back drive, mud flying from the wheels. The frostbitten park rolled past—bare oaks, skeletal hedgerows, rooks rising in a black cloud at their passing. Ahead the road stretched narrow and rutted toward the village, toward the railway, toward anywhere that was not Aldridge Manor. Eleanor leaned her head against Gabriel’s shoulder. Her ankle throbbed; her palm stung where the hairpin had cut; the cold ate through the wool dress until she shook. None of it mattered. Behind them the bell kept ringing, fainter now, swallowed by distance and the rattle of wheels. She did not look back again. They reached the crossroads where the signpost pointed to York one way, Liverpool the other. Gabriel slowed the horse. “North or south?” he asked, voice steady for the first time in weeks. Eleanor looked at the pale sky, at the mud on her boots, at his bruised knuckles white on the reins. “South,” she said. “Then west. Then wherever the ship takes us.” He grinned (crooked, tired, real) and turned the horse’s head. They drove into the morning, two small figures shrinking against the vast frost-whitened park, hand in hand, while behind them the manor windows blazed with the rising sun and the open casement on the west wing banged once, twice, then fell silent forever.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 8)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This is a strong final chapter that largely delivers on the plan's promise. Let me work through it carefully. The chapter opens with sensory grounding that feels earned rather than decorative: "the colour of dishwater," kippers through floorboards, the ordinary machinery of a house preparing to sell someone. These details are specific and class-conscious in the right way. The hairpin cutting half-moons into Eleanor's palm is the kind of physical detail the plan called for—sensuality living in accidents, in the body's small betrayals. The reunion on the roof is handled with admirable restraint. "No words. None left." is exactly the register the plan demanded—plain prose when frightened. The nightgown catching on the latch and being left "flapping like a white flag" is a genuinely good image, doing double work (the surrender of her old life, the literal flag of her escape). It earns its metaphor. The descent sequence is tense and physical. Slate shattering, Gabriel hanging by one arm, Eleanor's numb hands—these are concrete and believable. The plan specified "every climb is a gamble" and this delivers. The landing in the kitchen garden muck, the ankle turning, the moment of clinging—"sweat, horses, frost, terror"—is the kind of messy, specific longing the plan promised. Old Josiah is a slight convenience—the plan mentioned a groom seeing them and Gabriel staring him down, but instead we get a pre-arranged ally with a dog-cart. This softens the escape somewhat and feels like a small cheat against the plan's stated preference for no convenient rescues. The plan said "A groom sees them, opens his mouth—Gabriel stares him down." Instead we get a friendly accomplice. This is a meaningful deviation that reduces tension. The kennel dogs falling silent when Eleanor whistles is a lovely character beat—it shows competence, it's unexpected, it costs nothing in terms of pace. The dialogue is sparse and functional. "Can tha walk?" / "I'll run if I have to." is good—it's in character, it's brief, it carries the right emotional weight. "North or south?" / "South. Then west. Then wherever the ship takes us." is slightly more on-the-nose, a touch too neat as a closing exchange, but it works within the register. The ending image—the open casement "banging once, twice, then fell silent forever"—is the plan's central motif paid off. It's effective. However, "fell silent forever" is a slight overreach; "forever" is the kind of word that tips into sentimentality. The plan's ending was "they drop to the grass together and start walking"—the actual ending is more elaborate, with the dog-cart and the crossroads, which is fine but slightly more resolved than the plan's deliberately open-ended vision. The manor windows "blazed with the rising sun" and "astonished eyes" is a personification that works. The torn nightgown "white and forlorn" is good. The rooks rising in a black cloud is perhaps the most conventional image in the piece—it's the kind of Gothic shorthand that the plan explicitly wanted to avoid ("No panoramic descriptions of moors"). Purple prose is largely absent. The writing is disciplined. There are occasional moments where the prose reaches slightly too hard—"the vast frost-whitened park" in the final paragraph feels like it's straining for grandeur—but these are minor. The characters are consistent with their profiles. Eleanor's pride, her physical toughness, her refusal to look back more than once. Gabriel's steadiness, his dialect ("Can tha walk?"), his practical focus. The plan's instruction that "they will be clumsy, jealous, unkind for whole minutes" doesn't apply here since this is the escape chapter, but the clumsiness is physical rather than emotional, which is appropriate. The chapter follows the plan closely but with the Josiah deviation noted above. The plan's ending—"two people walking away while the manor watches in outrage and the sky slowly turns the colour of weak tea"—is approximated but the dog-cart changes the register from foot-escape to carriage-escape, which is slightly less raw than intended. Overall this is accomplished, controlled work. It doesn't quite reach masterpiece level—the Josiah convenience, the "forever" slip, the rooks, and the slightly too-tidy final exchange hold it back—but it's genuinely good Victorian-inflected prose that earns most of its emotional beats. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Compelling Plot: 14 Coherent: 17 Weak Dialogue: 5 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 7 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 4 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 4 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 15 Characters Consistent with Profile: 16 Followed Chapter Plan: 13 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Final Piece Evaluation

Judge Response (First Entry):
[Analysis] This is a substantial, ambitious piece of Victorian romantic fiction that demonstrates considerable craft. Let me assess it carefully across all dimensions. STRENGTHS: The story maintains strong internal coherence across eight chapters. Character names, physical details (Gabriel's bruised jaw, the ivy's condition, Eleanor's reading habits), and plot threads (the ledger, Cresswell's arrival, the ribs) remain consistent throughout. The Yorkshire dialect deployed for Gabriel is used judiciously and authentically—it appears when he's relaxed or emotional, recedes when he's formal, which is a nice character touch. The central conceit (window-climbing as both literal plot device and metaphor for transgression, intimacy, and escape) is well-executed and genuinely tied to the prompt. The ivy becomes a recurring motif that earns its symbolic weight through repetition and variation rather than being forced. Character work is generally strong. Eleanor is not a passive heroine—she initiates the kiss, she makes the difficult choice, she burns her diaries. Gabriel is rendered with physical specificity (the Yorkshire vowels, the rough hands, the stubborn dignity) that prevents him from being a generic romantic hero. The class dynamics are handled with some sophistication; his "bed's too good for the likes of me" line is both characterful and thematically resonant. The dialogue is largely excellent. Lines like "Don't thank me. Just don't fall" and "Then I'd best not fall" achieve the compressed emotional weight that good Victorian-inflected romance requires. The Catullus scene (Chapter 3) is genuinely inventive—using "Vivamus, mea Lesbia, atque amemus" without translating it is a smart choice that lets the reader supply the meaning. WEAKNESSES: The piece leans heavily on certain repeated gestures: fingers brushing, breath pluming white, the casement left unlatched. By Chapter 5 these have become somewhat mechanical. The "cold fingers grazing" motif appears so frequently it loses its charge. Some metaphors don't fully land. "Books lay everywhere like bright-plumed birds shot mid-flight" is strained—the image of shot birds doesn't cohere with the warmth of the reading scene. "The whole decaying manor leaning in to watch" is a cliché of Gothic fiction. "The silence stretched thin" appears multiple times. "The words tasted of rust" in Chapter 6 is effective; "tasted of ash" in Chapter 7 is less so, being more generic. The pacing in the middle chapters (3-5) is somewhat repetitive in structure: Gabriel arrives, they share intimacy, he descends, she watches. The emotional escalation is present but the structural variation is limited. Chapter 5's fourteen-night gap is handled well, but the reunion scene, while emotionally effective, relies on familiar beats (the forehead-against-chest, the arms coming round slowly). The transformation from "I can't" to "I'll come" in Chapter 7 is somewhat rushed. Eleanor's decision to leave—abandoning her mother's grave, the tenants, the dogs—is given weight in the text but the actual moment of commitment feels slightly compressed given the magnitude of what she's surrendering. The final chapter's escape sequence is competent but slightly cinematic in a way that feels anachronistic to the Victorian register—the rooftop scramble, the waiting dog-cart, the bell ringing behind them. It resolves cleanly but perhaps too cleanly for the moral complexity the earlier chapters established. Purple prose appears occasionally but not overwhelmingly. "Two small figures shrinking against the vast frost-whitened park" at the close is effective; "blazed with the rising sun" is more generic. The piece generally earns its lyrical moments. The "unearned transformation" question: Eleanor's decision to leave is the central transformation, and the story does work to earn it across seven chapters. However, the speed of the final commitment (one conversation on a rooftop) after fourteen nights of locked windows feels slightly compressed. The emotional logic is present but the dramatic logic is slightly rushed. Overall this is a genuinely accomplished piece of romantic fiction—well above average, with real craft in dialogue, character consistency, and thematic coherence. Its weaknesses are those of a skilled writer working in a demanding form: occasional over-reliance on repeated gestures, some metaphors that don't fully land, and a final act that resolves slightly too neatly. [Scores] Believable Character Actions: 16 Nuanced Characters: 15 Pacing: 13 World Building: 16 Compelling Plot: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Coherent: 17 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 3 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 7 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 6 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 6 Unearned Transformations: 7 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 15 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 18
Avg Final Item Score: 75.9/100
+ Romance — Love in the Limelight (Chapter Avg: 81.9 | Final: 75.9)
Initial Writing Prompt:
Romance: Love in the Limelight. Write a short story set in Wales. Task: write a short story over several chapters. A famous (fictional) actor ducks into a small-town bookstore to escape paparazzi. The story takes us through the characters meeting in this odd circumstance. Over the course of the first scene, the actor and the bookstore owner have a conversation charged by an undercurrent of unspoken chemistry. Write the actor as somewhat of a rogue with a fragile ego, which needs to be fed by having everyone like him. He is thoroughly charming, but the bookstore owner seems (at least superficially) immune to this; which paradoxically provokes a genuine attraction and derails the charm offensive. The bookstore owner, despite the superficial rebuffs of the actor's charm, is inwardly more than a little charmed and flustered despite themselves. Write primarily in dialogue, in the distinct voices of each character. The story will progress beyond this synopsis, and it's up to you where it goes.
+ Planning Phase (5 Steps)
Romance: Love in the Limelight. Write a short story set in Wales. Task: write a short story over several chapters. A famous (fictional) actor ducks into a small-town bookstore to escape paparazzi. The story takes us through the characters meeting in this odd circumstance. Over the course of the first scene, the actor and the bookstore owner have a conversation charged by an undercurrent of unspoken chemistry. Write the actor as somewhat of a rogue with a fragile ego, which needs to be fed by having everyone like him. He is thoroughly charming, but the bookstore owner seems (at least superficially) immune to this; which paradoxically provokes a genuine attraction and derails the charm offensive. The bookstore owner, despite the superficial rebuffs of the actor&#x27;s charm, is inwardly more than a little charmed and flustered despite themselves. Write primarily in dialogue, in the distinct voices of each character. The story will progress beyond this synopsis, and it&#x27;s up to you where it goes. -- Your task is to create a writing plan for this prompt. The scope will be a short story, written over {n_chapters} chapters. Each chapter will be approximately 1000 words so plan accordingly for this scope. Your plan should be comprehensive and in this format: # Brainstorming <Brainstorm ideas for characters, plot, tone, story beats, pacing over the {n_chapters} chapters. The purpose of brainstorming is to cast a wide net of ideas, not to settle on any specific direction. Think about various ways you could take the prompt.> # Reflection <Reflect out loud on what works and doesn't work in these ideas. The purpose of this reflection is to narrow in on what you think will work best to make a piece that is a. compelling, and b. fits the prompt requirements. You are not making any decisons just yet, just reflecting.>
Model Output:
# Brainstorming ### Characters - Actor: Possible names – Rhys Maddox, Cassian Vale, Ioan Hart, or simply “Rhys Ifans-type” energy with a fictional twist. Age 38–44. Known for brooding anti-heroes on screen but desperately wants to be universally adored off-screen. Charms reflexively, flirts like breathing, but panics if someone doesn’t immediately melt. Fragile underneath the rogue swagger. Welsh mother, raised partly in London, so he can code-switch between polished English and lyrical Welsh-accented warmth. - Bookshop owner: Names – Elin Vaughan, Sian Powell, Cerys Llewellyn, or Lowri Morgan. Mid-30s. Dry, quietly acerbic, fiercely protective of her quiet life in a small coastal or valley town (options: Aberystwyth, Hay-on-Wye, Narberth, Laugharne, or a completely fictional town like “Porth Maddoc”). Reads people instantly, allergic to performative nonsense, but secretly a hopeless romantic who hides behind cardigans and sarcasm. Grew up locally, left for university, came back when her mother got sick. - Secondary characters: a teenage Saturday girl who is star-struck, an old fisherman regular who couldn’t care less about celebrities, Elin’s best friend (caffeine-fuelled primary-school teacher who WILL lose her mind when she finds out), Rhys’s long-suffering publicist who keeps texting, maybe a local journalist who smells blood. ### Possible settings - Hay-on-Wye (obvious book town, but risks cliché) - A small independent shop in Cardigan or Fishguard with sea-spray on the windows - A converted chapel bookshop in the valleys - A tiny store on the high street of Machynlleth with the bell that always sticks ### Tone variations 1. Warm, gently comedic, autumnal, with Welsh-language phrases sprinkled in. 2. Slightly melancholic, rainy, both characters a bit broken. 3. Full-on screwball rom-com with paparazzi chases and mistaken identity. 4. Slow-burn literary romance with long silences and poetry quotes. ### Story-beat possibilities across 8 chapters - Ch1: The escape into the bookshop, first meeting, verbal sparring. - Ch2: He hides upstairs/in the back; they are forced into prolonged proximity while paparazzi circle. - Ch3: He buys half the shop just to stay longer; she refuses his black Amex with theatrical disdain. - Ch4: Storm traps him overnight; they drink tea/whisky, talk about books, childhoods, fame. - Ch5: Morning after; town wakes up and realises who he is; locals react in very Welsh ways. - Ch6: His publicist descends; she watches him perform for cameras and feels the mask slide back on. - Ch7: Crisis – old scandal story about him resurfaces just as they’re getting close; he expects her to google and hate him. - Ch8: Resolution – he chooses vulnerability over charm; she chooses to let someone in; they figure out what “real” looks like away from limelight. Alternative arcs - She’s the one with a secret (married? witness protection? wrote an anonymous cult novel?) - He’s trying to escape because he’s secretly ill / having a breakdown. - The bookshop is about to close; he offers to “save” it and she resents the white-knight act. - Slow reveal that she actually recognised him immediately but refused to give him the satisfaction. ### Pacing considerations - Ch1–3: almost real-time in the shop, tension from outside threat (paparazzi). - Ch4–5: one long night + morning after – classic “forced proximity” bottle episode. - Ch6–7: outside world intrudes, stakes rise. - Ch8: intimate, small-scale ending – no grand gestures, maybe just rain and honesty on a harbour wall. ### Themes to possibly weave in - Performance versus authenticity - The different kinds of Welsh identity (cosmopolitan celebrity vs rural continuity) - What “home” means when one of you can never really go home again - The eroticism of someone refusing to be dazzled # Reflection The strongest fuel for this story is the friction between his pathological need to be liked and her refusal to flatter him. That single dynamic can power the entire book. Every beat should circle back to it: he tries a line that would melt anyone else, she raises one eyebrow, and something genuine leaks out of him despite himself. Conversely, her interior monologue can betray how much his attention is secretly undoing her defences. The “famous actor hiding in Welsh bookshop” setup risks being twee or When Harry Met Sally… does Hay-on-Wye, so the tone has to stay grounded and a little spiky; too whimsical and we lose the stakes of his fragile ego. Keeping the town fictional (let’s call it Trallwn or Penmynydd) avoids real-world cliché and lets us control how “Welsh” it feels without parody. Forced-proximity overnight during a storm is almost mandatory for a short romance of this length; it’s the fastest way to get past banter into actual intimacy, but we have to earn it – the storm shouldn’t feel like a rom-com screenwriting cheat. Welsh weather will cooperate without apology. The eight-chapter structure is perfect for a slow escalation: - First three chapters can stay almost entirely inside the shop on that first afternoon. - Chapter four is the long, dangerous night where masks drop. - Chapters five through seven let reality crash in (paparazzi, exes, Google, money). - Chapter eight can be quiet again – a deliberate return to smallness after the circus. The risk is that he comes across as simply an arsehole who gets rewarded for being pretty. We need moments where his vulnerability feels earned and specific, not just “famous people are lonely too”. Giving him one private, non-performative kindness early (maybe he instinctively comforts the nervous teenage shop assistant without realising cameras are nowhere near) will plant the seed that there’s a real person under the charm-offensive. Her resistance has to feel active, not just tsundere cliché. Making her genuinely unimpressed because she’s seen charisma weaponised before (ex-partner who was a minor politician? famous poet father?) gives her immunity real roots. Dialogue is the prompt’s explicit demand, so almost every chapter should be 70–80% conversation, but the prose still has to do heavy lifting on physicality and subtext – the way he keeps touching the spine of a book he pretends to browse, the way she grips the counter when he steps too close. Best direction: keep it emotionally realistic, gently funny rather than farcical, unmistakably Welsh without ever explaining the Welshness, and let the romance bloom out of mutual recognition that the other person is the first in years who sees through the act – and still wants to stay.
Great now let's continue with planning the short story. Output in this format: # Intention <State your formulated intentions for the piece, synthesised from the the parts of the brainstorming session that worked, and avoiding the parts that didn't. Be explicit about the choices you have made about plot, voice, stylistic choices, things you intend to aim for & avoid.> # Chapter Planning <Write a brief chapter plan for all {n_chapters} chapters.>
Model Output:
# Intention This will be an intimate, dialogue-driven slow-burn romance of approximately 8,000 words total (8 chapters of ~1,000 words each). Tone: warm but unsentimental, quietly funny, grounded in specific Welsh textures (rain on slate, kettle always on, bilingual snippets that are never translated for the reader’s benefit). No grand cinematic gestures; the climax is two people deciding to be honest on a damp Tuesday afternoon. Core choices: - Setting: the fictional small town of Trallwn on Cardigan Bay—real enough to smell the salt, invented enough to protect the story from tourist-board clichés. - Actor: Rhys Maddox, 41, celebrated for intense, “difficult” roles, secretly addicted to being liked by everyone he meets. Charm is reflex, panic is private. - Bookshop owner: Elin Vaughan, 36, dry, precise, protective of silence. Recognised him the moment he walked in but refuses to give him the dopamine hit of recognition. - Central tension: his need to be adored versus her refusal to adore on command. Every charm offensive that fails chips away at his mask and, paradoxically, at hers. - Voice: third-person limited alternating between Rhys and Elin, but 75–80 % of the page is dialogue or near-dialogue (internal or spoken). Prose is spare, sensuous when it needs to be, never purple. - Welshness: lived-in, not decorative. Place names, occasional Welsh phrases, the rhythm of the sentences will feel Cymraeg-shaped even in English. - Heat level: grown-up, gradual, more suggestion than explicit until it earns explicitness. - Avoid: paparazzi car chases, evil tabloid villains, billionaire-saves-bookshop tropes, dramatic declarations in the rain, anyone saying “I’ve never felt this way before” without irony. # Chapter Planning Chapter 1 – The Bell That Sticks (First afternoon, ~60 real-time minutes) Rhys barrels in soaked, paparazzi two streets behind. Elin clocks who he is, says nothing, refuses to lock the door “for one man’s ego”. Their first sparring match over Dylan Thomas, expensive coats on radiators, and whether famous people are allowed to browse in peace. Ends with the paparazzi temporarily thrown off and Rhys realising he is more interested in the woman who isn’t impressed than in escaping. Chapter 2 – Upstairs Among the Poetry (Late afternoon) The shop’s upper floor is half living-space, half overspill stock. Rhys is “trapped” while the photographers circle the square. He tries every trick in his considerable arsenal; she dismantles each with surgical calm. Yet she keeps making tea. He discovers a first-edition R.S. Thomas she can’t bear to sell; she discovers he can quote it verbatim. Chemistry simmers in the silences between volleys. Chapter 3 – Closing Time That Doesn’t Close (Early evening) Rain starts hammering. The last customer leaves. Rhys offers to buy the entire shop’s stock if she’ll let him stay until the coast is clear. She laughs in his face, then—almost against her will—invites him to shelve books with her. Physical proximity while reaching for high shelves; first moment where hands brush and neither pretends it was accidental. Chapter 4 – Storm Overnight (Night into small hours) Atlantic storm cuts power and mobile signal. They are properly stuck. Whisky replaces tea. Conversation turns from books to childhoods: his Cardiff-council-estate mother who still calls him “cariad” in front of journalists; her father who left when she was nine and now sends postcards from film sets. First genuine vulnerability on both sides. Ends with them asleep on opposite sofas under shared blankets, not touching—yet. Chapter 5 – Morning After the World Finds Out (Next morning) Power returns; phones explode. A local teenager has tweeted. Trallwn wakes to the news that Rhys Maddox is in Siop Llyfrau Vaughan. Queue forms outside before Elin has finished making coffee. Rhys performs for the crowd; Elin watches the mask slam back on and feels something ache. She kicks everyone out at noon, including him—then immediately regrets it. Chapter 6 – The Mask and the Mirror (That afternoon and evening) Rhys’s publicist arrives with a plan to “spin” the overnight stay. Old photos of Rhys with an ex resurface online. He assumes Elin will google, believe the worst, and is shocked when she refuses to look. Instead she asks the question no interviewer ever has: “When you’re alone, who do you ring?” He has no answer. They walk the sea wall in horizontal rain; he tries to leave, she won’t let him run without finishing the conversation. Chapter 7 – The Quiet Offer (Following morning) The bookshop’s lease is actually ending—something Elin never mentioned. Rhys’s instinct is to throw money at it; hers is to refuse charity from someone who’ll be gone tomorrow. Instead he offers something riskier: to stay a full week, no phones, no pretence, and help her pack or save the shop on her terms. She counters with a single condition—if he can go twenty-four hours without trying to make her like him, she’ll consider it. He accepts, terrified. Chapter 8 – Twenty-Four Hours (Two days condensed) Rhys unplugged, no audience, no charm reflex. He is awkward, quiet, sometimes rude. Elin sees the frightened boy under the swagger and realises she has been performing too—performing self-sufficiency.
