On the final evening the paparazzi are long gone, the town has moved on, and the two of them sit on the floor of the empty shop surrounded by boxes. He asks, plainly, if he may kiss her. She answers by doing it first. Fade on small, ordinary future plans spoken aloud for the first time: train times, spare keys, whose turn to make supper.
With a view to making the writing more human, discuss how a human might approach this particular piece (given the original prompt). Discuss telltale LLM approaches to writing (generally) and ways they might not serve this particular piece. For example, common LLM failings are to write safely, or to always wrap things up with a bow, or trying to write impressively at the expense of readability. Then do a deep dive on the intention & plan, critiquing ways it might be falling into typical LLM tropes & pitfalls. Brainstorm ideas to make it more human. Be comprehensive. We aren't doing any rewriting of the plan yet, just critique & brainstorming.
Model Output:
### How a Human Writer Would Likely Approach This Piece A human who actually cared about this story (especially one with any Welsh blood or small-town experience) would start from bones, not from polish. They’d probably: - Spend a day just muttering dialogue aloud while walking the dog or doing dishes, trying to hear Rhys’s Cardiff-London mash-up cadence against Elin’s drier Ceredigion lilt. - Write the first chapter in one furious, messy sitting because they’re annoyed at how often celebrities are written as cartoon princes or villains. - Leave half-finished cups of tea everywhere and keep stopping to google things like “exact smell of a bookshop after rain” or “what does the A487 sound like at 3 a.m.” - Let the characters swear more than is strictly publishable because that’s how people talk when the mask slips. - Feel protective of Elin in a way that sometimes makes them write her too sharp, then go back and soften her because they don’t want her to become a Manic Pixie Dream Librarian. - Worry that the storm trope is lazy and try to justify it by remembering actual nights when the Teifi burst its banks. - Get genuinely angry if someone suggests making Rhys “redeem” himself with a grand gesture because that would betray the entire point. They would write from irritation and tenderness in equal measure, not from a checklist. ### Common LLM Telltale Habits That Would Ruin This Particular Story 1. **Safety & Symmetry** LLMs love both characters to be equally wounded/healed by the end, mirroring each other perfectly. Real humans are lopsided; one person is usually more broken, or more afraid, or simply worse at feelings. 2. **Polite, lyrical prose** The temptation to write “rain lashed the mullioned windows like the tears of forgotten gods” instead of “rain hammered the bastard roof again and the gutter was singing that same annoying three-note tune it always does.” 3. **Over-explaining emotions** Telling us “he felt seen for the first time” instead of just showing him fidget with a 50p coin for thirty silent seconds because he doesn’t know what to do with his hands when no one is watching. 4. **Charm as a superpower** LLMs often let Rhys’s charm actually work on side characters, so the reader is reassured he’s desirable. A human would let the teenage Saturday girl roll her eyes and say “Mam says you were pure sex in that vampire thing but you’re shorter than I thought.” 5. **Perfect rhythmic dialogue** Every line lands, every comeback is witty. Real conversation is full of “anyway,” “you know what I mean?,” sentences that trail off, overlaps, and sudden subject changes. 6. **Tidy bows** Chapter 8 ends with a neat kiss and a five-year plan. Humans know the story probably ends with one of them having to catch a train in 20 minutes and they still haven’t decided whose toothbrush stays. 7. **Exoticising Welshness** Sprinkling “cariad” and “cwtch” like garnish. A real Welsh writer would use the words when annoyed or drunk, not when trying to be cute. 8. **Earned vulnerability on schedule** LLMs pace the mask-dropping like clockwork: 22 % in chapter 4, 45 % in chapter 6, full nudity in chapter 8. Humans let it happen messily—maybe he cries in chapter 2 over something stupid like a cracked teapot and then clams up for days. ### Deep Critique of the Current Plan Through This Lens - Chapter 1: Still risks being too cute (“the bell that sticks” is already flirting with whimsy). A human might open with Rhys almost getting hit by a bus because he’s looking at his phone, then swearing in Welsh at the driver who knows exactly who he is. - Chapter 3’s “hands brush on ladder” moment is straight out of rom-com 2003. Humans would make it clumsier—he drops three hardbacks on her foot and they both say “shit” at exactly the same time. - Chapter 4’s whisky-and-childhood-confessions is the biggest danger zone. It’s one sleepless night away from every fanfic ever. The plan assumes they’ll talk neatly about their fathers. Real people get drunk and argue about whether Radio 1 has gone downhill, then one of them accidentally says something raw at 4:17 a.m. while hunting for biscuits. - Chapter 7’s “twenty-four hours without charm” challenge is clever-clever. It smells like the writer showing they’ve read pride-and-prejudice fan forums. A human might have Elin simply say “Stop performing, I’m tired” and watch him deflate like a punctured lilo. - The entire lease-is-ending subplot feels like a contrived third-act obstacle. Real small-town bookshops close for boring reasons (business rates, damp) and people don’t dramatically save them; they just drink in the pub and feel sad. - The ending as planned is far too composed. Humans would probably cut to black on something unfinished: one of them staring at an unsent text, or the kettle boiling while they both pretend they’re not listening to the silence. ### Brainstorming Ways to Humanise It (Without Rewriting Yet) - Let dialogue overrun and stutter. Half-sentences, regionalisms, people talking over each other. - Give Rhys one genuinely ugly habit (picks at his cuticles until they bleed when anxious; leaves wet towels on the floor because someone always used to pick them up for him). - Let Elin be wrong sometimes—snappish when she’s just hungover, unfairly mean about one of his films because she once fancied the co-star. - Include banal details that no one asked for: the shop radio only gets Radio Cymru and Magic Soul, the toilet flush needs two hands, the neighbour’s dog barks every time someone says the word “tax.” - Make the weather inconvenient rather than romantic—leaking roof, cold that gets into your bones, socks that never dry. - Let side characters be unimpressed or openly hostile. The old fisherman tells Rhys his last film was “rubbish” and asks if he wants a cup of tea anyway. - Allow moments of nothing happening: ten minutes of shelving books in complete silence because neither trusts themselves to speak. - Have Rhys google himself at 3 a.m. and read something that makes him feel sick; never tell us exactly what it was. - Let them almost kiss in chapter 5 and then not—because someone’s phone buzzes, or the moment just curdles, and the miss is more erotic than the kiss would have been. - Give Elin a cat that hates Rhys on sight and keeps pissing in his shoes. Never resolve it; the cat wins. - End on a line that feels thrown away, not sculpted. Something like “I’ve got milk in for tomorrow if you’re staying” and the story stops before we hear the answer. Essentially: trust awkwardness, ugliness, and anticlimax more than grace, rhythm, or closure. That’s where the blood is.
Ok now with these considerations in mind, formulate the final plan for the a humanlike, compelling short piece in {n_chapters} chapters. Bear in mind the constraints of the piece (each chapter is just 1000 words). Above all things, the plan must serve the original prompt. We will use the same format as before: # Intention <State your formulated intentions for the piece, synthesised from the the parts of the brainstorming session that worked, and avoiding the parts that didn't. Be explicit about the choices you have made about plot, voice, stylistic choices, things you intend to aim for & avoid.> # Chapter Planning <Write a brief chapter plan for all {n_chapters} chapters.>
Model Output:
# Intention This is a small, scruffy, Welsh-hearted romance about two people who are terrible at being liked in the ways they secretly want to be. It will feel overheard rather than written, like someone left a dictaphone on the counter of Siop Llyfrau Vaughan. Length stays tight: 8 × ~1,000 words, no padding. Everything must serve the original prompt: the famous actor hiding in the bookshop, the rogue with the fragile ego, the owner who refuses to flatter him, the charged conversation that derails his charm and quietly undoes her. Key decisions - Town: Trallwn, invented, grey, salty, end-of-season. - Rhys Maddox: 41, famous for being “difficult” on screen, desperate to be loved off it. Voice slides between polished English for cameras and sudden Cardiff Valleys when he’s rattled. - Elin Vaughan: 36, local, sharp, exhausted by charisma. Doesn’t perform warmth, but isn’t cruel; just refuses to give him the drug he’s after. - Prose: short sentences, half-finished thoughts, local clutter. Dialogue 80 %, internal 15 %, description 5 % and only when it smells or leaks or annoys. - Welshness: lived, not brandished. Phrases appear when people are tired or cross, never explained. - Heat: adult, awkward, mostly off-page or half-lit. - Absolutely no: grand rescues, perfect rain-kisses, speeches, tidy futures, evil paparazzi, shop saved by celebrity chequebook. Things I will chase - The ache of being seen without applause - The sexiness of someone refusing to be impressed - The particular loneliness of a wet Tuesday in west Wales - Dialogue that stumbles, overlaps, goes nowhere useful, then suddenly cuts deep - Small, unfinished gestures instead of cinematic ones Things I will murder on sight - “For the first time in his life he felt truly seen” - Anyone calling anyone cariad to be cute - A kiss that fixes everything - The cat becoming a metaphor # Chapter Planning Chapter 1 – Wet Dog Energy Rhys crashes in, soaked, swearing in two accents. Elin recognises him instantly, says nothing, refuses to lock the door “for one man’s drama.” He tries every version of charming; she counters with prices and damp radiators. Ends with him realising the photographers have gone and he still doesn’t want to leave. Chapter 2 – The Bell Rings Twice Upstairs is half stockroom, half Elin’s overflow life: kettle, cat food, laundry drying on a maiden. Rain starts. Rhys keeps talking to fill silence; Elin keeps not helping him. He finds a battered R.S. Thomas she’s priced at £3; she won’t let him pay £300 for it. First flicker that her indifference is effort, not absence. Chapter 3 – Shelving as Foreplay Shop should close at five. It’s six-thirty and still pouring. They shelve paperbacks together because doing nothing is worse. He drops a stack on her foot, apologises too much, then stops apologising. Hands brush. Neither acknowledges it. A silence lasts thirty-seven seconds and feels dangerous. Chapter 4 – Power Cut, Whisky, 3:47 a.m. Lights die. Phones die. They drink cheap blended whisky from chipped mugs. Conversation lurches from stupid (best service-station pasty) to brutal (his mother still clips bad reviews, her dad phones drunk from LA every Christmas). He cries about something small and pretends it’s the peat in the whisky. They fall asleep on separate sofas, one blanket short. Chapter 5 – Trallwn Wakes Up Hungover Teenager from the chip shop has tweeted. Queue outside by 9 a.m. Rhys switches to red-carpet mode; Elin watches him hand out selfies like communion and feels something sour. She clears the shop at noon, tells him to leave with the rest. He goes. She immediately hates the quiet. Chapter 6 – Sea Wall, Horizontal Rain He comes back after dark because the B&B woman asked for a selfie and he couldn’t breathe. They walk the harbour wall getting soaked again. Old stories about him resurface online; he waits for her to ask. She doesn’t. Instead: “Who do you ring when it’s bad?” He has no one. The almost-kiss happens here and is ruined by a gull shitting on his shoulder. Chapter 7 – The Stupid Bet Next morning the landlord has stuck a notice on the door: lease ends in six weeks. Rhys offers money; she tells him to piss off with his hero complex. Instead he says he’ll stay a week, no phone, no charm, help her pack or fight or whatever she actually wants. She says fine, but if he makes her laugh on purpose even once, he’s out. He agrees, terrified. Chapter 8 – The Twenty-Four Hours That Took Three Days Time compresses. Rhys is quiet, clumsy, picks at his thumbs, leaves mugs everywhere. Elin catches herself missing the noise. The cat pisses in his shoe and she doesn’t apologise. On the last night the shop is half-empty, radio only gets static. He asks, plain Welsh, “D’you want me to stay gone?” She answers by kissing him first, badly, teeth bumping. They laugh, horrified. Fade on them trying to find a clean mug for tea while the kettle screams and tomorrow is still undecided.
Perfect. Now with the outline more crystallised, and bearing in mind the discussion on human writing vs LLM pitfalls, we will flesh out our characters. Lets go through each of our main characters: - Write about their background, personality, idiosyncracies, flaws. Be specific and come up with examples to anchor & ground the character's profile (both core and trivial) - Briefly describe their physicality: appearance, how they carry themselves, express, interact with the world. - Concisely detail their motives, allegiences and existing relationships. Think from the perspective of the character as a real breathing thinking feeling individual in this world. - Write a couple quotes of flavour dialogue / internal monologue from the character to experiment with their voice. Output like this: # Character 1 name <character exploration> # Character 2 name <character exploration> etc
Model Output:
# Rhys Maddox **Background** Born 1983 on a Caerphilly council estate, mum cleaned offices, dad was “between jobs” until he wasn’t there at all. Got spotted doing drama GCSE monologue in the school canteen, rest is red-carpet history. First big role at 24, Oscar nomination at 29, tabloid monster by 31. Hasn’t lived in Wales full-time since he was 17 but still says “tidy” when drunk. Keeps a flat in London he never uses and a storage unit full of his mam’s old furniture he can’t bring himself to sell. **Personality & flaws** Pathologically allergic to being disliked. Will spend forty minutes in a petrol station making the cashier laugh because she looked bored. Remembers nothing about scripts but everything about who was kind to him on set. Picks skin around thumbnails until they bleed when anxious—then hides his hands like a guilty schoolboy. Lies about tiny things (says he’s read books he’s only seen the film of) because the idea of someone thinking less of him is physical pain. Can turn charm on like a tap, but when it fails he freefalls into sulky child. Terrified of silence. **Idiosyncrasies** Buys people presents within an hour of meeting them. Calls everyone “love” when nervous. Says “fair play” genuinely and ironically in the same breath. Still flinches if a phone camera flashes unexpectedly. Sleeps with the telly on because actual quiet makes him panic. **Physicality** 5′10″ but convinced he’s short because co-stars wear lifts. Dark hair that’s going silver at the temples; lets makeup cover it for work but not real life. Permanent half-smile like he’s waiting for applause. Expensive coats, cheap socks with holes. Hands always moving—fiddling, gesturing, reaching for someone’s arm to keep them listening. **Motives & loyalties** Wants to be the version of himself his mam clips out of Hello! magazine. Loyal to her and to three school friends who still take the piss out of him mercilessly (they’re the only people he never performs for). Currently has no one he calls when a red-top runs a bad story at 2 a.m. **Voice samples** External: “No, honestly, lock the door if you want—I’m not that bothered, like. Only if it’s easier for you, Elin, I mean it.” Internal: *She hasn’t smiled once. Not once. What the fuck is wrong with me today?* Drunk at 3 a.m.: “I was good in that film, wasn’t I? Tell me I was good.” # Elin Vaughan **Background** Raised in Trallwn, only child. Dad was a semi-famous poet who buggered off to America when she was nine; still sends postcards with one-line poems she never answers. Read English at Aberystwyth, stayed for a boy, came home when her mum got sick, never really left again. Took over the bookshop eight years ago. Mum died last year. Lease is up because the landlord wants holiday lets. **Personality & flaws** Dry doesn’t begin to cover it. Weaponises silence. Hates being looked at for too long—crosses her arms like armour. Secretly sentimental (keeps every birthday card since 1994 in a shoebox). Allergic to bullshit but lonely enough that she sometimes lets the bullshit talk just for the noise. Can be cruel when cornered; regrets it instantly but would rather die than apologise first. **Idiosyncrasies** Makes tea too strong on purpose because weak tea offends her. Writes prices in books with a fountain pen and blots them like it’s 1953. Has a laugh like a sneeze—she tries to smother it. Swears in Welsh when English feels too theatrical. Feeds the cat tins of tuna she can’t afford. **Physicality** 5′6″, dark red-brown hair always escaping a knot. Pale in a way that startles people in summer. Wears men’s shirts and cardigans because they hide her. Moves like someone perpetually apologising for taking up space until she’s angry—then suddenly fills the room. Bites the inside of her cheek when thinking. **Motives & loyalties** Mainly wants to be left alone, except when she desperately doesn’t. Loyal to the shop, to her mum’s memory, to three women in the town who’ve known her since primary school and still call her “Elin fach” even though she’s 36. Hasn’t had sex in two years and pretends she’s fine with it. **Voice samples** External: “If you wanted peace and quiet you should’ve picked a library, not a shop that’s open to the public.” Internal: *Christ, he’s even better-looking when he’s miserable. Stop staring, you absolute clown.* To the cat, fondly: “You little bastard, that was my last tin.”

Chapter 1

Great. Now let's write this. Follow your plan. Output chapter 1, without any additional commentary. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The bell above the door gave its usual half-hearted clack, stuck halfway like it couldn’t decide whether to announce anyone or not. Rhys Maddox shouldered in, dripping, coat worth more than the shop’s yearly rent plastered to him like wet newspaper. He smelled of cold rain and expensive aftershave fighting a losing battle. “Shut the door, you’re letting the Atlantic in,” Elin said without looking up from the till. She was pricing a stack of dog-eared Ruth Rendells, fountain pen scratching. Rhys kicked it closed behind him, leaned his back against it like he was barricading. “There’s three of them out there with cameras longer than my arm. If you lock it they’ll know I’m here.” Elin flicked her eyes up. Recognised him in the time it took to blink, gave nothing away. “I don’t lock the door for one man’s drama. Shop’s open till five.” He laughed, too loud, the laugh he used on chat shows when the host made a dig. “Fair play. I like a woman with principles.” He peeled the coat off, looked for somewhere to put it that wasn’t the floor. Everywhere was books. “Radiator by the poetry,” she said. “Mind the cat.” There was no cat in sight. Rhys draped the coat anyway; water ran down the pipes like it was crying on his behalf. He turned back, hands already spreading in apology or appeal. “Look, five minutes. Ten. I’ll buy something. I’ll buy loads. Just don’t chuck me back out there yet.” Elin capped the pen. “You’re wet enough for all of us. Browsing’s free. Buying’s extra.” He flashed the grin that had sold cologne in three continents. It bounced off her like hail off slate. Something in his shoulders twitched. “Right. I’m Rhys, by the way.” “I know who you are.” The grin faltered, recovered. “Occupational hazard.” “Occupational something.” She moved out from behind the counter, started straightening a shelf that didn’t need it. “Local history’s at the back if you’re after somewhere to hide. Less footfall.” Rhys followed her like a dog that wasn’t sure it was invited. “You always this welcoming?” “Only on days ending in Y.” She reached up to nudge a crooked guidebook; the cardigan slipped off one shoulder. Pale skin, freckles, bra strap the colour of strong tea. Rhys looked too long and knew it. He tried again, softer. “Rough day out there.” “Rough century by the looks of you.” That made him laugh for real, a short surprised bark. “Christ. Don’t hold back.” “Never have.” She turned, leaned a hip against the shelf. “So. You going to stand there dripping or actually look at something?” He glanced around like he’d only just noticed the walls were books. “Surprise me.” Elin reached without looking, pulled a slim paperback from the Welsh-interest shelf. R.S. Thomas. Cover price pencilled in her neat slanted hand: £3. “Here,” she said. “He doesn’t suffer fools.” Rhys took it, thumbed the pages like they might bite. “I know this one. ‘A Peasant.’ I had to do it for audition once. Director said I made him sound sexy. Reckoned that was wrong.” “It was,” Elin said. He laughed again, quieter. “You don’t like me very much, do you?” “I don’t know you.” “That usually works in my favour.” “Clearly.” Silence pooled. Outside, gulls screamed over the harbour. Rhys shifted weight, coat dripping steady on the radiator. He tried the grin again, smaller. “Can I at least buy this? Three quid feels insulting.” “It’s what it’s worth.” “To you, maybe.” He dug out a wallet that probably cost more than her car. “I’ll give you three hundred. For the inconvenience.” Elin looked at the notes like they smelled bad. “Put it away, Hollywood. I’m not a charity.” “Never said you were.” “You didn’t have to.” He closed the wallet, slid it back in his pocket. Hands now with nothing to do, went to the coat, the hair, the thumbnail already ragged. “Five minutes, then. I’ll be quiet as a mouse.” Elin went back to the till. “Mice don’t talk this much.” Rhys opened his mouth, closed it again. He took the R.S. Thomas to the armchair by the window, the one with the spring that stabbed you in the arse. Sat. Opened the book upside down. Realised. Turned it. Stared at the page like it might save him. Elin watched from under her fringe. The man was vibrating with the need to be liked and she wasn’t giving him a millimetre. It was almost cruel. Almost. Outside, footsteps splashed past, slowed, moved on. The paparazzi losing interest or getting cold. Rhys’s shoulders dropped a fraction. He spoke without looking up. “You’ve got a sticking bell.” “Tell me something I don’t know.” Another silence. Rain eased to a mutter. He tried one last time, voice softer, almost Cardiff again. “Thanks. For not locking the door.” Elin shrugged. “Wasn’t for you.” Rhys smiled at the poem, small and private, like she’d handed him something after all.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 1)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This is a genuinely impressive piece of opening chapter writing. Let me assess it carefully across the relevant dimensions. CRAFT AND DIALOGUE The dialogue is the chapter's strongest element and its reason for being. The exchanges are sharp, asymmetrical, and character-revealing without being expository. "Only on days ending in Y" is a perfectly calibrated line—dry without being theatrical. "Rough century by the looks of you" lands as a genuine surprise. The rhythm of Rhys trying charm and Elin deflecting it is executed with real skill: each volley is distinct, each deflection slightly different in texture. The dialogue doesn't repeat itself structurally, which is harder than it looks. The voices are distinct. Rhys's slides between polished and rattled, as planned. Elin's is economical to the point of weaponisation. Neither character speaks in the other's register. METAPHOR AND PROSE STYLE "coat worth more than the shop's yearly rent plastered to him like wet newspaper" — this works. "He smelled of cold rain and expensive aftershave fighting a losing battle" — also works, though slightly more familiar. "The grin that had sold cologne in three continents. It bounced off her like hail off slate." — this is excellent. Concrete, earned, not overwrought. "water ran down the pipes like it was crying on his behalf" — this is the one moment where the prose reaches a little too hard. It's not terrible, but it's the chapter's most self-conscious metaphor. "Elin watched from under her fringe. The man was vibrating with the need to be liked and she wasn't giving him a millimetre. It was almost cruel. Almost." — This is the chapter's most tell-don't-show moment. The observation is accurate but slightly over-explained. The "Almost. Almost." construction is a minor tic that signals writerly self-consciousness. FOLLOWING THE PLAN The chapter follows its plan faithfully: Rhys crashes in soaked, Elin recognises him and says nothing, he tries charm, she counters with prices and damp radiators, it ends with him realising the photographers have gone and still not wanting to leave. The R.S. Thomas book appears as planned. The £300 offer and her refusal are executed. The ending — "Rhys smiled at the poem, small and private, like she'd handed him something after all" — is exactly the right note to close on, and earns its slight warmth. CHARACTER CONSISTENCY Rhys's thumbnail-picking anxiety, his sliding accent, his compulsive charm, his wallet, his upside-down book — all consistent with the profile. Elin's fountain pen, her weaponised silence, her refusal to perform warmth — all consistent. The bra strap detail is well-placed: it establishes physical awareness without being gratuitous, and Rhys "looked too long and knew it" is exactly right for his character. WEAKNESSES The "Almost. Almost." construction is a minor but real flaw — it's the kind of thing a writer does when they don't quite trust the scene to carry its own weight. The "crying on his behalf" pipe metaphor is the chapter's one genuinely strained moment. The opening description of the bell is slightly over-written ("stuck halfway like it couldn't decide whether to announce anyone or not") — it's trying a little hard to establish tone before the tone has been earned. These are small complaints against a chapter that largely succeeds at something difficult: establishing two distinct, compelling characters through almost pure dialogue, in a short space, without resorting to backstory dumps or obvious emotional signposting. OVERALL This is confident, controlled work. The Welshness is lived rather than brandished. The chemistry is present without being announced. The ending is earned. The chapter does what it sets out to do and does it with genuine craft. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Compelling Plot: 14 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 3 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 4 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Characters Consistent with Profile: 18 Followed Chapter Plan: 18 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 18

Chapter 2

Ok now chapter 2. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The rain settled in properly, drumming on the slate roof like it had a personal grudge. Rhys had been downstairs twenty minutes and already looked homeless in a two-thousand-pound coat. Elin jerked her chin at the narrow stairs behind the counter. “Up there’s drier. Kettle works. Don’t touch the boxes marked Private.” He followed because there was nothing else to do. The stairs creaked like they were telling tales. At the top was a long attic room running the length of the shop: shelves bowing under unsorted stock, a clothes maiden draped with greying bras and socks, a kettle on a camping stove, one skylight rattling in the wind. A black-and-white cat glared from a pile of proof copies, tail twitching like a metronome. “Make yourself useful,” Elin said. “Tea’s in the Welsh Dragon tin. Milk’s off, we take it black like adults.” Rhys peeled off the coat at last, hung it on the back of a chair that already had three cardigans. He rolled his sleeves (expensive watch, scarred knuckles) and filled the kettle because doing something felt better than standing still. Elin dragged a box of paperbacks towards the shelves. “You can hand me those. Crime section needs alphabetising and I’m sick of looking at it.” He crouched opposite her, knees cracking. “Yes, boss.” “Don’t.” “Don’t what?” “Call me boss like it’s cute.” He handed her a Val McDermid, careful, like it might explode. “What should I call you then?” “Elin’s fine. Or nothing. Nothing works.” They worked. Rain hammered. The cat stalked over and sat on Rhys’s foot, weighing up whether to bite him. He froze. “That’s Nel. She hates everyone, you’re honoured.” “She’s vibrating.” “She purrs like a broken generator.” Rhys risked a finger. Nel allowed it, then savaged his cuff. He laughed under his breath. Elin watched the side of his face. “You’re quieter up here.” “No audience.” She snorted. “Poor you.” He glanced up. “You always this kind to strangers?” “Only the ones who drip on my floor.” He passed her three books at once, clumsy. Their fingers brushed; neither moved fast enough to pretend it was accident. The moment stretched, ridiculous. Elin took the books and turned away first. The kettle clicked off. Rhys made tea too strong because he copied how she’d done it downstairs. He handed her a mug that said Cardigan Bay Dolphin Survey 2009 – one handle missing. “Ta,” she said, and meant it more than she wanted to. They drank leaning against opposite shelves. Steam fogged the skylight. Rhys nodded at the R.S. Thomas still in his back pocket. “Three quid. You robbed me.” “You offered three hundred.” “Still robbery.” Elin shrugged. “Some things aren’t for sale.” “Everything’s for sale. Just depends how lonely the seller is.” She gave him a look sharp enough to shave with. “Speak for yourself.” He rubbed the back of his neck, caught himself doing the thumbnail thing, shoved both hands in his pockets. “Sorry. Comes out sounding cuntish.” “Accurate, though.” Silence again, but softer now. Nel jumped onto a box and started washing her arse with commitment. Rhys tried a different tack. “You live up here?” “Some nights. When the flat’s too quiet.” She stopped, annoyed at herself for answering. He nodded like he understood exactly. “Hotels get like that. Even the big ones. You can hear the minibar humming through the walls.” Elin sipped tea, said nothing. He kept going, quieter. “Sometimes I leave the telly on Cartoon Network so it feels like there’s kids in the next room. Pathetic, isn’t it?” She looked at him then, really looked. The famous face looked suddenly young, wet hair sticking up like a kid dragged out of the sea. “Little bit,” she said, but her voice had lost its edge. Rhys smiled at the floorboards. “You’re brutal.” “Someone has to be.” He moved to the skylight, peered out. “Still pissing down. They’ve definitely gone, haven’t they?” “Unless they’ve learned to breathe underwater.” He laughed, short. Turned back. “I could go. If you want.” Elin set her mug on a stack of Ian Rankins. “Shop’s still open downstairs. You’re a customer.” “That’s not what I asked.” She met his eyes. Held them. “You wanting permission to stay or permission to leave?” He opened his mouth, closed it. Didn’t know. Elin picked up another book, slid it into place with unnecessary force. “Doesn’t matter. Rain’s doing what it wants. We’re just in its way.” Rhys watched her hands (ink under one nail, paper cut on the thumb) and felt something shift in his chest like a box falling off a shelf. Nel jumped down, stalked between them, sat squarely in the middle and stared at Rhys like he was late with rent. He looked from cat to woman. “Think she’s telling me to piss off.” “Nel’s honest,” Elin said. “Learn from her.” But she didn’t move to open the stairs. And he didn’t move to leave. The rain kept falling, steady and indifferent, and upstairs the kettle cooled and the books waited and neither of them could quite remember what silence was supposed to feel like.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 2)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This is a strong piece of work that largely delivers on its ambitious plan. Let me examine it carefully across the relevant dimensions. VOICE AND DIALOGUE: The dialogue is the chapter's greatest strength. Both characters speak in genuinely distinct registers. Elin's lines are clipped, deflecting, occasionally weaponised ("Accurate, though"). Rhys's lines have that quality the profile promised — sliding between polished and rattled, performing charm and then catching himself. The exchange about "permission to stay or permission to leave" is particularly well-handled: it's the kind of line that does real work without announcing itself. The Cartoon Network confession lands because it's specific and slightly pathetic in exactly the right way. The dialogue rarely feels written; it feels overheard, which was the stated intention. CHARACTER CONSISTENCY: Both characters are faithful to their profiles. Rhys does the thumbnail thing, catches himself, hides his hands. He lies by omission (the charm tap turning on, then failing). He fills silence compulsively. Elin weaponises silence, crosses her arms (metaphorically — she turns away first after the finger-brush), makes tea too strong, and gives him a look "sharp enough to shave with." The moment where her voice "lost its edge" after the Cartoon Network confession is exactly the right calibration — she's not immune, she's effortful about it, and the effort occasionally slips. This is precisely what the prompt asked for. PROSE QUALITY: The prose is tight and largely earns its effects. "Stairs creaked like they were telling tales" is a mild cliché but not offensive. "A look sharp enough to shave with" is a well-worn simile but deployed with enough confidence to pass. The description of Nel purring "like a broken generator" is good. The final paragraph — "the kettle cooled and the books waited and neither of them could quite remember what silence was supposed to feel like" — is the chapter's most overtly "literary" moment and it mostly earns it, though "neither of them could quite remember what silence was supposed to feel like" is slightly overwritten given the chapter's otherwise spare register. FORCED POETRY / PURPLE PROSE: There are a few moments where the prose reaches slightly too hard. "Something shift in his chest like a box falling off a shelf" is trying to be both mundane and resonant simultaneously — it's a clever idea but the execution is a touch clunky. The opening line ("drumming on the slate roof like it had a personal grudge") is a familiar personification of rain, though "personal grudge" gives it some personality. These are minor infractions in an otherwise disciplined piece. CHAPTER PLAN ADHERENCE: The plan called for: kettle, cat food, laundry drying on a maiden (all present), rain starting (present), Rhys talking to fill silence (present), Elin not helping him (present), the R.S. Thomas at £3 (present and well-used), and "first flicker that her indifference is effort, not absence" (present — the voice losing its edge moment). The chapter follows its plan faithfully without feeling mechanical about it. PLOT / ENGAGEMENT: For a chapter where essentially nothing happens — two people make tea and shelve books — it maintains genuine tension. The finger-brush is handled with admirable restraint (neither moved fast enough to pretend it was accident). The ending, with both of them failing to leave, is earned rather than manufactured. WEAKNESSES: The "box falling off a shelf" metaphor is the chapter's weakest moment. The final paragraph slightly over-explains the emotional state. Nel as a character risks becoming exactly the kind of cat-as-metaphor the plan said it would murder on sight — though she's used with enough comic specificity (washing her arse, sitting squarely between them) that she stays on the right side of that line. The chapter is perhaps slightly too comfortable — it doesn't quite have the dangerous thirty-seven-second silence the plan promised for Chapter 3, but that's Chapter 3's job. OVERALL: This is accomplished, disciplined work. It respects its characters, trusts its dialogue, and resists the temptation to over-explain or over-poeticise. The Welshness is lived rather than brandished. The chemistry is present without being announced. Minor prose infelicities prevent a top score, but this is genuinely good writing. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Compelling Plot: 14 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 3 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 4 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 5 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Characters Consistent with Profile: 18 Followed Chapter Plan: 17 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 3

Ok now chapter 3. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The clock downstairs had chimed five, then half-past, then given up and gone quiet. The rain was no longer rain; it was weather with commitment issues. Elin flicked the sign to CLOSED out of habit, then didn’t lock the door. She told herself it was because the catch stuck worse when it was wet. Rhys was still upstairs, moving boxes like he’d been hired by the hour. She could hear him humming something tuneless, off-key, probably one of those songs that had been in the charts when he was fifteen and still living in Caerphilly. She went up. He’d taken his jumper off; the T-shirt underneath clung where the rain had got him earlier. Shoulders broader than the posters suggested. She looked anyway. “You missed last orders,” she said. He straightened, wiped dust off his hands onto his jeans. “Thought you’d chuck me out at five sharp.” “Thought about it.” “And?” “Couldn’t be arsed walking you to the door.” He grinned, quick and crooked. “Lazy. I like it.” Elin rolled her eyes, but the corner of her mouth twitched traitorously. She pointed at the last tower of paperbacks. “Those need to go on the top shelf. I’m five-foot-six on a good day.” Rhys looked at the shelf, looked at her. “Step-ladder’s downstairs.” “Or you could be useful for once.” He moved before she finished the sentence. They worked side by side, passing books up. The attic smelled of damp paper and tea and whatever cologne he’d put on three countries ago. Every time he reached past her the air shifted. “Left a bit,” she said. “No, your other left.” “Bossy.” “You offered.” He stretched for a high gap; the T-shirt rode up. A thin scar curved under his ribs, pale against tanned skin. Elin’s hand paused mid-pass. He noticed, dropped the hem quick. “Appendix,” he muttered. “Came out in Marbella. Sun was nice.” She handed him the next book without comment. Ten minutes in, the pile was gone. They stood in the sudden space like people who’d forgotten why they started. Rhys rubbed the back of his neck. “Done. Can I go home now, miss?” “Don’t know where home is for you, do I?” He laughed under his breath. “Fair.” Elin turned to go downstairs. The bulb on the landing flickered once, twice, held. She stopped on the top step. “You can come down if you want. Or stay brooding up here with Nel. Your choice.” Rhys glanced at the cat, who was licking her arse again with theatrical commitment. “Think I’ll risk downstairs.” They went single file. Halfway down he misjudged the narrow turn, shoulder brushing the wall. A small avalanche of loose poetry paperbacks slid from a shelf and smacked him in the chest. He caught most, dropped three. One landed open on the stairs: Margaret Atwood, page dog-eared at a line about cages. “Shit, sorry—” Elin crouched to help. Their heads almost knocked. She picked up the Atwood, closed it gently. “Careful,” she said. “Some of us like our books unconcussed.” He looked at her hands on the cover, then at her face, closer than manners allowed. “You’ve got ink on your cheek.” “Where?” She swiped the wrong side. “Here.” He reached, thumb brushing just below her cheekbone. Stopped. Left it there half a second too long. Elin didn’t move. Could feel the heat of his hand like a match held close. He dropped it first. “Sorry.” “Don’t be.” They gathered the rest in silence. When they stood again the stairs felt smaller. She went down ahead of him; he followed so close she could feel him breathing. In the shop the lights were still on, orange and tired. The radiator ticked. Elin went behind the counter out of habit, needing the width of it between them. Rhys hovered. “I should— I mean, they’ve definitely gone now, haven’t they?” Elin glanced out the window. Street empty except for one sodden crisp packet doing ballet in the wind. “Looks like.” He nodded too many times. Picked up the R.S. Thomas from the armchair, turned it over like he’d forgotten it was his. Elin watched him fidget. “You’re twitching.” “Am I?” “Worse than Nel when the vet’s mentioned.” He laughed, rubbed his face. “I hate this bit.” “What bit?” “The bit where I leave and you forget I exist in about four minutes.” Elin leaned her elbows on the counter. “Who says I’ll forget?” His eyes snapped to hers. Hope, ridiculous and naked. She felt cruel for enjoying it. “I’ve got a memory like a steel trap for idiots.” The hope dimmed to something smaller, warmer. “I’ll take it.” Silence pooled again, but different now; thick, like soup left too long on the hob. Rhys put the book down carefully. “Can I use your loo? Before I brave the Atlantic again?” “Upstairs. Mind the flush, it needs coaxing.” He went. Elin listened to his footsteps overhead, the groan of pipes, the unmistakable sound of someone pissing like a racehorse. Normal. Human. She caught herself smiling at nothing and stopped. When he came down he’d splashed water on his face; hair stuck up in spikes. He looked about nineteen. “Right,” he said. “Thanks. For— you know. Everything.” Elin shrugged. “You moved boxes. We’re even.” He moved towards the door, stopped with his hand on the handle. Turned back. “Elin.” “What?” “If I came in tomorrow— not hiding, just— came in. Would you throw me out?” She considered him for a long moment. The radiator hissed like it wanted to answer for her. “Depends,” she said at last. “You planning on buying anything or just taking up oxygen?” He grinned, the real one, tired and crooked and suddenly shy. “I’ve got a list.” “Then maybe not.” He opened the door. Rain had eased to a spiteful drizzle. He stepped out, turned up his collar, looked back once. “Night, Elin Vaughan.” “Night, Rhys Maddox.” The bell gave its half-arsed clack as he left. Elin stood listening to his footsteps fade, then went and locked the door properly. Leaned her forehead against the cold glass. “Idiot,” she said to the empty street. Wasn’t sure which one of them she meant.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 3)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter 3 of this piece is a confident, controlled piece of romantic tension writing that largely delivers on its ambitious plan. Let me assess it carefully across the relevant dimensions. STRENGTHS: The dialogue is the chapter's greatest asset. It's genuinely distinct between the two characters — Rhys's nervous verbosity ("Can I go home now, miss?"), his transparent need for approval ("the bit where I leave and you forget I exist in about four minutes"), and Elin's weaponised brevity ("Couldn't be arsed walking you to the door") all feel earned and consistent with the profiles. The voices don't bleed into each other, which is harder to achieve than it looks. The physical choreography of the scene is well-handled. The scar reveal, the ink-on-cheek moment, the books cascading on the stairs — these are small, specific, believable beats that generate heat without melodrama. The thumb-on-cheekbone moment is the chapter's best: "Stopped. Left it there half a second too long." That restraint is exactly right. The chapter follows its plan faithfully: shelving as foreplay, the hand brush, the dangerous silence, the ending with him asking to return. The plan promised "hands brush, neither acknowledges it" and "a silence lasts thirty-seven seconds and feels dangerous" — the execution delivers the spirit of these beats even if the thirty-seven seconds becomes a more diffuse "thick, like soup left too long on the hob." Character consistency is strong. Rhys's naked hope when she says she won't forget him, then the quick recovery when she deflates it — "The hope dimmed to something smaller, warmer" — is psychologically precise for a man with a fragile ego. Elin's "felt cruel for enjoying it" is exactly the self-aware, slightly guilty interiority the profile demands. The Welshness is lived-in rather than performed. No one explains anything. The town, the rain, the radiator — they're texture, not tourism. WEAKNESSES: Some metaphors don't quite land. "Silence pooled again, but different now; thick, like soup left too long on the hob" is the chapter's most strained image — soup left too long goes wrong, gets a skin on it, burns. It's trying to evoke something warm and slow but the connotations are slightly off. "Weather with commitment issues" in the opening is clever but feels like it's reaching for a Twitter-ready line rather than serving the prose. "Like a match held close" for the heat of his hand is functional but not distinguished — it's the kind of simile that appears in competent romantic fiction without being memorable. The Nel-licking-her-arse joke is deployed twice in the chapter (once upstairs, once in the "worse than Nel when the vet's mentioned" line). The second use is fine, but the first is slightly gratuitous — it's there for a laugh rather than doing double duty. The ending — "Idiot. Wasn't sure which one of them she meant" — is the chapter's most conventional beat. It's well-executed but it's a familiar romantic comedy closing move. The plan promised to avoid the predictable, and this lands just inside the border of expected. The "pissing like a racehorse" moment is a bold humanising choice that mostly works, but "she caught herself smiling at nothing and stopped" is slightly too on-the-nose about her internal state — it tells us what we've already been shown. OVERALL: This is skilled, disciplined romantic writing that earns its warmth through restraint. The dialogue is the standout element. The chapter's weaknesses are minor — a few metaphors that don't fully land, one too-familiar closing beat — but they don't undermine the chapter's considerable achievement. It's doing exactly what the prompt and plan asked for, and doing it with genuine craft. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Compelling Plot: 14 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 4 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 6 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Characters Consistent with Profile: 18 Followed Chapter Plan: 17 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 18

Chapter 4

Ok now chapter 4. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The lights died at twenty-past eleven with a flat, insulted click. One second the shop was orange and humming, next second blacker than the inside of a whale. The storm had finally bitten through the cable that fed half the street. Elin swore once, Welsh and filthy. Rhys’s voice floated down the stairwell. “That normal round here?” “Only when the weather’s feeling dramatic.” She found the torch under the counter by feel, clicked it on. The beam caught dust and startled moths. She climbed the stairs. Rhys was silhouetted against the skylight, a darker shape in the dark. “Power’s out till morning,” she said. “Welcome to Trallwn.” He laughed, small. “Cosy.” Elin rummaged in a cupboard, came up with two candles that smelled of someone’s nan’s funeral and a bottle of Bell’s that had been gathering dust since her mother’s wake. She set them on the low table between the sofas. “Drink?” “Thought you’d never ask.” She poured two fingers into chipped mugs. Handed him one. Their fingers didn’t brush this time; both made sure of it. They sat opposite ends of the table, knees almost touching. Candle flames wobbled like drunks. Rhys raised his mug. “To power cuts.” “And idiots who get trapped in them.” He drank. Pulled a face. “Christ. Tastes like regret.” “Accurate.” Silence settled, heavier now the rain had somewhere to aim. Elin tucked her feet under her. Rhys stared into the whisky like it might talk back. He spoke first, voice low. “You ever get scared of the quiet?” “All the time.” He nodded, surprised. “Thought it was just me.” “It’s not.” He turned the mug in circles. “I keep the telly on. Even when I’m not watching. Drives the cleaners mad.” Elin sipped. “I talk to the cat.” “Nel doesn’t answer.” “She judges. That’s enough.” He laughed once, almost soundless. The candle nearest him guttered; his face slid in and out of shadow. “Tell me something boring,” he said suddenly. “What?” “Something proper dull. About your day. Before I ruined it.” Elin considered. “I ate a cheese sandwich that had gone hard at the edges. Alphabetised the crime section twice because I hated how it looked the first time. Nel brought in half a mouse and left it in my slipper.” Rhys smiled at the flame. “Perfect.” “Your turn.” “I sat in a taxi for forty minutes while a runner tried to find oat milk for someone who doesn’t even drink coffee. Then I smiled at a wall of cameras and pretended I wasn’t dying inside.” Elin studied him. “You’re good at pretending.” “Occupational hazard.” They drank. The whisky loosened edges. He asked, quieter, “Your dad the poet?” She stiffened. “Who told you that?” “No one. Postcard on the noticeboard downstairs. Los Angeles postmark. One line: ‘The sea still remembers your name.’ Bit wankerish.” Elin snorted despite herself. “That’s him. Sends one every Christmas. Never asks how I am.” Rhys turned the mug again. “Mine used to send birthday cards to the wrong address till I was twenty-five. Then he stopped bothering.” “Better than pretending he’s deep.” “Maybe.” Another silence. The storm moved overhead like a lorry with no silencer. Rhys’s voice dropped to nearly nothing. “I still check the clippings my mam keeps. In a biscuit tin. Even the bad ones. She rings if I’m on the front page. Doesn’t matter what for.” Elin watched the side of his face. “You want her to stop?” “God, no. It’s the only time anyone says my real name.” He drank the rest in one. Set the mug down too hard; it clinked like a bell. Elin poured him another without asking. He took it, hands shaking just enough to notice. “I’m shit at this.” “At what?” “Being off.” She understood. Didn’t help. He rubbed his eyes with the heel of one hand. “Sorry. Didn’t mean to—” “Shut up, Rhys.” He did. Minutes passed. The candle on the left drowned in its own wax. Darkness crept closer. Elin spoke to the remaining flame. “When Mam was dying I read her the same detective novel every night. She knew who did it by week two but pretended she didn’t. Said she liked my voice.” Rhys looked at her then, really looked. The candlelight made her eyes very green. “I’m sorry,” he said. “Old news.” “Still hurts.” She shrugged, small. “Some days.” He shifted, restless. The sofa springs groaned. He picked at his thumbnail; a bead of blood appeared. Elin reached without thinking, closed her hand over his to still it. His skin was hot. They both froze. She should let go. Didn’t. He turned his hand slowly, palm up, like testing if she’d pull away. She didn’t. Fingers laced, clumsy. Neither looked at the other. Nel jumped onto the table, stuck her head in Rhys’s empty mug, got offended by the smell, and left. Elin laughed once, shocked out of her. Rhys’s thumb moved across her knuckles, barely there. “You’re shaking.” “So are you.” The candle gave up. Darkness swallowed them whole. They stayed like that, hands locked, breathing the same small space of air. After a while his head tipped forward until his forehead rested against hers. No kissing. Just the weight of contact. Outside, the wind howled like it wanted in. Inside, the whisky cooled and the blankets stayed folded and eventually Rhys’s breathing evened out. Elin felt it happen, the slow surrender to sleep leaning on her. She didn’t move. Counted heartbeats instead of sheep. At some point she whispered into the dark, “You’re still an idiot.” He made a small sound, already gone. Elin closed her eyes. The storm kept its distance now, listening.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 4)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This is a genuinely accomplished piece of writing that largely delivers on its ambitious plan. Let me work through it carefully. **Strengths:** The dialogue is the chapter's greatest achievement. It moves with the naturalism the plan promised: short, overlapping, often going nowhere useful before suddenly cutting deep. "Thought you'd never ask" / "To power cuts" / "And idiots who get trapped in them" — this is economical and characterful. The exchange about the dad's postcard ("The sea still remembers your name" / "Bit wankerish") is excellent: it reveals Rhys's observational intelligence, punctures the father's pretension, and makes Elin laugh against her will, all in two lines. The "tell me something boring" request is a lovely, unexpected move that feels genuinely character-specific to Rhys — it's the kind of thing a man exhausted by performance would ask. The characters are consistent with their profiles. Rhys's thumb-picking, the blood bead, the hiding of hands — all present. His voice sliding between polished and raw works. Elin's weaponised silence, her refusal to comfort, her "shut up, Rhys" (which is both dismissal and intimacy) — all correct. The moment she closes her hand over his without thinking, then doesn't let go, is the right kind of involuntary gesture for her character. The chapter plan called for: whisky, cheap blended, chipped mugs; conversation lurching from stupid to brutal; him crying about something small and pretending it's the whisky; falling asleep on separate sofas, one blanket short. The chapter delivers most of this faithfully. The "crying" is transmuted into something subtler — the shaking hands, the voice dropping — which is arguably better than literal tears. The falling asleep together is rendered with real tenderness: the forehead-to-forehead contact, the counted heartbeats, the whispered "you're still an idiot." The blanket detail is slightly elided (blankets "stayed folded") which is a small deviation but works. **Weaknesses and concerns:** Some of the descriptive writing strains. "Blacker than the inside of a whale" is the kind of image that sounds vivid but doesn't quite land — whales aren't particularly associated with darkness in a way that earns the comparison. "Candle flames wobbled like drunks" is functional but not distinguished. "The storm moved overhead like a lorry with no silencer" is better — it's local, unglamorous, right for Trallwn. But "the storm kept its distance now, listening" at the end is the chapter's weakest moment: it's the kind of pathetic fallacy that the plan explicitly wanted to avoid, and it tips into the purple. The "darkness crept closer" is a cliché. "His face slid in and out of shadow" is competent but not original. The Nel interruption (cat sniffing the mug) is well-deployed — it breaks the tension at exactly the right moment and produces Elin's involuntary laugh, which is consistent with her character's sneeze-laugh. This is the plan's "small, unfinished gestures" working correctly. The forehead-to-forehead moment is well-handled — it's intimate without being a kiss, which is exactly what the plan called for. However, "No kissing. Just the weight of contact" is slightly over-explained; the prose tells us what it is rather than trusting the image. The "Elin's eyes very green" in candlelight is a minor vanity — it's the kind of detail that signals "romantic moment" a little too obviously. The chapter's emotional arc is well-constructed: it moves from banter to confession to physical contact to sleep, and each step feels earned rather than rushed. The whisky-as-regret line is good. The biscuit tin of clippings detail for Rhys is excellent characterisation — specific, sad, true to his profile. **Overall assessment:** This is strong work. The dialogue is the best thing here and it's very good. The characterisation is consistent and nuanced. The emotional beats are earned. The weaknesses are mostly in the descriptive passages, where the writing occasionally reaches for effect and doesn't quite get there. The plan is followed faithfully with intelligent adaptations. The chapter avoids the listed prohibitions (no "truly seen," no cariad, no fixing kiss). The Welshness is lived rather than brandished. This is a chapter that does what it sets out to do with skill and occasional genuine beauty. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Compelling Plot: 14 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 5 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 5 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Characters Consistent with Profile: 18 Followed Chapter Plan: 17 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 5

Ok now chapter 5. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
Morning arrived in pieces. First the gulls started screaming like unpaid debt collectors. Then the generator down the street coughed itself awake and the lights flickered back on, too bright, accusatory. Elin woke with a crick in her neck and Rhys Maddox half-slumped against her shoulder, one arm flung across her lap like he’d fallen mid-argument. She eased away before he woke. He made a small sound, lost, then blinked up at the skylight like he’d forgotten where gravity lived. “Morning,” she said, already halfway across the room, kettle in hand. Rhys rubbed his face. “Did we—” “No.” “Good. I mean— not that— fuck.” He sat up properly, hair sticking out like a hedgehog in distress. Elin filled the kettle because it was easier than looking at him. “Power’s back. You’re free to go.” He stood, wobbled, found balance. “Right.” Downstairs the shop looked like a crime scene of yesterday: mugs, candle stubs, one sock Nel had dragged off somewhere. Rhys found his coat still dripping on the radiator, put it on like armour. Elin unlocked the front door out of habit. Cold air rushed in, smelling of salt and chip fat. She expected quiet. Instead she got people. A queue snaked halfway down the street. Mostly teenagers in hoodies, phones already out, but also Mrs. Pugh from the post office and Dai the lifeboat lad who’d never read a book in his life. Someone had taped a hand-written sign to the window: RHYS MADDOX SPOTTED HERE – SELFIES £5 FOR RNLI. Rhys stopped behind her, stared. “Shit.” “Welcome to Trallwn,” Elin muttered. The first girl in line shrieked when she saw him. Phones lifted like weapons. Rhys’s face did the thing: the smile switched on, bright and automatic, the one that lived on billboards. He stepped outside before Elin could stop him. “Morning, ladies! Bit early for you lot, isn’t it?” The accent was smoother now, public-school edges sanded on. The crowd surged. Elin watched from the doorway as he posed, arm round shoulders, tilting heads for better angles, laughing at jokes that weren’t funny. Every time he laughed the sound got louder, thinner. She felt it in her stomach like spoiled milk. Ten minutes in, a girl of about sixteen handed him a Sharpie and turned round so he could sign the back of her hoodie. He wrote something long and looping, added a heart. The girl nearly cried. Elin went inside, started slamming books around. She didn’t know why she was angry, only that she was. By eleven the queue had doubled. Someone produced a camping chair. Dai sold tea from a flask at two quid a cup. The bell kept giving its pathetic clack every time someone pushed in to gawp. Rhys appeared in the doorway, flushed, hair perfect again somehow. “They’re lovely, aren’t they?” Elin didn’t look up from the till. “Lovely.” He hovered. “I could— I mean, if you want me to tell them to piss off—” “Shop’s open to the public,” she said. “Your public, apparently.” He deflated slightly. “I’ll sign a few more, then shift them on. Promise.” He went back out. The laughter got louder. Elin served three people who only wanted to know if he was still here, one woman who bought a 50p bookmark just to get closer to the window, and a small boy who asked if Rhys was actually Spider-Man. At quarter to twelve she’d had enough. She stepped outside, clapped her hands once, sharp. “Right. That’s it. Shop’s closing early. Everybody out.” Groans. Someone shouted “Five more minutes!” “No. Out. Now. I’ve got a life.” Rhys turned, surprised. “Elin—” “You too.” The smile flickered. “What?” “I said out.” The crowd started shuffling off, disappointed but obedient; you didn’t argue with Elin Vaughan when her voice went flat like that. Rhys lingered. “Did I do something?” She looked at him then, really looked. The mask was still on, perfect teeth, perfect hair, perfect apology in the eyes. “You did exactly what you always do,” she said. He opened his mouth, closed it. The last teenager drifted away. The street emptied until it was just them and the gulls. Rhys tried again, quieter. “I can come back when—” “No point.” He nodded once, small, like he’d been expecting it. Pulled the coat tighter. “Right. I’ll— thanks. For last night. And the sofa. And—” “Just go, Rhys.” He went. Elin watched him walk away down the empty street, hands in pockets, shoulders up round his ears. He didn’t look back. She locked the door, turned the sign to CLOSED, leaned her forehead against the cool glass again. The shop felt too big suddenly. Too quiet. Nel appeared from nowhere, wound round her ankles once, then stalked off, disgusted by the entire human race. Elin stood there a long time. Eventually she went upstairs, found Rhys’s forgotten scarf draped over the sofa like a question. Held it a moment, then shoved it in a drawer with the same force she used to slam books. “Fucking actors,” she told the empty room. Her voice cracked on the last word. Downstairs the bell stayed silent. The radiator ticked itself cool. Outside, the day carried on, grey and ordinary, like nothing had happened at all.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 5)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter 5 executes its plan with considerable skill and restraint. The chapter plan called for Rhys switching to "red-carpet mode," Elin watching him distribute selfies "like communion" and feeling something sour, clearing the shop at noon, telling him to leave, and then immediately hating the quiet. The chapter delivers all of this faithfully, with the added texture of the RNLI sign, Dai selling tea, and the small boy asking if Rhys is Spider-Man — details that feel genuinely local and observed rather than manufactured. The dialogue is the chapter's strongest element. It's sparse, clipped, and doing real work. "You did exactly what you always do" lands hard precisely because it's not explained. Rhys's stuttering gratitude at the end — "Thanks. For last night. And the sofa. And—" — is perfectly calibrated: the trailing off says everything about a man who doesn't know how to be dismissed without a script. Elin's "Just go, Rhys" is devastating in its flatness. The voices are distinct and consistent with their profiles throughout. The chapter also earns its emotional beats. The "spoiled milk" simile for Elin's stomach feeling is one of the better metaphors — grounded, unglamorous, right for her character. The scarf in the drawer is a strong closing gesture: physical, unspoken, and it avoids the trap of over-explaining her feelings. "Her voice cracked on the last word" is the one moment that edges toward telling rather than showing, but it's brief and earns its place given how much has been withheld. The opening is efficient. "Morning arrived in pieces" is a mild cliché but not offensive. "Unpaid debt collectors" for the gulls is fresh and funny. The hedgehog simile for Rhys's hair is light and works. The "mask" language near the end ("The mask was still on, perfect teeth, perfect hair") is slightly more on-the-nose than the rest of the chapter — it nudges toward telling us what we've already been shown — but it's not egregious. The chapter plan mentioned Elin feeling "something sour" and the chapter delivers this without over-explaining it. The anger she feels watching him perform is rendered through action (slamming books) rather than interior monologue, which is the right call. The one interior note — "She didn't know why she was angry, only that she was" — is honest and appropriately uncertain rather than falsely self-aware. Where the chapter is slightly weaker: the crowd scene, while functional, is the least textured section. The individual figures (Mrs. Pugh, Dai) are named but don't quite come alive. The chapter plan promised Elin watching him "hand out selfies like communion" — the actual text doesn't use that image, which is a minor loss since it was a strong one. The chapter also doesn't quite capture the full complexity of Elin's interiority during the crowd scene; we get the sour feeling but not the layered ambivalence the character profile suggests she'd have. The ending is well-judged. Nel's disgust, the radiator ticking, the grey ordinary day — these are earned rather than imposed. The chapter avoids the trap of making the departure too cinematic. Rhys not looking back is the right choice. Overall this is confident, controlled work that serves both the characters and the chapter plan. It's not flawless — a few moments of slight over-explanation, the crowd scene could be richer — but it's consistently good and occasionally excellent. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Compelling Plot: 14 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 2 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Characters Consistent with Profile: 17 Followed Chapter Plan: 17 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 6

Ok now chapter 6. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The rain came back after dark, sideways and vicious, rattling the windows like it wanted paying. Elin was on the floor behind the counter, surrounded by half-packed boxes, drinking cold tea straight from the mug because the kettle had given up. The radio only caught static and some preacher from Swansea telling everyone to repent. She turned it off. The bell clacked once, weak, like it was embarrassed. She looked up. Rhys stood in the doorway, soaked again, coat collar turned up, hair plastered to his skull. He looked smaller than he had that morning. “Door was on the latch,” he said. Elin stayed on the floor. “Clearly.” He closed it behind him, leaned against it the same way he had yesterday, like the world might chase him in. Water dripped off him in a steady rhythm. “I walked,” he said. “From the B&B. Woman asked for a selfie before she’d give me the key. I stood in her porch smiling till my face hurt. Then I walked here instead.” Elin said nothing. He rubbed a hand over his face. “I’m shit at this.” “You said.” He took one step in, then stopped, like the floor might bite. “Can I— can we not do the morning thing again? Where you hate me and I pretend I’m fine?” “I don’t hate you.” “You should.” Elin set the mug down. “Get over here before you drown the stock.” He came, slow, took off the coat, hung it on the same radiator. The place smelled of wet wool and old books and something that might have been her. He sat on the other side of the box mountain, knees up, arms wrapped round them. Looked about twelve. “I saw the pictures,” he said. “Online. This morning. Me with my arm round that girl’s mam. Caption says ‘Rhys Maddox cosying up in quiet Welsh town—new flame?’ There’s one of me signing the kid’s hoodie. They’ve cropped you out.” Elin shrugged. “Congratulations.” “Don’t.” Silence stretched. The rain found a new gear. He picked at his thumbnail; fresh blood already. “There’s worse stuff coming tomorrow. Old girlfriend sold some story. Usual rubbish. I just— I keep waiting for you to ask.” “Why would I ask?” “Because everyone does.” Elin looked at him then, straight. “I’m not everyone.” He laughed once, wet and hopeless. “No. You’re really fucking not.” She moved a box aside, made a space. “Sit properly. You look like a wet cat.” He crawled round, sat beside her on the floor, back against the counter. Close enough that their shoulders touched if either breathed too deep. Neither moved away. Minutes passed. The radiator gurgled like it had opinions. Rhys spoke to the opposite wall. “When it’s bad, when the papers go for me, I ring my mam. She says ‘you all right, cariad?’ and I say yeah even when I’m not. Then she tells me what she had for tea. That’s it. That’s the list.” Elin listened. He kept going, voice small. “I don’t have anyone else. Not really. Three mates from school who still take the piss, but they’ve got kids now. Proper lives. I can’t ring them at three a.m. because some cunt on Twitter called me a has-been.” He turned his head, looked at her sideways. “Who do you ring?” “No one.” “Ever?” “Mam’s dead. Dad’s in California writing haikus about palm trees. Friends have husbands and babies and shifts at the hospital. I talk to Nel. She’s a terrible listener.” He smiled at that, tiny and real. Outside, thunder rolled in off the sea, low and slow. Rhys shifted, restless. “I could go. Catch the last train. Be in London by morning. Hide in the flat I never use.” “You could.” He waited. Elin sighed through her nose. “Come on.” She stood, held out a hand. He took it. His was freezing. They went out the back door, into the alley that led to the sea wall. Rain hit them horizontal, stinging. Elin didn’t bother with a coat; Rhys’s was still dripping inside. They walked hunched, heads down, till the harbour opened up, black water slapping concrete. She climbed the wall first, sat with legs dangling over the drop. He followed, awkward, sat beside her. The wind tried to shove them both into the sea. They shouted over it. “You came back,” she yelled. “Couldn’t not.” “Why?” He looked at her, rain streaming down both their faces. “Because you didn’t ask for a selfie. Or a story. Or anything.” Elin wiped her eyes; couldn’t tell what was rain, what wasn’t. He leaned closer so she could hear. “I’m tired of performing.” She studied him. “Then don’t.” He laughed, bitter. “Easy as that?” “No. Hard as fuck. But start now.” He went very still. A gull swooped low, shrieked, and let go a perfect white streak that splattered across Rhys’s shoulder. He looked down, started laughing: proper, helpless, snotty laughing that bent him double. Elin laughed too, couldn’t help it. The sound got lost in the wind. When it eased, they were closer, shoulders pressed, sharing miserable warmth. He turned his head. “Can I—” “No.” He nodded, accepting. The rain kept falling. Neither moved.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 6)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter is a strong piece of work that largely delivers on its plan and the broader prompt. Let me examine it carefully. STRENGTHS: The dialogue is the chapter's greatest asset. It's genuinely distinct between the two characters — Rhys's voice slides between performed ease and raw vulnerability ("I'm shit at this"), while Elin's responses are clipped, dry, and precise without being cold. The exchange about who they ring when things are bad is the emotional core of the chapter and it lands cleanly. "Mam's dead. Dad's in California writing haikus about palm trees. I talk to Nel. She's a terrible listener." — this is excellent: economical, characterful, and quietly devastating. It reveals Elin's loneliness without sentimentalizing it. The gull moment is a genuine triumph. The plan called for "the almost-kiss happens here and is ruined by a gull shitting on his shoulder" and the execution is perfect — it's absurd, it's earned, it breaks the tension without deflating the emotion, and crucially it produces real laughter from both characters that feels organic rather than manufactured. The plan warned against "a kiss that fixes everything" and the chapter honors that: the almost-kiss is interrupted, he accepts the "no" without drama, and they sit in the rain together anyway. That restraint is sophisticated. The physical details are well-chosen and unforced: the blood on his thumbnail, the coat on the same radiator, the rain finding "a new gear." These are grounded and specific without being showy. CHARACTER CONSISTENCY: Rhys is exactly as profiled — the charm has collapsed, he's raw, he's picking at his thumbnail, he's performing vulnerability now instead of charisma but the self-awareness is there ("I keep waiting for you to ask"). His admission about his mam being the only person he calls is consistent with the profile's note that "currently has no one he calls when a red-top runs a bad story at 2 a.m." Elin's refusal to ask about the tabloid story, her hand extended to help him up, her laugh getting "lost in the wind" — all consistent with someone who weaponizes silence but isn't cruel. WEAKNESSES AND CRITICISMS: The chapter plan specified "The almost-kiss happens here and is ruined by a gull." The execution is faithful, but the "Can I— / No." exchange is perhaps slightly too clean, too cinematic in its brevity. It risks feeling like a scripted beat rather than a stumbled-toward moment. The plan's language about things being "half-lit" and "unfinished" is mostly honored, but this particular exchange is a little too polished. "The rain kept falling. Neither moved." — this closing line is competent but not exceptional. It's the kind of ending that works but doesn't surprise. The chapter plan promised endings that "cut deep" and this one settles for atmospheric. The line "something that might have been her" (referring to smell) is the chapter's one moment of forced poetry. It's trying to be suggestive and ends up slightly precious. "The radiator gurgled like it had opinions" — this is charming and earns its place. The pacing of the sea wall section is slightly rushed compared to the interior scene. The transition from floor to wall happens quickly and the wall scene, while effective, could use one more beat of specificity. The chapter is not amateurish. The prose is controlled, the dialogue is strong, the emotional beats are earned. It's not a masterpiece — the ending is safe, one or two moments are slightly too neat — but it's genuinely accomplished work. FOLLOWED CHAPTER PLAN: The plan called for: old stories resurfacing online (check), him waiting for her to ask (check, explicitly), "Who do you ring when it's bad?" (check, verbatim), the almost-kiss ruined by the gull (check). The chapter follows its plan faithfully and executes most of it well. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Compelling Plot: 14 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 3 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 2 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 4 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Characters Consistent with Profile: 18 Followed Chapter Plan: 18 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 7

Ok now chapter 7. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
Morning came in pale and hung-over. Elin found the notice taped to the inside of the shop door when she came down to make coffee: FINAL NOTICE – LEASE TERMINATION – 42 DAYS. The landlord had slipped it through the letterbox sometime before dawn, coward’s hours. She read it twice, said “fuck” very quietly in Welsh, and left it where it was. Rhys arrived at twenty past nine carrying two paper cups from the café that had opened purely because he was in town. He looked like he hadn’t slept; eyes red, hair still damp from a shower that hadn’t helped. He wore yesterday’s jeans and a hoodie that cost more than her monthly rent. “Brought peace offering,” he said, holding out a cup. “They wrote ‘Rice’ on it. Told them twice.” Elin took the coffee, didn’t drink. “Shop’s closing.” He followed her gaze to the notice. Read it. Went very still. “Right,” he said. “How much to buy the building?” “Don’t.” “I’m serious. Name the price. I’ll wire it today.” Elin turned on him so fast the coffee sloshed. “I said don’t. I’m not a damsel and this isn’t a fucking script where you ride in with your chequebook and I swoon.” Rhys flinched like she’d slapped him. “I’m trying to help.” “You’re trying to buy absolution. Different thing.” He opened his mouth, closed it. Put his own cup down untouched. Silence sat between them like a third person with opinions. Eventually he spoke to the floor. “What do you want, then?” “To not owe anyone. Ever.” “Fair.” Another silence. The radiator clanked like it was clearing its throat. Rhys rubbed the back of his neck. “I’ve got nothing to do for a week. Next job’s in Prague end of the month. I could— I could stay. Help you pack. Or fight the landlord. Or sit in the corner and shut up. Whatever you actually need. No money. No cameras. Just hands.” Elin looked at him for a long time. He waited, dripping guilt and coffee steam. She folded her arms. “One condition.” “Anything.” “You don’t try to make me like you. Not once. No jokes, no charm, no sad eyes, no buying the last custard cream before I get to it. Nothing. If you last a week without performing, I’ll think about letting you stay and be useful. One crack and you’re on the next train. Swear it.” He swallowed. “I swear.” “Say it proper.” “I swear on my mam’s life I won’t try to make you like me for seven days.” Elin studied his face like she was checking for loopholes. Found none she could name. “Fine,” she said. “There’s boxes in the back. Start with the Welsh-language shelves. And if you alphabetise wrong I’ll kill you.” Rhys nodded once, solemn. Picked up a flattened box, started building it with shaking hands. Elin watched him for a minute, then went to find the Stanley knife. They worked. By noon the Welsh shelves were half empty and Rhys hadn’t spoken unless spoken to. He moved like someone expecting a trap. When Elin reached past him for tape their arms brushed; he apologised like he’d assaulted her. “Sorry—” “Stop apologising. That counts as performing.” He shut up. At two, Mrs. Pugh from the post office came in wanting gossip. Rhys kept his head down, kept packing. Mrs. Pugh left disappointed. At four, Nel brought in a live mouse, dropped it proudly at Rhys’s feet, and watched him hop backwards onto a box of poetry. He didn’t laugh, didn’t curse, just waited till the mouse escaped under the skirting board, then went back to work. Elin pretended not to notice any of it. By six the shop looked wounded, half its stock in boxes, shelves staring like empty eye sockets. Rhys’s hoodie was covered in dust and one suspicious paw-print of mud. His thumbnail was bleeding again. Elin made tea, two mugs, put one beside him without a word. He took it with both hands like it might vanish. They drank standing up, leaning on opposite sides of the counter. Rhys broke the quiet, voice rough. “Day one nearly done. Still breathing.” “Don’t push your luck.” He nodded, stared into the tea. Elin set her mug down. “You missed dinner.” “I’m fine.” “You’re not. There’s pasta in the flat. Come up when you’re ready.” She left him there.
